question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support that is available to unpaid carers in Fermanagh and South Tyrone while they wait for formal care packages to be implemented.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to address delays in care package provision in rural areas, such as Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for the delivery of a care package in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail how staffing shortages within domiciliary care services are affecting care package delivery in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the the number of people in Fermanagh and South Tyrone who are waiting for an assessed care package to be put in place.

To ask the Minister of Justice whether any additional funding or legislative change is being considered by her Department to strengthen early intervention and safeguarding for women and girls, identified through sexual exploitation investigations.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of the Firearms and Explosives Branch annual operating cost, that is attributable to activities other than frontline firearms licensing decisions.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of the effectiveness of existing legislation, aimed at addressing sexual exploitation and the advertising of sexual services.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any engagement her Department and the PSNI have had with online platforms on which sexual services are advertised; and (ii) any steps that are being taken to disrupt their use for sexual exploitation in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of increased firearms licensing fees on farm businesses.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any safety measures currently in place on buses operated by Translink to protect passengers and staff, with particular regard to preventing and responding to violence, harassment, or intimidation, directed at women and girls.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of incidents of violence or anti-social behaviour, reported on buses operated by Translink in each of the last five years, that involved passengers who were women or girls, broken down by (i) physical assaults; (ii) verbal abuse or harassment, including misogynistic or sexualised abuse; and (ii) other forms of anti-social behaviour.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any support currently available to teaching and non-teaching school staff who are subjected to online abuse, harassment or intimidation, including through social media platforms; and (ii) any additional measures his Department (a) has in place; or (b) plans to introduce, to support schools and staff where such incidents involve publicly shared videos or content that is abusive, threatening or defamatory.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any training Translink bus drivers receive in relation to identifying, preventing, and responding to incidents involving violence or harassment, including incidents targeted at women and girls or of a sexual or gender based nature.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of incidents occurring on buses operated by Translink and reported to the Police Service of Northern Ireland, in each of the last five years, that involved women or girls as victims, and (ii) the offence recorded in each case.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the economic and social value of the post office network in Northern Ireland, particularly in areas affected by bank branch closures.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department ensures rural communities continue to have access to essential face-to-face government services.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with the UK Government regarding the delivery of commitments in the Green Paper, Future of the Post Office, as they relate to Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of properties that will become available for renting in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency in the next five years, under the Intermediate Rent Scheme.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether (i) any assessment been made of of the ongoing issues facing the construction industry; and (ii) she is aware of escalating concrete prices, as reported by the industry.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of illicit drug seizures at (i) City of Derry Airport; (ii) Belfast International Airport; (iii) George Best Belfast City Airport; (iv) Foyle Port; (v) Larne Port; and (vi) Belfast Port, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment made of the impact of the Substance Misuse Court, introduced at Belfast Magistrates’ Court in 2018, on levels of drug-related offending.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of illicit drug seizures that have been made by the Police Service of Northern Ireland and UK Border Force, involving drugs transported by land from the Republic of Ireland, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the information requested.  The recording of information on the number of illicit drug seizures is a matter for operational partners.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any further query relating to the number of illicit drug seizures involving drugs transported by land from the Republic of Ireland to the PSNI and Border Force.     Further information may be available in the Organised Crime Task Force Annual Report and Threat Assessments, this detail will have been provided by law enforcement partners.  Copies of the reports from 2009/2010 to 2023/2024 can be accessed online on the Organised Crime Task Force website publications pagehttps://www.octf.gov.uk/publications/type/octf-annual-report-threat-assessments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to establish additional Substance Misuse Courts in other court divisions across Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of individuals that have received custodial sentences, following arrest for possession of cocaine with an estimated street value exceeding £5,000, in each of the last three years.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of occasions since taking office on which she was advised by her officials not to attend events and proceeded to attend regardless; and (ii) whether any such instances have had an impact on the day-to-day operation of her Department.
answer_text: Officials advise and Ministers decide.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she took to identify and mitigate potential conflicts of interest in relation to the recent appointments to the board of Invest NI of individuals with political involvement in the same political party as her.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her role and level of direct involvement, in the appointment of members to the board of Invest NI.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any assessment that has made of the benefits to inward investment in Northern Ireland, arising from her attendance at a standalone event in Boston in 2025, including whether those benefits outweighed the cost to the public purse; and (ii) whether the host’s connection to her political party formed part of her consideration in deciding to attend.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any external advice she received regarding her decision to attend a conference in Boston in 2025, in light of advice from her departmental officials against attending.
answer_text: Contrary to recent reports, my officials advised I should address this event if it was part of a wider programme. The advice was :Golden Bridges is a well-established event which brings together business, civic and cultural leaders from Derry, Donegal and Massachusetts to build transatlantic partnerships. The Minister has been asked to speak at the 2025 event and to articulate her vision and priorities for economic growth and prosperity. The event attracts an influential Irish-American audience and the Conference programme will honour those members of the business community and Diaspora who have made an outstanding contribution to building international connections between Ireland and the US and investing in the success of the North West and wider region. The conference is a professionally staged and well-received event and should the Minister plan to visit the US as part of a wider programme at that time, it would certainly be an event which the Minister should address. However, given the Minister’s wider commitments and cost implications of traveling, it might be better to decline as a standalone event. 		In line with this advice, I attended the event as part of a wider programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to to expand the electric vehicle charging infrastructure in Enniskillen and other key towns and villages, within Fermanagh and South Tyrone.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with (i) businesses; (ii) farms; and (iii) tourism operators, in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, regarding the transition to electric vehicles.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any targets her Department has set for increasing electric vehicle uptake, in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment she has made of the adequacy of electric vehicle charge point provision in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, given its rural nature and distances between settlements.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of public electric vehicle charge points that are currently available in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency.
To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has made any assessment of the impact of differing school holiday schedules across schools within the same area.
answer_text: All schools, regardless of phase, type or sector, have autonomy to set their opening dates whilst maintaining the minimum number of teaching days, less any exceptional closures granted by the Department. Well in advance of the forthcoming academic year, the Education Authority publishes dates when key services (meals, transport and waste) will operate to assist schools when finalising their own school calendar. Departmental guidance strongly encourages schools to align their calendars with these dates to ensure access to services and to support consistency across schools.For the 2026/27 school year, the Department has exceptionally permitted one optional closure day per term to support staff engagement with the TransformED significant programme of education reform.  The Department has specified dates for these closures to ensure systemwide alignment and is further committed to ensuring greater alignment of School Development Days in the future.In the coming months, the Department intends to review legislation on minimum school operational days and will consider whether greater alignment of school calendars is desirable. Any proposals will be subject to public consultation.At present, should a school choose to open on days when services are non-operational, it is advised to ensure parents are clearly informed that transport will not be available and that Translink passes will not be valid.  If a school closes on days when services are operational, dedicated school transport services do not operate, however where services are shared across more than one school with differing holiday periods, there may be occasions where services operate with reduced passenger numbers.  No environmental impact assessment of low-occupancy transport has been undertaken.In respect of childcare costs, the Department’s Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme provides a 15% subsidy on registered childcare costs. This applies to nurseries, childminders, afterschool clubs, holiday schemes and approved home childcare and can be combined with TaxFree Childcare to deliver significant savings for working parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has to mitigate the congestion issues on the Thomas Street Roundabout, Dungannon, at peak times.
answer_text: Thomas Street Roundabout is on a very constrained site which means further works are limited at this location.I have asked my officials to continue to monitor the area and consider any opportunities to assist in reducing the traffic congestion, particularly with regard to further development along the route.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to support the construction of social housing by (i) the Housing Executive; and (ii) housing associations.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has had any engagement with its counterparts in Great Britain, including the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, to ensure that dealers of used agricultural machinery in both jurisdictions are fully informed of the requirements under the relevant movement schemes.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of used agricultural farm machinery from Great Britain to Northern Ireland, including the provision of guidance for businesses and traders, falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of special schools; and (ii) the number of children enrolled in special schools, in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: Information from the 2025/26 Annual School Census indicates that, in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency, (i) there are two special schools; and (ii) 383 pupils enrolled in special schools on census day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the Western Health and Social Care Trust’s decision to temporarily relocate the Pessary Clinic from the South West Acute Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being taken to address staffing pressures at the South West Acute Hospital following the temporary relocation of the Pessary Clinic.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the anticipated timeframe for the Pessary Clinic’s return to the South West Acute Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any analysis has been carried out on how the temporary move of Pessary Clinic from the South West Acute Hospital will affect (i) patient access; (ii) travel times; and (iii) continuity of care, for service users.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of medical doctors, currently employed or contracted by the Driver and Vehicle Agency, to assess the medical fitness of drivers; and (ii) any assessment she has made of the adequacy of this clinical resource in meeting current casework demands.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) does not directly employ medical officers. Fitness to drive medical assessments are currently conducted on behalf of the DVA by six qualified medical practitioners at 3fivetwo Healthcare, which the DVA considers is sufficient to meet its current casework demands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any funding available to community groups for programmes to inform and educate young people around domestic abuse, in line with the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls.
answer_text: TEO is the Department which funds community groups under the EVAWG Strategy, and does so through the Regional and Local Change Funds.DoJ, jointly with DoH, are responsible for the Domestic & Sexual Abuse Strategy.It is incumbent on us all to do all we can to tackle the scourge of violence against women and girls in society, and within my Department several business areas contribute to work that strengthens protection, prevention, and frontline support for women and girls. This includes:Staff within Universal Credit Service Centres and Working Age Services are trained and guided to respond appropriately where domestic abuse is disclosed, and all 35 Jobs & Benefits offices act as designated Safe Spaces offering signposting and referral to specialist support;Delivering two programmes under the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), Developing Women in the Community and Fresh Start in the Community, each of which include some activity aligned with the EVAWG Strategic Framework and current funded projects will continue until March 2027;Funding the Advocacy Project through Dove House Community Trust;Providing significant support for women at risk of domestic violence through the Supporting People Programme;The Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) undertakes a range of work to support victims of domestic abuse, including delivery of the Sanctuary Scheme, homelessness prevention initiatives and extensive mandatory staff training;In addition, a number of my Department’s ALBs, such as the Arts Council and Sport NI, undertake or support programmes designed to contribute to tackling violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the number of bovine TB cattle reactors in each of the last four years.
answer_text: The number of cattle that have tested positive to the Tuberculin skin test or the supplementary interferon gamma blood test in each of the last four calendar years is as follows:	in 2022- 19,555; 	in 2023- 19,588; 	in 2024- 21,942; and	in 2025- 20,271.		It is encouraging to note a small improvement in bovine TB disease statistics in recent months with herd incidence now standing at 9.66% for the 12 months to the end of February.  This is the lowest rate since summer 2022.		However, animal incidence, which is reflected in the number of cattle being slaughtered, hasn’t fallen by as much since its sharp rise in early 2024.  		Despite this small improvement in the disease picture, I recognise that our disease rates remain at historically high levels.  This reinforces the need for us to maintain our focus on tackling all of the factors that contribute to the spread and endurance of this costly disease.  		In recent months, working closely with the TB Partnership Steering Group, we have increased the amount of supplementary testing being carried out in breakdown herds; launched a major cross-border regionalisation research pilot; and shared information with farmers about the risk to their herds from cattle that previously had inconclusive skin test results.  		In the coming weeks, my department shall launch a new public consultation on potential wildlife intervention options; and we will commence a procurement exercise to deliver on-farm biosecurity advice. 		Progress can be made when we work collectively, and I would assure members that tackling bovine TB remains a top priority for me and my officials.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the vacancies within DFI Roads Western Division, why her Department is not holding interview open days in each division area for the new campaign to recruit more industrial road workers.
answer_text: I am pleased that my Department’s recruitment campaign for Industrial Road Workers launched on Monday 16th March 2026.The expression of interest stage closed on 31 March 2026 and it is encouraging to hear there has been an exceptionally high response to the campaign.Road Workers undertake important road maintenance activities such as grass cutting, gully cleaning and pothole repair and this recruitment campaign demonstrates my commitment to improving our network for the benefit of all road users.The recruitment exercise is being taking forward as a pilot utilising Talent Dog which is a new innovative recruitment approach that speeds up the application and appointment process. To keep the administration of the campaign straightforward, the decision was taken to use a single venue for interviews. Interview days are planned in Ballymena on 27th and 28th April 2026 with appointments likely to be in June 2026 following successful completion of the pre-employment process.The exceptionally high response to the expression of interest stage of the campaign indicates that the location of the interviews is not presenting a challenge for applicants and this more streamlined approach may be used in similar recruitment campaigns in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when road maintenance works will be carried out on Ballytroddan Road, Dungannon.
answer_text: Details of the schemes being taken forward are published in my Department’s annual report to Council.  Further schemes that are programmed as a result of additional in-year funding are announced in advance of delivery.Officials are currently considering the 2026/27 programme of works, details of which will be included in my Department’s 2026/27 annual report to Council. Programmes cannot be finalised until such times as budgets confirmed.In the meantime, an ad hoc inspection of the Ballytroddan Road identified actionable defects which have been instructed for repair. My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the anticipated timeline, alongside the procedural stages required, for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee legislation to secure Royal Assent, before the end of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: The Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG) policy intent was agreed by the Executive on 26 March 2026.My department aims to introduce the required primary legislation for the REPG scheme to the Assembly before summer recess 2026.The Bill will follow the standard legislative stages detailed in the Assembly’s guidance on law making.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any plans her Department has should the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee legislation not pass during the 2022-27 Assembly mandate, including an expected timeline for reintroduction in the next mandate; and (iii) any assessment her Department has made of the implications that a delay in passing the legislation during the 2022-27 Assembly mandate, would have for meeting climate change and renewable electricity targets.
answer_text: My Department aims to introduce the required primary legislation for the REPG to the Assembly before summer recess 2026. Once the Primary Bill is in place, subordinate legislation will be taken forward to set out the detailed arrangements.The REPG is a critical enabler for our net zero ambitions and to position our region as a competitive and attractive destination for renewables’ investment. Delivering additional renewable electricity generation to meet the 80% target is however complex, demanding a careful balance between security of supply, affordability, and legislative compliance. It therefore requires joined-up working, across government and with our delivery partners, to unlock the progress needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the appointment of a new Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist in the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT), to detail (i) where this post is based; (ii) how the role will operate across the WHSCT; and (iii) whether any element of the service will be available in South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: The newly appointed Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist is a Trust wide position. The nurse is based at Altnagelvin Hospital however will work alongside Consultants at both South West Acute Hospital, Omagh and Altnagelvin Hospital sites. The Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist will run a combination of face to face and telephone Nurse Led clinics covering Altnagelvin, Omagh and South West Acute Hospitals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of funded places available within the education system for therapeutic interventions, including but not limited to music therapy, art therapy, and play therapy; and (ii) any plans to expand or review these services.
answer_text: The HOPE Independent School Counselling and Therapy Service is available for post-primary pupils in mainstream schools, special schools and EOTAS provisions.  The contract provides counselling, play, music, drama and art therapy and there are just over 58,000 individual sessions available each academic year.  The number of sessions allocated to settings will vary depending on their pupil enrolment figure. The HOPE contract commenced in September 2024 and is a five-year contract (three years with possible extension for additional two years).In addition, schools have flexibility to use their individual budgets to best meet the needs of their pupils, and this may include employing external services to provide therapeutic interventions. There are no plans at this time to expand or review these services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of targets in the Forest Service Business Plan 2025-26.
answer_text: Forest Service continues to progress delivery against 2025/2026 Business Plan targets. This remains ongoing until the 31 March 2026, following which Forest Service and Audit verification processes take place to determine the outturn positions.	Verification processes take some weeks to complete; however, these will be finalised to support the publication of Forest Service’s Annual Report and Accounts 2025/2026, expected by the end of June 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has received the Inspector's report on the A29 Cookstown bypass public inquiry.
answer_text: The A29 Cookstown Bypass is estimated to cost in the region of £70m and will provide a new 4km single carriageway with overtaking lanes in both directions; the upgrading of connections between the Omagh and Dungannon roads; the provision of a combined footway/cycle way along the entire length of the scheme and the development of a potential greenway route.The scheme will also alleviate traffic congestion within the Cookstown town centre opening it up to opportunities for active and sustainable travel choices, reduce journey times along the link corridor and improve road safety.  My officials have reviewed the Inspector’s Report.My Department is currently investigating the social value opportunities that this scheme could bring to Cookstown. Social value allows organisations to consider how their activities can create positive benefits for residents, businesses and stakeholders that may be impacted by their projects.  Officials engaged with the public through an online Social Value consultation between Monday 29 September 2025 and Monday 27 October 2025.  The Department also hosted an in-person event on Tuesday 7 October 2025 at the Burnavon Arts and Cultural Centre, Cookstown. Officials are now reviewing the representations received to provide a comprehensive report for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration his Department has given to the Forest Service estate remaining in public ownership, as opposed to being privatised or transferred to private or semi-private entities.
answer_text: My Department manages the Forest Service estate to provide a wide range of public benefits in support of Northern Ireland government priorities and to contribute to the delivery of the Programme for Government. The Department has not considered the transfer of its forest estate to any external entities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any (i) forestry land sales; (ii) transfers; (iii) leasing arrangements; and (iv) disposal considerations, made by the Department or Forest Service, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Sales.No sales of forestry land or DAERA owned land have taken place over the past five-years.  TransfersFour small areas of forestry land, totalling 0.34 hectares, have been transferred from the Forest Service estate to neighbouring landowners for boundary reconciliation purposes in the period, one in 2022, one in 2024 and two in 2025.A further 0.15 hectares of forestry land was transferred to DFI roads in 2023.DAERA has transferred one small area of land from its estate to DFI Roads for roadside improvement works in the period.  LeasesLeases were agreed with telecommunication providers for five areas in the period totalling 0.05 hectares, four of these in 2023 and one in 2024.In 2023 a lease of 0.1 hectares was agreed for an area of land adjacent to a neighbouring property for the purpose of garden extensionNo land leases were agreed within the period for DAERA owned land.DisposalsForest Service has not considered any further disposals during the period.No Disposals were considered by DAERA within the period for DAERA owned land.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the total area of woodland held in (a) private; and (b) public, ownership; and (ii) the amount of this that is managed by Forest Service.
answer_text: As of 31 March 2025, Northern Ireland had a total of 118,898 hectares of woodland. Forest Service manages 74,964 hectares of forestry land, of which 62,137 hectares are classified as woodland. This includes 3,799 hectares held under lease from other public bodies and a further 2,233 hectares leased from other landowners. The ownership details of the remaining 56,761 hectares of woodland are not held by this Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has engaged with local councils or business representative bodies in border constituencies, to develop a cross-border competitiveness strategy, ahead of the reduction of hospitality VAT in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise that the VAT differential across Ireland presents a significant challenge for hospitality businesses in the north. 	I have recently met with Hospitality Ulster and the NI Food to Go Association to discuss their proposals for a VAT reduction pilot scheme. Following these discussions, I wrote jointly with the Minister of Finance to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury seeking an early meeting to address the issue of VAT disparity.		I will continue to work closely with the Minister of Finance to advocate for a fairer tax framework that supports local businesses and enables them to compete effectively across the island.		My Department will continue to monitor business sentiment as part of our wider assessment of economic conditions, and I remain committed to ongoing engagement with key stakeholders, including local authorities, as we work to support a sustainable and thriving hospitality sector in line with my four economic priorities.		Invest NI provides a broad range of support to small and medium sized businesses across the North primarily in the form of advice, guidance, innovation grants and access to finance. My Department and partner organisations also continue to provide a broad range of supports to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department will support businesses in border areas, in advance of the lowering of the hospitality VAT rate in the Republic of Ireland from 13.5 per cent to 9 per cent, in July 2026.
answer_text: I recognise that the VAT differential across Ireland presents a significant challenge for hospitality businesses in the north. 	I have recently met with Hospitality Ulster and the NI Food to Go Association to discuss their proposals for a VAT reduction pilot scheme. Following these discussions, I wrote jointly with the Minister of Finance to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury seeking an early meeting to address the issue of VAT disparity.		I will continue to work closely with the Minister of Finance to advocate for a fairer tax framework that supports local businesses and enables them to compete effectively across the island.		My Department will continue to monitor business sentiment as part of our wider assessment of economic conditions, and I remain committed to ongoing engagement with key stakeholders, including local authorities, as we work to support a sustainable and thriving hospitality sector in line with my four economic priorities.		Invest NI provides a broad range of support to small and medium sized businesses across the North primarily in the form of advice, guidance, innovation grants and access to finance. My Department and partner organisations also continue to provide a broad range of supports to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any mechanisms her Department will put in place to monitor the impact of the Republic of Ireland’s reduced 9 per cent VAT rate on hospitality (i) trade flows; (ii) employment; and (iii) pricing trends in Northern Ireland’s border regions, from July 2026 onwards.
answer_text: I recognise that the VAT differential across Ireland presents a significant challenge for hospitality businesses in the north. 	I have recently met with Hospitality Ulster and the NI Food to Go Association to discuss their proposals for a VAT reduction pilot scheme. Following these discussions, I wrote jointly with the Minister of Finance to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury seeking an early meeting to address the issue of VAT disparity.		I will continue to work closely with the Minister of Finance to advocate for a fairer tax framework that supports local businesses and enables them to compete effectively across the island.		My Department will continue to monitor business sentiment as part of our wider assessment of economic conditions, and I remain committed to ongoing engagement with key stakeholders, including local authorities, as we work to support a sustainable and thriving hospitality sector in line with my four economic priorities.		Invest NI provides a broad range of support to small and medium sized businesses across the North primarily in the form of advice, guidance, innovation grants and access to finance. My Department and partner organisations also continue to provide a broad range of supports to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact on Northern Ireland manufacturers of being excluded from the British Industry Supercharger, given that the scheme applies only to the GB electricity market.
answer_text: The British Industry Supercharger (“the Supercharger”) was launched in April 2024 to reduce electricity costs for Energy Intensive Industries (EIIs) such as steel, chemicals, cement, glass, metals, and paper. The Department for Business and Trade estimated that the Supercharger will deliver savings of between £320m and £410m for eligible EIIs from 2025. This support is expected to be worth around £24 to £31 per megawatt hour on average, with the most electricity-intensive industries benefiting the most.The Supercharger provides eligible EIIs in Britain with a package of electricity cost reductions for renewable energy levies, capacity market charges and network costs. My Department’s assessment is that some of the energy levies, such as Feed-in Tariffs and Contracts for Difference do not apply here, meaning support for these policy costs is not applicable. The other elements of the Supercharger, namely relief for capacity and network costs, are not available to local businesses. Introducing a similar scheme here would increase costs for other consumers, particularly as our smaller customer base offers less scope to spread those costs compared with Britain.My officials are currently considering the potential costs and wider implications associated with replicating a similar scheme here. Any such scheme would need to take account of the Single Electricity Market arrangements and EU State Aid requirements.Any action we take must be considered carefully to avoid unintended consequences, especially at a time when many households continue to face cost-of-living pressures and fuel poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department intends to develop Northern Ireland specific relief schemes, to mirror the British Industry Supercharger.
answer_text: The British Industry Supercharger (“the Supercharger”) was launched in April 2024 to reduce electricity costs for Energy Intensive Industries (EIIs) such as steel, chemicals, cement, glass, metals, and paper. The Department for Business and Trade estimated that the Supercharger will deliver savings of between £320m and £410m for eligible EIIs from 2025. This support is expected to be worth around £24 to £31 per megawatt hour on average, with the most electricity-intensive industries benefiting the most.The Supercharger provides eligible EIIs in Britain with a package of electricity cost reductions for renewable energy levies, capacity market charges and network costs. My Department’s assessment is that some of the energy levies, such as Feed-in Tariffs and Contracts for Difference do not apply here, meaning support for these policy costs is not applicable. The other elements of the Supercharger, namely relief for capacity and network costs, are not available to local businesses. Introducing a similar scheme here would increase costs for other consumers, particularly as our smaller customer base offers less scope to spread those costs compared with Britain.My officials are currently considering the potential costs and wider implications associated with replicating a similar scheme here. Any such scheme would need to take account of the Single Electricity Market arrangements and EU State Aid requirements.Any action we take must be considered carefully to avoid unintended consequences, especially at a time when many households continue to face cost-of-living pressures and fuel poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has to bring in additional relief measures for energy intensive industries, in line with those available in Great Britain.
answer_text: The British Industry Supercharger (“the Supercharger”) was launched in April 2024 to reduce electricity costs for Energy Intensive Industries (EIIs) such as steel, chemicals, cement, glass, metals, and paper. The Department for Business and Trade estimated that the Supercharger will deliver savings of between £320m and £410m for eligible EIIs from 2025. This support is expected to be worth around £24 to £31 per megawatt hour on average, with the most electricity-intensive industries benefiting the most.The Supercharger provides eligible EIIs in Britain with a package of electricity cost reductions for renewable energy levies, capacity market charges and network costs. My Department’s assessment is that some of the energy levies, such as Feed-in Tariffs and Contracts for Difference do not apply here, meaning support for these policy costs is not applicable. The other elements of the Supercharger, namely relief for capacity and network costs, are not available to local businesses. Introducing a similar scheme here would increase costs for other consumers, particularly as our smaller customer base offers less scope to spread those costs compared with Britain.My officials are currently considering the potential costs and wider implications associated with replicating a similar scheme here. Any such scheme would need to take account of the Single Electricity Market arrangements and EU State Aid requirements.Any action we take must be considered carefully to avoid unintended consequences, especially at a time when many households continue to face cost-of-living pressures and fuel poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of the Single Electricity Market on energy-intensive industries.
answer_text: The Single Electricity Market (SEM) is an all-island wholesale electricity market covering both the north and south of Ireland. It operates independently from the electricity market in Britain, with its own market rules, governance and pricing arrangements.As with all electricity consumers operating within the SEM, energy-intensive industries are subject to the wholesale market price established through the SEM arrangements, alongside other charges and factors that make up their final electricity costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures her Department is considering to prevent the relocation of high skilled industrial jobs from rural Northern Ireland to GB sites with lower energy costs.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of retaining highly skilled employment in rural areas and is acutely aware of the pressures that energy costs can place on businesses, particularly those which are energy intensive. The Department is engaging on an ongoing basis with both British Government and Irish Government counterparts to explore these issues and to consider how the North’s competitiveness can be protected within existing policy and regulatory frameworks.You will be aware of the British Industry Supercharger, which launched in April 2024 to reduce electricity costs for Energy Intensive Industries (EIIs) such as steel, chemicals, cement, glass, metals, and paper.  My Department’s assessment of that is that some of the energy levies, such as Feed-in Tariffs and Contracts for Difference do not apply here, meaning support for these policy costs is not applicable.  The other elements of the Supercharger, namely relief for capacity and network costs, are not available to local businesses. Introducing a similar scheme here would increase costs for other consumers, particularly as our smaller customer base offers less scope to spread those costs compared with Britain.My officials are currently considering the potential costs and wider implications associated with replicating a similar scheme here. Any such scheme would need to take account of the Single Electricity Market arrangements and EU State Aid requirements.Invest NI also plays a key role by delivering a suite of energy and resource efficiency supports available to eligible businesses across the North to help reduce costs through energy and resource savings thereby supporting their continued presence and growth locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of recent geopolitical instability, whether she will expedite her assessment of how Northern Ireland can support the ambition of the provision of 10 per cent domestic supply of critical minerals, within the UK Critical Minerals Strategy.
answer_text: My Department has a number of research and evidence-gathering programmes in place concerning critical minerals. Some of this work forms part of my Department’s ongoing Review of Mineral Licensing, alongside research into recycling, the circular economy, and wider supply chain resilience.	On 27 February 2026, a major milestone in the Review of Mineral Licensing was reached when I announced the launch of a Call for Evidence. The Call for Evidence provides an opportunity for the general public and stakeholders to shape a new mineral licensing policy.My Department will continue to monitor emerging evidence and the evolving global context as this work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to support low-income families struggling to afford private sector rental costs.
answer_text: Financial support for low-income families may be available through Universal Credit, subject to meeting the necessary eligibility criteria. Additional financial support may also be available through the Discretionary Support Scheme which can help those in need of short-term assistance in crisis or emergency situations. My Department’s Make the Call service is available to ensure people are receiving all the benefits, services and supports they are entitled to.Further information on Universal Credit and the Discretionary Support Scheme can be found at: www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/universal-credit and www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/discretionary-support Further information on how to contact the Make the Call service can be found at: www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/make-call-service

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the reason why Local Housing Allowance weekly rates remain frozen at 2024-2025 levels, and have not been uprated to more closely reflect current market rents.
answer_text: Local Housing Allowance (LHA) rates are reviewed annually at the Autumn Budget. The Secretary of State for Work and Pensions confirmed in his Written Ministerial Statement on 26 November 2025 that LHA rates would be maintained at their current 2024/25 levels for 2026/27. Under the principle of parity, the same restriction on the LHA rates applies to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the reason why the Local Housing Allowance rate in the Fermanagh area is the lowest in Northern Ireland; and (ii) any steps his Department is taking to ensure that housing supports are more closely aligned with current private rental market levels.
answer_text: (i) Local Housing Allowance (LHA) rates are set by reference to local private market rents rather than by ministerial discretion. In Northern Ireland LHA is determined for each Broad Rental Market Area (BRMA) using rental evidence and set at the 30th percentile of local market rents for each property size. County Fermanagh sits within the South West BRMA (Enniskillen–Omagh and surrounding rural areas). (ii) The UK Government has frozen the LHA rates since 2024/25.  Under the principle of parity, the same restriction applies to Northern Ireland. I recognise that as the LHA rates remain frozen, they may not reflect more recent increases in private rents in the Fermanagh area or the rest of Northern Ireland. Households can get extra help through the Discretionary Housing Payment (DHP) scheme if their Universal Credit or Housing Benefit does not fully cover their rent. However, each DHP is considered on a case-by-case basis, and the scheme is budget limited.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current legal and operational status of the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass project, specifically whether the Vesting Order for lands for the scheme, executed in 2021, remains in full force and effect.
answer_text: The Vesting Order for the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass Project was made on 19 July 2021 and became operative on 24 August 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to repaint road markings and reinstate reflective road studs, along the B127 in County Fermanagh, from the Share Discovery Village to the Lady Craigavon Bridge.
answer_text: Road markings serve a very important function in conveying information and requirements about the road network and poorly maintained road markings can have a negative impact on road safety.  My Department therefore undertakes regular routine maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, including defective signs and road markings, are noted. Road markings are not replaced within a routine/specific time frame, rather when approximately 30-40% of the marked area is found to be ineffective or worn away.  Where the condition of road markings falls below the required standards, they are renewed as soon as possible within the available funding limits.  Repairs are prioritised to concentrate on regulatory road markings, such as stop lines, and those longitudinal lines necessary for continued road safety.The Department awarded two new road marking contracts for the Western Division last summer; the appointed contractor is currently working through a schedule of outstanding works.  This schedule has been prioritised to complete those locations with a road safety need first concentrating on the trunk road network.Arrangements have already been agreed with the contractor to commence large scale road centre line renewal across Fermanagh during more favourable weather conditions, and the B127 route will be included on the works programme.  A priority list is being prepared for cats eye reflective road stud replacement which will be delivered later in 2026 subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the status of the applications for petroleum licences (i) PLA1/16; and (ii) PLA2 /16 , including whether the applications (a) have been closed and rejected; and (b) had any compensation granted.
answer_text: Since the Executive agreed in December 2024 to introduce a temporary moratorium on petroleum licensing, no applications for petroleum licences have been accepted or processed.On 24 March 2026, I will introduce the Petroleum Exploration and Licensing (Repeal) Bill to the Assembly. This Bill, which will permanently ban all forms of onshore petroleum exploration and development in the North, includes transitional arrangements that will address with the two current applications, PLA1/16 and PLA2/16.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any flexibility arrangements that are in place for shift workers across the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Both my Department and HSC Employers recognise that the opportunity for staff to work flexibly can greatly improve the ability of staff to balance home and work responsibilities. This, in turn, benefits the HSC as it helps to enable the service to retain skilled staff and to therefore reduce recruitment costs.All HSC employees have the opportunity to request flexible working from the first day of their employment, regardless of their role, band or service they work in.A range of work-life balance and flexible working arrangements are available within all HSC Organisations including:Fixed working patterns to give certainty over hours worked and/or location.Part-time working (any number of hours under 37.5).Flexi-time around core hours including staggered start and finish times.Compressed/elongated hours to allow work to be condensed or stretched in a regular pattern or over a specific time period, such as seasonal working.Term-time working.Job-share.Flexible retirement.Team self-rostering.Homeworking for some or all of the working pattern.All HSC staff are encouraged to discuss with their manager what Flexible Working provisions may be available in the role they are taking up, prior to commencing employment, to help balance home and work responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, following the closure of the Rates Review consultation, whether he will expedite his decision on the Small Business Rates Review to allow this sector to adjust to any final determination ahead of the end of the financial year.
answer_text: My view is that there is a significant opportunity to broaden the amount of support applied through the scheme in 2026/27 and to increase the number of businesses that receive access to Small Business Rate Relief scheme. I have already set aside around £10 million for changes to the scheme in the draft Budget 2026-29/30. I believe that this is finance well invested and I will be making that case to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of solar farms currently in the planning system.
answer_text: This information is published here:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/renewable-energy-planning-applications-list-april-2002-september-2025. The latest number of applications marked as solar farms live in the planning system as of 30 September 2025 is 4. The data is sourced from Accredited Official Statistics, and the next release is due at the start of April covering the period up to 31 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of solar farms currently in the planning system, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: My Department publishes a significant suite of data on renewable energy planning applications. This includes data on single wind turbines, wind farms, hydroelectric, solar panels, biomass / anaerobic digestors amongst others. I would refer the member to tables 7.2 – 7.4 in the attached links for details of these application types by council area. In terms of solar farms, I would refer the member to individual councils for more details on this application type. https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/system/files/2025-12/planning-statistics-q2-2025-26-bulletin.pdfhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/system/files/2025-12/planning-statistics-2025-26-Q2-tables.ods

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on Lisnaskea Health and Care Centre.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of businesses her Department engaged with, regarding the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill, as a percentage of the total number of businesses registered in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In 2024, my Department carried out public consultation on proposals for reforms to employment law, including an Employment Rights Bill. That consultation was open to 100% of businesses in the North, and prompted responses from 25 industry representative bodies which have a combined local membership of over 18,000 individuals and businesses. The consultation has been followed by over 100 engagements at which many businesses have been in attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of long term stays beyond two days in an elective surgery hub, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are currently three dedicated elective surgery hubs across Northern Ireland: Daisy Hill Hospital, Mater Hospital and South West Acute Hospital.  Table 1 below sets out the number of long-term stays beyond two days in each of these for all elective admissions, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
