This data is operational and has not been validated by Trusts. Data recording processes differ on Epic and so data may not be comparable to PAS.1 FY 25/26 data available up to 31/01/2026.Funding has recently been secured through the Elective Care Framework to enhance pre-operative assessment services across the region. This will improve patient optimisation and support a reduction in post-operative length of stay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of how Northern Ireland can support the UK Critical Minerals Strategy’s ambition to secure at least 10 per cent of UK critical mineral needs from domestic sources.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken an assessment of how the North could support the British Government’s Critical Minerals Strategy ambition to secure at least 10 per cent critical mineral demand from domestic sources.	My Department has a number of research and evidence-gathering programmes in place concerning critical minerals. Some of this work forms part of my Department’s ongoing Review of Mineral Licensing, alongside research into recycling, the circular economy, and wider supply chain resilience.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the European Critical Raw Materials Act, particularly the opportunity for Northern Ireland to support the Act’s ambitions, to ensure EU access to a secure and sustainable supply of critical raw materials, enabling Europe to meet its 2030 climate and digital objectives.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the European Critical Raw Materials Act and its objectives to strengthen the security and sustainability of supply chains for critical raw materials in support of the EU’s climate and digital ambitions.		My Department has a number of research and evidence-gathering programmes in place concerning critical minerals. Some of this work forms part of my Department’s ongoing Review of Mineral Licensing, alongside research into recycling, the circular economy, and wider supply chain resilience.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions that have taken place with her Department and the Department for Business and Trade, regarding the implications of the UK’s Vision 2035 Critical Minerals Strategy, for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Departmental officials engage, on a routine basis, with the Department for Business and Trade (DBT) and with the other Devolved Administrations through an established forum for information sharing on minerals and critical minerals. This engagement has included high-level discussion on the British Government’s Critical Mineral Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made on the potential of the mining sector to contribute towards the four pillars of Northern Ireland’s economic vision, good jobs, productivity, regional balance and decarbonisation.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken an assessment of the mining sector’s potential contribution to my Economic Vision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any assessment her Department has been made of the UK Modern Industrial Strategy’s conclusion that critical minerals represent a foundational industry for high-growth sectors; and (ii) any steps her Department is taking to develop Northern Ireland’s critical minerals sector.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the conclusions set out in the British Government’s Modern Industrial Strategy regarding the role of critical minerals in supporting a range of high-growth and strategically important sectors.		My Department has a number of research and evidence-gathering programmes in place concerning critical minerals. Some of this work forms part of my Department’s ongoing Review of Mineral Licensing, alongside research into recycling, the circular economy, and wider supply chain resilience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will fund a review into the impact of the rates revaluation exercise on hotels, pubs and restaurants.
answer_text: Funding a review into the impact of the rates revaluation on hotels, pubs and restaurants is not necessary. The Reval2026 exercise has been stopped, and no revised values have been implemented. As a result, hotels pubs and restaurants will not experience any impact from a revaluation.The decision to stop the exercise followed concerns raised by the hospitality sector, political representatives and the wider business community. Bringing the process to a halt creates space for constructive engagement with hospitality and other stakeholders so that their views can be considered, where possible, within the statutory rating framework.My priority is to ensure a fair, transparent and evidencebased rating system for all ratepayers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support available for trade associations and trade bodies, such as the Federation of Small Businesses in Northern Ireland and the Northern Ireland Food To Go Association, in light of the services they provide to the small business sector.
answer_text: I recognise the important work trade associations do to promote the interests of their members including, often, the provision of specialised guidance and support services. Another role of trade associations is interaction with government, including lobbying and advocacy on behalf of their members in relation to the development of policy and legislation. In that context, it is important that trade associations are independent of government and should ultimately be sustainable based on their fees-based membership model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Small Business Rates Relief Scheme.
answer_text: The consultation on Small Business Rate Relief Options closed on 29 January. I am now considering the detail of responses and advice from my officials, and will be bringing forward proposals to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any protections that exist for non-domestic tenants in the face of rising rents on high streets.
answer_text: Rents for non-domestic tenants are a private commercial matter between landlords and tenants, and my Department has no remit to intervene in these arrangements. However, I recognise the pressures faced by businesses operating on our high streets. To support local economic development, I have established 11 Local Economic Partnerships across the north, backed by a £45 million Regional Balance Fund. These partnerships are developing projects tailored to the needs of their areas, including initiatives to assist high street businesses, where they deem it appropriate. My Department also provides wider support to businesses through Invest NI and InterTradeIreland. In addition, councils offer direct assistance to local businesses through the Go Succeed service. Responsibility for town centre regeneration rests with the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support available for businesses that have moved beyond the inception and early growth stages, in order to sustain their operations and maintain their market share.
answer_text: My department, through Invest NI provides a broad range of support to all businesses across the North primarily in the form of advice, guidance, innovation grants and access to finance. Invest NI maintains the nibusinessinfo.co.uk website, which provides over 10,000 pages of guidance, tools and resources on broad range of areas such as starting a business, regulations, finding property and funding, and employment guidance. It serves as the primary resource for advice for local businesses, entrepreneurs and employers. To ensure that all businesses, regardless of their size, can access the funding they need to scale to the next level, Invest NI provides a range of finance solutions under the banner of ‘Access to Finance’. These funds include both debt and equity support. To ensure businesses can access the support they need to innovate, Invest NI provides financial support through its Innovation Voucher and Business Innovation Grant schemes. These initiatives provide grants to help local small and medium sized businesses develop and bring to market new products and services, or improve existing processes, all with the aim of increasing profits and driving growth. Invest NI also provides expert advice to all businesses to help them to operate more efficiently, implement waste management strategies, reduce energy costs and contribute to a greener economy.   In addition to above, Invest NI provides targeted support for businesses with investment projects that align with its priorities and policy objectives. This includes helping businesses:Begin, or expand, exporting, explore international markets, attend trade missions, and understand dual market access Invest in digitalisation, innovation, R&D, and protect intellectual propertyImprove management capability, workforce skills, productivity and create jobs; andImplement operational excellence, improve supply chain management, and find and develop appropriate property solutions.Responsibility for local economic development in the North lies with the local councils and their Go Succeed initiative provides expert advice, mentoring and grants for start-up, and established businesses, seeking to maintain and grow their market share.Details on all the support above can be found on the Invest NI website. If the Member would like to discuss further, she should contact Invest NI’s Head of Regional Business for the Western Region, Ethna McNamee, and if any business would like to discuss the support available to them, they should contact Invest NI’s Business Support Team on 0800 181 4422.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any remedial packages his Department will offer farmers in the Lough Neagh basin area to address damage to lands, drainage, fencing and habitats following flooding caused by unseasonal levels of rain.
answer_text: At this stage, a remedial support package for farmers in the Lough Neagh basin area affected by unseasonal levels of rain is not planned, however I am continuing to monitor the situation.	The SAP’s Farm Sustainability Payment Scheme is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and help withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively, encouraging businesses to become more environmentally sustainable and efficient		A Crisis Framework is being developed as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) This framework will provide a pre-defined approach for government intervention in the event of a significant crisis impacting the agricultural sector in Northern Ireland		The SAP is a strong example of climate adaptation action being progressed by my Department, as we work with and support our agricultural sector to be more resilient to the more frequent and severe impacts brought by climate change. As such, delivery of the SAP is a key action in the draft Northern Ireland’s third Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3), which my Department has developed, with input from all Departments. 		As a cross-cutting Programme with more than 280 actions to build climate resilience, the NICCAP3 requires Executive agreement. I am currently engaging with my Executive colleagues to seek their agreement to finalise NICCAP3 and lay it in this Assembly as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any long term plans in place to address flooding in the Lough Neagh basin area.
answer_text: As you will be aware from my response to AQW 40620/22-27 my Department has the statutory obligation to regulate and control the water level in Lough Neagh within the range of 12.45m to 12.60m above the Belfast Ordnance level datum, insofar as climatic conditions allow. In managing the Lough water levels a range of stakeholder considerations are taken into account, including those from the fisheries, recreation, tourism and conservation sectors. However, despite the best efforts of my Department, it is not always possible to manage the water level within the statutory limits during prolonged periods of heavy rainfall, falling on already saturated ground such as that experienced in the past number of weeks. Officials will continue to monitor the water level daily and will continue to manage Lough Neagh water level in accordance with the current legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has engaged with the UK and US Governments on the strategic importance of minerals and critical minerals.
answer_text: I have not engaged directly with either the British Government or the US Government on matters relating to minerals or critical minerals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will reassess the river drainage systems for effectiveness in the Lough Neagh Basin area, in response to current, unprecedented, levels of flood water.
answer_text: In relation to water levels in Lough Neagh, my Department manages operations under the Lough Neagh Levels Scheme (1955), which sets a control range of 12.450m–12.600m OD (Belfast), subject to weather conditions. During prolonged heavy rainfall, inflows to the Lough can significantly exceed outflows at Toome due to natural restrictions in the Lower Bann channel.A review of the 2015/16 winter flooding (the Strong Report) concluded that meaningful reductions in peak water levels would require removal of natural channel restrictions, which would be prohibitively expensive and environmentally damaging. The report also found that alternative floodgate operating regimes would have had an insignificant impact on flood levels. There are therefore no plans to reassess the current operating regime.Since mid-January 2026 all gates at Toome have been fully open, with no restriction on outflow.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any immediate remedial steps that are being taken to address the serious flooding in surrounding agricultural lands in the Lough Neagh basin.
answer_text: You may be aware that my Department has the statutory obligation to regulate and control the water level in Lough Neagh within the range of 12.45m to 12.60m above the Belfast Ordnance level datum, insofar as climatic conditions allow. In managing the Lough water levels a range of stakeholder considerations are taken into account, including those from the fisheries, recreation, tourism and conservation sectors. However, despite the best efforts of my Department, it is not always possible to manage the water level within the statutory limits during prolonged periods of heavy rainfall, falling on already saturated ground such as that experienced in the past number of weeks. The water level in Lough Neagh is controlled by five sluice gates at Toome. The gates have been fully open (all clear) since Mid-January 2026. My Department does not expect to make any adjustments to the gates until the water level drops below the level of 12.600m and closer to 12.450m above Ordnance Datum. It may also be helpful for you to know that the water level of Lough Neagh and the gate opening status is published each working day on the Department’s website and can be accessed via the following link https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/lough-neagh-levels.   My Department is doing everything it can to manage this flooding issue and we remain on a heightened state of alert. For any risk of flooding to life or property the most appropriate way to report this is to use the Flooding Incident Line (FIL) number, 0300 2000 100. The FIL is a flood reporting platform and calls will be passed to the relevant agency who will assess the call and respond with measures as appropriate including, sandbags and pumping, if required.My officials continue to monitor the water level daily.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether (i) the Mid South West Growth Deal remains on track for approval of its business plan, in December 2026; and (ii) funding drawdown is contingent upon that business plan being formally signed off.
answer_text: The Mid South West Growth Deal remains on track for signing their Deal in Winter 2026-27.  Funding drawdown is contingent upon the Deal being signed, associated post Deal documentation being submitted and agreed, which includes the signing of a tripartite Financial Agreement between Council, Treasury and my department.  Following this, each of the individual projects will require the signing of a Contract for Funding before funding can flow. The exception to this is funding for the A4 Enniskillen Bypass; it was agreed by the Executive in February 2024 that additional funding of £16.2 million be allocated to this project, and  it was also agreed to commit the £12.5 million from the Executive’s contribution to the MSW Growth Deal, enabling funding to flow to the A4 Enniskillen Bypass in advance of Deal signing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average wait time for disability adaptions in Housing Executive properties, as of February 2026.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Housing adaptations are categorised as either minor or major. Minor adaptations typically involve smallscale items such as grab rails or lever taps, which can be delivered through the response maintenance service and may not always require an Occupational Therapist (OT) referral. Major adaptations, by contrast, require an OT referral and generally involve more substantial internal or external works, often requiring design input and statutory approvals.As the service operates on a demand-led basis, there is no maintained waiting list or average waiting time for minor adaptations. Instead, works are carried out in accordance with the response times set dependant on the required works.The priority classifications below apply to minor disabled adaptation works delivered via the response maintenance service.TASK COMPLETE ON-SITE DATES FOR DISABLED ADAPTATIONS WORKSCategory A1To be completed within Five Business Days.Category A2To be completed within Twenty Business Days.Category A3To be commenced and completed in the specified period on the Task Order request.The A3 classification is used for minor adaptation works which would exceed the A2 priority time frames. Potential reasons for this include the extent of the works or the need for specialist materials/equipment.The average wait times for Major Adaptations for persons with a disability from April 2021 - February 2026 is 112 weeks. This timeframe can vary due to a number of factors including but not limited to; complexity of design solutions required, obtaining statutory approvals, availability of decant properties, and internal resource and contractor capacity.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the Education Authority's remit in addressing traffic issues outside schools, including how responsibilities are divided between his Department, the PSNI, and local council areas.
answer_text: While the safety of all children is a key priority, neither the Education Authority (EA), the Department of Education or individual schools have any statutory responsibility for traffic management or road safety on the public highway.The EA authorise School Crossing Patrols (SCP) to operate at specific locations, but their primary duty is to assist pedestrians to cross the road safely at that location and not to address broader road safety issues, such as parking or speeding. Parents remain primarily responsible for ensuring their child's safety, even when an SCP is operating. Responsibility for traffic management beyond the school boundary lies with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) and with the PSNI for enforcement. DfI has advised that double yellow lines located at school entrances are always enforceable, including during school holidays. Some school entrance locations are included as part of Traffic Attendants’ routine patrols and DfI can also respond to specific requests for enforcement should parking at a particular location cause a concern. The majority of “School Keep Clear” markings are advisory only and, as such, cannot be enforced by Traffic Attendants. However, where “School Keep Clear” markings have been legislated, these will be accompanied by signage advising of restriction dates and times, which can be enforced by Traffic Attendants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the most recent meeting of the Mid South West Growth Deal Steering Group.
answer_text: My Department is not represented on this group, nor does it receive updates.  The group is led and managed by the three local councils involved in the Mid South West Growth Deal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement that has taken place between his Department and the Department for the Economy, to develop a coordinated response to the impact of the Republic of Ireland’s reduced hospitality VAT rate on Northern Ireland businesses.
answer_text: I recognise fully the significant challenges facing our hospitality industry, particularly in relation to the impact of higher VAT rates here, relative to the South. I have repeatedly raised this with the British Government, most recently in a letter to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST) ahead of the British Government’s Autumn Budget. Unfortunately, the British Government has so far failed to take the decisive action needed to provide a level playing field for our businesses to compete in the all-island economy.I have also taken a coordinated approach with the Economy Minister. The Economy Minister and I met jointly with representatives from the local hospitality sector on 13 January 2026 to hear their concerns and discuss proposals on how to best support our businesses. Following this I wrote to the CST to request a meeting, alongside the Economy Minister, and local hospitality sector industry bodies, to highlight the urgent need for the British Government to consider reforms to the VAT system, to support our local hospitality businesses.Ultimately, VAT remains an Excepted Matter, and the power to change VAT rates rests solely with the British Government. For my part, I will continue to make the case for a tax system which is fair, proportionate and progressive, and allows our businesses to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any assessment his Department has made of the competitiveness gap facing Northern Ireland’s hospitality sector, as a result of differing VAT rates with the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQW 40283/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any financial support interventions his Department is planning to introduce to support the indigenous hospitality industry, in light of the Republic of Ireland implementing a nine per cent VAT rate for hospitality, from July 2026.
answer_text: In May 2025, my department published the Cost of Doing Business research report, which highlighted the disparity of VAT rates across the all-island economy and the negative implications for businesses, especially for hospitality businesses, operating near the border. For example, modelling the VAT differential between hospitality businesses operating in the North compared to those in the South, this found there could be annual VAT savings of £71,000 for a small guesthouse, and £545,000 for a large hotel operating in the South, under VAT rates of 9%. Further scenarios available in the report can be accessed here.I shared this report directly with the then Chief Secretary to the Treasury to make the case for a reduction in VAT to enable our hospitality businesses to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy. I also wrote to the current Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 17 February to request a meeting, alongside the Economy Minister, and local hospitality sector industry bodies to highlight the urgent need for the British Government to consider reforms to the VAT system to support our local hospitality businesses.I have repeatedly engaged with the British Government to press for changes in the VAT system. Unfortunately, the British Government has so far failed to act on my calls for change, and the evidence which has been provided to support this case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any financial modelling his Department has undertaken to assess and mitigate the risk of hospitality business closures, arising from more competitive VAT rates in the Republic of Ireland, particularly in border areas.
answer_text: In May 2025, my department published the Cost of Doing Business research report, which highlighted the disparity of VAT rates across the all-island economy and the negative implications for businesses, especially for hospitality businesses, operating near the border. For example, modelling the VAT differential between hospitality businesses operating in the North compared to those in the South, this found there could be annual VAT savings of £71,000 for a small guesthouse, and £545,000 for a large hotel operating in the South, under VAT rates of 9%. Further scenarios available in the report can be accessed here.I shared this report directly with the then Chief Secretary to the Treasury to make the case for a reduction in VAT to enable our hospitality businesses to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy. I also wrote to the current Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 17 February to request a meeting, alongside the Economy Minister, and local hospitality sector industry bodies to highlight the urgent need for the British Government to consider reforms to the VAT system to support our local hospitality businesses.I have repeatedly engaged with the British Government to press for changes in the VAT system. Unfortunately, the British Government has so far failed to act on my calls for change, and the evidence which has been provided to support this case.

answer_text: It is not possible to identify individuals who progress directly from school into ApprenticeshipsNI or Higher Level Apprenticeships (HLAs). For ApprenticeshipsNI, data is only available for those aged 16 to 19 at the point they begin their apprenticeship. Similarly, because data on students moving straight from school into a HLA is not fully captured, reporting is limited to HLA starts aged 16 to 19 within the academic year. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has to bring apprenticeship policy and funding criteria in line with those operating elsewhere in the United Kingdom.
answer_text: Apprenticeship policy and funding are devolved matters, and each region has developed arrangements that best suit its own economic and labour-market context. The ApprenticeshipNI programme is built on a demand-led model that prioritises collaboration between training providers and employers. Currently my Department provides 100% funding for the off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship: totalling almost £50 million per year. This includes Level 2 and Level 3 apprenticeships for all participants, regardless of age, across all sectors. My Department remains committed to ensuring our apprenticeship offering is competitive and effective. While there are no plans at present to fundamentally alter the current model to mirror any single approach elsewhere, we will continue to monitor and review the wider landscape, ensuring our policy and funding criteria remain aligned with the evolving needs of our local economy while reflecting best practices identified across these islands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the progress of actions set out in the Apprenticeship Action Plan.
answer_text: Delivery against the Apprenticeship Action Plan has begun strongly, marked by the successful launch of the new AppsNI and HLA programmes in September 2025. A range of further actions are already in progress, including delivery of the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund, the Pre-Apprenticeship Academies, and ongoing work to enhance our communications. Additional initiatives such as the Apprenticeship Navigators and the Apprenticeship Connections pilots are scheduled to commence in the coming weeks, with completion targeted by March 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) assessment her Department has made of the economic impact of public-sector apprenticeships in Northern Ireland; and (ii) plans her Department has to boost uptake and availability of these.
answer_text: Public Sector Apprenticeships (PSAs) align with my economic mission and priorities: increasing the proportion of working age people in good jobs, promoting regional balance, and raising productivity. My Department has considered PSA’s contribution in the context of wider labourmarket needs. Continued apprenticeship provision can contribute towards meeting targets in the NI Skills Barometer. Through the Apprenticeship Action Plan my Department has committed to exploring what is possible within the current funding framework, however ongoing budget pressures may restrict how far we can take this work forward in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he will be in a position to reinstate the £6.6 million in funding for the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass project.
answer_text: It is a matter of regret that some elected members circulated erroneous and misleading statements on funding for the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.  As a project within the Mid South West Growth Deal, and to which there is an existing Executive commitment, the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass will have first call on future Executive capital funding allocations, as and when the funding is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the impact the surrender of £6.6 million in funding for the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass Project, will have on the Mid South West Growth Deal.
answer_text: The impact of the surrender of £6.6 million in funding for the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass will have no direct impact on the overall Mid South West Growth Deal, as the Deal will still continue work on Outline Business Case (OBC) development for all their other projects.  It is regrettable that inaccurate statements by some members around a routine exercise of redistributing Executive funding resulted in confusion around funding for the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.As Members will be aware, ringfenced funding can only be used for its designated purpose. If a department cannot spend funding within the designated year for the purpose for which it has been allocated, then it is returned for reallocation. This does not mean funding is lost; projects for which there is an Executive commitment, such as the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass, will receive the funding in future years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the impact the surrender of £6.6 million in Capital DEL will have on the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass Project.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 39959/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has assessed the need for a dedicated financial assistance scheme for farmers whose land along rivers has been impacted by flooding.
answer_text: Work on the development of a Crisis Framework as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) has commenced.  I intend to bring forward plans for this framework in the coming months which will provide a pre-defined approach for government intervention in the event of a significant crisis impacting the agricultural sector in Northern IrelandMore broadly, the SAP’s Farm Sustainability Payment Scheme is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and to provide a balance between providing a safety net which will help farm businesses withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively and encouraging them to become more environmentally sustainable, efficient and resilient.The SAP is a strong example of climate adaptation action being progressed by my Department, as we work with and support our agricultural sector to be more resilient to the more frequent and severe impacts brought by climate change. As such, delivery of the SAP is a key action in the draft Northern Ireland’s third Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3), which my Department has developed with input from all Departments. As a cross-cutting Programme with more than 280 actions to build climate resilience, the NICCAP3 requires Executive agreement. I am currently engaging with my Executive colleagues to seek their agreement to finalise NICCAP3 and lay it in this Assembly as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support schemes that are available to farmers who have experienced flood damage to agricultural land, including the deposition of debris following recent flooding events.
answer_text: There are currently no specific support schemes available to farmers who have experienced flood damage to agricultural land including the deposition of debris following recent flooding events. Work on the development of a Crisis Framework as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) has commenced.  I intend to bring forward plans for this framework in the coming months which will provide a pre-defined approach for government intervention in the event of a significant crisis impacting the agricultural sector in Northern IrelandMore broadly, the SAP’s Farm Sustainability Payment Scheme is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and to provide a balance between providing a safety net which will help farm businesses withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively and encouraging them to become more environmentally sustainable, efficient and resilient.The SAP is a strong example of climate adaptation action being progressed by my Department, as we work with and support our agricultural sector to be more resilient to the more frequent and severe impacts brought by climate change. As such, delivery of the SAP is a key action in the draft Northern Ireland’s third Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3), which my Department has developed with input from all Departments. As a cross-cutting Programme with more than 280 actions to build climate resilience, the NICCAP3 requires Executive agreement. I am currently engaging with my Executive colleagues to seek their agreement to finalise NICCAP3 and lay it in this Assembly as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the costs of the most recent resurfacing works on the A46 Lough Shore Road, Enniskillen; and (ii) for her assessment of its effectiveness.
answer_text: The most recent resurfacing works were completed on the Loughshore Road Autumn 2025 costing £414,000.The Loughshore Route is subject to naturally occurring geotechnical ground movement at several locations along the A46 route, two of which are within the site limits of the recent resurfacing scheme.One area of the carriageway at the western site boundary of the scheme has experienced underlying ground movement resulting in a depression in the new carriageway surface. The Department has installed a temporary lane closure while the matter is further investigated. The resurfacing works completed did not cause or contribute to this issue as settlement is a recurring problem at this specific location.  Prior to the resurfacing scheme, a large section of the site had minimal verge width with a few areas with no verge or supporting embankment at all.  In the interests of improving longer term road safety along this winding section of carriageway, additional verge width was provided with a view to a permanent vehicle restraint system installation at a later stage when ground settlement is complete.  The new verge embankment has also settled at two locations and remedial works are planned over the coming weeks to address this. Settlement may continue over a longer period and officials will continue to monitor this location. This section of the Loughshore Road closely straddles Lough Erne and water seepage and run off will also impact on the condition of the underground soil structure and the load bearing capacity of the road sub-base.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has undertaken any assessment of access to cash across rural constituencies, including Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: I am deeply concerned about citizens not being able to access cash in their local community, particularly in our rural towns and villages. This is an issue I raised directly with LINK, who operate the main ATM network, and the wider banking sector, at my Banking Roundtable on 3 December 2025. LINK is also the body responsible for carrying out cash access assessments, either following a community request, or where a cash access facility is closing or has closed. LINK indicate that they consider a range of factors, including the location and available hours of ATMs. You can find out more about LINK’s access to cash process here.I have also been pressing the banking sector to accelerate the rollout of banking hubs and challenging the criteria used to determine whether an area qualifies for one, especially for rural areas like Fermanagh and South Tyrone, where there are currently none. I stressed that their criteria need to be appropriate and reflect our particular circumstances here, including cross border travel and commerce.I understand LINK has recently undertaken seven assessments in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, including in Lisnaskea, Dungannon, Enniskillen, Irvinestown and Clogher. It is disappointing that in each case, LINK concluded that no additional services were required.My officials are now preparing a report setting out the key issues raised at my Banking Roundtable, which I will share with Treasury and the Financial Conduct Authority to seek appropriate action. The report will emphasise the need for authorities to maintain access to cash, particularly in rural areas, and to review the criteria used by LINK in its accesstocash assessments to ensure they better reflect local requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any assessment that has been made of the impact of limited access to ATMs in rural areas, particularly on shift workers who are unable to access cash during shop opening hours; and (ii) any steps being taken to mitigate any negative effects on families. and the rural economy.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 39626/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement his Department has had with (i) banks; (ii) ATM operators; or (iii) the UK Government, regarding the provision of ATMs in rural areas.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 39626/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre report on the economic impact of the Extended Travel Authorisation scheme will be published.
answer_text: Officials from my Department are currently engaged with UUEPC in preliminary work including stakeholder engagement and comparative policy analysis. 		In order to provide the best possible basis for assessing any economic impacts the Electronic Travel Authorisation scheme may have, a number of key data inputs are required, which are expected to be released later this year. 		Once received, these will be assessed, alongside the qualitative elements currently under consideration, and the report published in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether Land and Property Services are sufficiently resourced to deal with, and adjudicate, the incoming case appeals from hospitality business owners requesting rates re-evaluation before 1 April 2026.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to my oral statement, and the subsequent debate in the Assembly on 2 February, regarding Reval 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact that delays in Article 161 approvals are having on new housing delivery.
answer_text: The Article 161 process is an operational matter for NI Water.  However, NI Water has advised that the Article 161 process, for the adoption of a sewer, is a developer-led process, with applications being approved by NI Water.NI Water has advised that from its perspective, there are no delays in the approval of Article 161 applications. NI Water has further advised that in 2025, 186 applications were received, of which 183 received a response within the 60-day Service Level Agreement target, resulting in 98% compliance. If there is a specific case you are referring to please forward to my Private Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any protections the Education Authority provides for school patrol personnel enduring severe weather conditions.
answer_text: The Education Authority is responsible for employing School Crossing Patrol Officers (SCPs). It has advised me that SCPs are provided with an appropriate uniform which complies with national guidance. This includes a fulllength coat, a hat or cap and gloves. During periods of extreme weather conditions, schools are likely to be closed and, in such circumstances, SCPs are not required to be on duty. SCPs are on duty for a limited time each day and whilst on duty, they are required to be visible to motorists and to assist children to cross the road; when not on duty, SCPs may access school facilities. The installation of shelters for SCPs would require capital investment, planning permission, and, in most cases, the acquisition of additional land. In the current financial climate, with many competing priorities across the schools' estate, it would appear difficult to justify the provision of shelters with such limited utility as an efficient use of public funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any mitigations in place for hospitality accommodation operators, where pre-booked accommodation will fall within new financial year prices, adjusted to take account of potential rates increases.
answer_text: As the Member will be aware, I announced on 29 January that I was stopping the Reval 2026 process.Rate bills for the new financial year will therefore be issued on the basis of the current valuation list, meaning no changes arise from any revised values.I would direct the Member to my oral statement to the Assembly:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she intends to take to support the hospitality, pub and food to go sectors, which are facing rates hikes, to remain in business.
answer_text: I am aware of the increasing pressures on hospitality businesses due to recent changes to employer’s national insurance contributions and national minimum wage. These changes come on the back of challenging years during the pandemic for the sector.You will be aware that the Minister of Finance has stopped the Reval 2026 process, and I know this has been welcomed by our tourism and hospitality industries. Like Minister O’Dowd, I am in listening mode and will continue to engage with the sector on their concerns.In recent weeks, Minister O’Dowd and I have met with Hospitality Ulster and the NI Food To Go Association to discuss their proposal for a reduced VAT pilot scheme here given the VAT differential with the south. I will continue to advocate, alongside the Minister of Finance, for a fairer tax regime for our local businesses, allowing them to compete on an all-island basis. We have committed to seek a meeting with Treasury to discuss the proposal for a reduced VAT pilot scheme.My Department and partner organisations continue to provide a broad range of supports to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of whether shelters should be provided for school patrol personnel as a matter of course.
answer_text: The Education Authority is responsible for employing School Crossing Patrol Officers (SCPs). It has advised me that SCPs are provided with an appropriate uniform which complies with national guidance. This includes a fulllength coat, a hat or cap and gloves. During periods of extreme weather conditions, schools are likely to be closed and, in such circumstances, SCPs are not required to be on duty. SCPs are on duty for a limited time each day and whilst on duty, they are required to be visible to motorists and to assist children to cross the road; when not on duty, SCPs may access school facilities. The installation of shelters for SCPs would require capital investment, planning permission, and, in most cases, the acquisition of additional land. In the current financial climate, with many competing priorities across the schools' estate, it would appear difficult to justify the provision of shelters with such limited utility as an efficient use of public funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work his Department is doing to mitigate the impact from (i) current; and (ii) future, global economic shocks.
answer_text: While global economic shocks are beyond the control of any administration, my department works with The Executive Office Civil Contingencies team to share intelligence and co-ordinate cross-government action for emergency responses. This includes progressing recommendations from the COVID-19 Inquiry on a cross departmental basis, to strengthen future emergency planning and help reduce the economic impact of major events. My department is using insights from the recent Module 1 and 2 reports to improve its governance and preparedness for future global emergencies, and will also consider any further findings from the future Module 9 report which will focus on the economic response.Additionally, following the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, my department developed Procurement Policy Note (PPN) 03/21, Supply Chain Resilience, to assess and strengthen the resilience of government supply chains. This ensures departments map supply chains for their critical supplies’ contracts, and where needed, put in place mitigating measures to minimise disruption to the delivery of essential public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the equitable provision of sensory units between rural and urban settings.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of sensory units within community settings, in each county.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any work her Department is doing to mitigate the impact from (i) current; and (ii) future, global economic shocks.
answer_text: While global economic shocks are beyond the control of any administration, my department works closely with the Executive Office Civil Contingencies team to share intelligence and coordinate crossgovernment action across a range of areas, including supply chains and economic disruption. I also harness the insights of my Tariff Working Group, Ministerial Engagement Forum and regular engagement with the Chief Executives and Chairs of the department’s delivery partners to monitor and, where possible, mitigate, emerging challenges.

This data will be available in early March 2026. The EA Catering Service currently employs 4,771 staff across all school meal hubs.
