question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail any current strategies her Department has in place to develop and implement a dedicated women skills-based learning programme; and (ii) for her assessment on whether such a programme will (a) offer more opportunities for women; and (b) tackle economic inactivity.
answer_text: My Department provides targeted support particularly for women through a suite of programmes and initiatives many of which include support for childcare costs where appropriate. The Inclusive Futures programme, funded by the Skills Fund, helps female returners gain skills, reskill, or upskill through industry relevant accredited qualifications and work placements at all six Further Education Colleges. It offers childcare and travel support to reduce participation barriers and currently supports 120 places. The programme provides flexible, tailored pathways for women to re-enter learning or employment and aims to support their transition into sustainable employment or further training.The Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund specifically promotes uptake from three underrepresented groups across apprenticeship programmes, including women.  Projects under this Challenge Fund include Women’s Tec which is targeting low female representation in sectors like construction, engineering and mechanics, using a holistic cross-sectoral approach. Workplus leads a second project focused on supply and demand barriers to greater female participation for women in software apprenticeships. Both projects run until late summer 2026 and will be evaluated to inform future apprenticeships policy.As part of the Magee Expansion’s Widening Participation Action Plan, a STEM Pathways to Learning programme will be established to raise early awareness of STEM subjects and careers. The initiative will aim to strengthen the pipeline of underrepresented learners into Higher Education, particularly girls from socially and economically disadvantaged areas in the North West, by supporting their future progression into STEM education and training. In terms of impact, our programmes are well placed to offer more opportunities for women by providing flexible pathways into education, training, and employment while diversifying opportunities for women to participate in skills-based learning.  We will continue to review our offering, and associated support, to ensure that our programmes support those who face barriers, including females.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has had any engagement with employers, to help combat women's economic inactivity, by offering more flexible working conditions or supported working environments.
answer_text: My forthcoming Employment Rights Bill will include enhancements of the right to make a statutory flexible working request for all employees.  To make it easier for people to ask their employer for flexible working arrangements, the right to request will apply from the first day of employment; employees will be permitted to make two statutory flexible working requests rather than the current 1 in a 12-month period; and employees will no longer be required to state the potential impact of a flexible working request. I intend that these enhancements will support employees, including women, to access and stay in employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that women's economic inactivity rate remains high, to detail (i) any engagement her Department has had with women who have lived experience of economic inactivity; and (ii) how these experiences are being incorporated in any policy development for skill-based or lifelong learning policies.
answer_text: It is vital that our policies and programmes are developed with input from those who will benefit from them and have lived experience, to ensure that they are fit for purpose.  As such, we continue to engage with a range of representative groups at development and review stages.  We already offer several programmes that are specifically designed in collaboration with women, including initiatives tailored for those with lived experience of economic inactivity.  Engagement has been undertaken with further education colleges and community-based organisations that work directly with women facing barriers to reentering learning or employment. These insights have informed the design of current provision, including the Inclusive Futures and the Women Returners Access Programme (WRAP).  We continue to engage with women with lived experience of barriers through evaluation of previous female returner programmes and the collection of participant feedback and case studies.  This engagement continues to shape wider skills provision to ensure it addresses and reflects the needs of cohorts including women.The Inclusive Futures programme, funded through the Skills Fund, is delivered across all six further education colleges.  It reflects feedback from women facing barriers by providing flexible delivery, accredited qualifications, work placements and financial support for childcare and travel.  The Women's Return Access Programme (WRAP), part of the Step Up initiative, has supported many women back into education or the labour market since April 2023. Evaluations of WRAP and personalized participant case studies show that mentoring is a crucial feature of its programme, helping to build confidence, develop key skills, and provide both career and personal support. As a result, mentor-led study programmes have been developed, enabling women to overcome barriers and feel a greater sense of belonging.The Department has engaged with the Alliance for Lifelong Learning and women affected by barriers to education and employment through a range of recent events delivered by the Forum for Adult learning NI (FALNI) and AONTAS.   These engagements and their outputs constitute part of the Department’s evidence base in relation to Lifelong Learning.The Department’s refreshed approach to widening participation places learnercentred support at its core, recognising the specific barriers faced by leaners, including women. The proposals currently out for consultation have been informed by evidence and extensive codesign with stakeholders, and are intended to support more flexible and inclusive pathways through higher education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans Waterways Ireland has to incentivise private craft and fleet owners on Lough Erne, to transition to alternative and low emission fuel sources, in light of her Ministerial Statement on the North South Ministerial Council Inland Waterways Sectoral Meeting.
answer_text: Waterways Ireland currently has no plans to incentivise private craft and fleet owners to transition to alternative and low emission fuel sources. Waterways Ireland recognises the benefits of transition to lower-emission fuels and technologies across the inland waterways sector and is currently taking action, within its operational remit, to reduce its own emissions.Whilst the organisation actively promotes Lough Erne for recreational boating, it has no powers to regulate fuel choice for private craft or fleet owners using the navigation, nor does it supply fuel or operate bunkering facilities.Waterways Ireland will however be promoting the success of its Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil scheme and will continue to encourage the transition to lower-emission practices through setting an example and highlighting positive outcomes when interacting with its customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average response time for the restoration of road markings, within the Mid Ulster local council area, as of January 2026.
answer_text: Road markings are not replaced within a routine/specific time frame, rather when approximately 30-40% of the marked area is ineffective or worn away. Where the condition of road markings falls below the required standards, they are renewed as soon as possible within the available funding limits. Repairs are prioritised to concentrate on regulatory road markings and those longitudinal lines necessary for continued road safety. The Department does not hold readily available information on the average response time for the restoration of road markings.My Department awarded a new Road Marking Contract for the Mid Ulster District Council area in August 2025, however there is currently a backlog of road markings for renewal. Additionally, the current winter weather conditions are unsuitable for large scale marking renewal works. Once more favourable weather conditions return, the Department will be prioritising road marking renewal across the district to clear the current backlog.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department’s road safety initiatives include any policies or guidance on the maintenance of road markings, particularly in areas of heavy traffic usage.
answer_text: Road safety is a key priority for my Department and we are fully committed to reducing the number of accidents on our roads. Road markings serve a very important function in conveying information and requirements about the road and there is no doubt that poorly maintained road markings can have a negative impact on road safety. With this in mind, my officials undertake regular routine maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, including defective signs and road markings, are noted. The frequency of these inspections depends on the type of road and the volume of vehicular and pedestrian traffic. Town centres and major traffic routes are generally inspected monthly, while all other roads and footways are inspected at either three or six-monthly intervals.Road markings are not replaced within a routine/specific time frame, rather when approximately 30-40% of the marked area is ineffective or worn away. Where the condition of road markings falls below the required standards, they are renewed as soon as possible within the available funding limits. Repairs are prioritised to concentrate on regulatory road markings and those longitudinal lines necessary for continued road safety. Additional road markings are considered for renewal subject to the availability of funding. The guidance for the maintenance of road markings within my Department is included in the Roads Service Policy & Procedure Guide : RSPPG_E019 - Road Maintenance Standards for Safety.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any (i) reporting mechanisms; (ii) key performance indicators; and (iii) audit or assurance arrangements, in place to (a) monitor delivery of the measures set out in the Executive’s third response to the UK Covid-19 Inquiry Module 1 Report recommendations; and (b) to ensure value for money.
answer_text: We can advise that governance, monitoring and assurance arrangements are in place to support delivery of the measures outlined in the Executive’s third response to the UK Covid19 Inquiry Module 1 Report recommendations, and to ensure value for money.Delivery of the Module 1 recommendations is overseen through a cross-departmental governance structure, the NICS Covid-19 Inquiry Modular Recommendations and Lessons Learned Steering Group, which coordinates departmental input and tracks implementation progress. This structure has been used throughout the preparation of the Executive’s Module 1 response and supports ongoing monitoring across all departments. In addition, progress updates are provided in line with the streamlined UKwide approach agreed with the devolved administrations. Under this approach, twiceyearly updates will be published in May and November, with initial updates still required six months after publication of each new Inquiry module report.Our third response sets out actionfocused commitments aligned to each of the Inquiry’s Module 1 recommendations. These include agreed deliverables on wholesystem emergency preparedness, risk assessment, data and research, UKwide exercises, transparency measures, and strengthened civil contingency structures. The delivery of these actions recorded in the Recommendations Implementation Plan functions as the key performance framework against which progress is assessed

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to the Executive’s third response to the UK Covid-19 Inquiry Module 1 Report recommendations, to detail (i) any additional capital investment required (a) over the remainder of; and (b) beyond, the current budget period; and (ii) any additional staffing resources (a) that have been allocated; or (b) are anticipated to be required, including the grades and functions of any new posts.
answer_text: In relation to the Executive’s third response to the UK Covid-19 Inquiry Module 1 Report recommendations, we can confirm the following:Capital InvestmentNo additional Capital investment is anticipated either for the remainder of the current budget period or beyond it in connection with the implementation of the Module 1 recommendations.Staffing ResourcesWithin the Department, two additional Deputy Principal posts have been allocated to the Civil Contingencies Division to specifically support the implementation of recommendations arising from the Covid Inquiry modules. All other work led by TEO CCD is being delivered by core staff, reflecting resourcing arrangements developed post COVID. The Department is not aware of any additional staffing allocations that may have been made by other Departments for this work

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to the Executive’s third response to the UK Covid-19 Inquiry Module 1 Report recommendations, to detail (i) any expenditure incurred to date; and (ii) any specific actions, programmes, or projects delivered under the headings of (a) strengthening frameworks; (b) enhancing data infrastructure; and (c) improving training programmes and response capabilities.
answer_text: The Executive remains fully committed to implementing the recommendations arising from the UK Covid-19 Inquiry Module 1 Report and continues to strengthen preparedness, resilience and data capability across departments. The Executive’s third progress update, which was published on 16 January 2026, sets out the latest position.Expenditure incurred to dateImplementation activity to date has been delivered within existing departmental baselines. No specific additional programme-level funding has yet been allocated solely for the delivery of Module 1 actions, although it is anticipated that financial costs may arise as further improvements to systems, analytical capacity and exercising programmes progress. Actions, programmes and projects deliveredSince publication of the Executive’s first (January 2025) and second (July 2025) updates, departments have continued to progress a wide programme of work. Key actions under each heading include:Strengthening frameworksDevelopment and refinement of Northern Ireland’s Civil Contingencies Framework and a Northern Ireland-specific Civil Contingencies Risk Register, reflecting lessons from the pandemic and strengthened cross-government governance arrangements. Continued engagement with UK Government and devolved administrations on strategic risk assessment, red-team methodologies and strengthened assurance processes to improve whole-system preparedness. Enhancing data infrastructureThere is ongoing collaboration between NISRA and the Office for National Statistics on UK-wide data sharing, development of linked datasets, and refinement of methodologies.Improving training programmes and response capabilitiesContinued use of Resilience Direct to support multi-agency planning, exercising and post-exercise learning. Engagement with UKG and devolved governments to prepare a UK-wide pandemic response exercise, consistent with Recommendation 6. Strengthening of red-team practice and assurance mechanisms through collaboration with the Cabinet Office’s Crisis Management Excellence Programme. Our officials will continue to work collectively across departments and with UK and devolved counterparts to ensure full delivery of the recommendations, with progress reported through the established update cycle.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any guidance issued by her Department on the safe and effective use of salt bins, provided by her Department.
answer_text: My Department targets the limited resources available on the busier main through routes, as per the current winter service policy. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, and provides action where it is most needed and most-effective.In addition to gritting this 28% Primary Salting Network, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. There is no obligation to use the salt bins to treat roads or footpaths, and their usage should only be undertaken if the member of the public feels safe to do so.There is no law preventing individuals from clearing snow and ice from public footways outside their homes or businesses, again this is at the discretion of the individual.Further guidance and advice is available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/clearing-ice-and-snow-footwaysIt must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department assesses if snow and ice was cleared carefully from footways, given NI Direct guidance states that those who clear snow and ice carefully are unlikely to be liable.
answer_text: My Department targets the limited resources available on the busier main through routes, as per the current winter service policy. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, and provides action where it is most needed and most-effective.In addition to gritting this 28% Primary Salting Network, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. There is no obligation to use the salt bins to treat roads or footpaths and their usage should only be undertaken if the member of the public feels safe to do so.There is no law preventing individuals from clearing snow and ice from public footways outside their homes or businesses again this is at the discretion of the individual.Further guidance and advice is available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/clearing-ice-and-snow-footwaysIt must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to the announcement of the Shared Ireland Media Initiative, to outline any engagement their Department has had with Ofcom in relation to protecting the independence of Northern Ireland-based media.
answer_text: We understand the Shared Island Media Programme aims to support new talent and capacity building in relation to cross-border journalism and media content. We have not been engaged in the programme or with Ofcom on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether members of the public assume any legal liability when using salt bins, provided by her Department, to treat roads or footpaths.
answer_text: My Department targets the limited resources available on the busier main through routes, as per the current winter service policy. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, and provides action where it is most needed and most-effective.In addition to gritting this 28% Primary Salting Network, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. There is no obligation to use the salt bins to treat roads or footpaths and their usage should only be undertaken if the member of the public feels safe to do so.There is no law preventing individuals from clearing snow and ice from public footways outside their homes or businesses again this is at the discretion of the individual.Further guidance and advice is available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/clearing-ice-and-snow-footwaysIt must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any criteria used by her Department, to identify which B and C roads will be gritted.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements are finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are complete and our salt barns are filled to capacity.My Department targets the limited resources available on the busier main through routes, as per the current winter service policy. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, and provides action where it is most needed and most-effective.In addition to gritting this 28% Primary Salting Network, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Where possible, once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is secured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. The full policy can be viewed on the Department’s website below:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022	It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey. During this recent cold spell, staff across all our roads divisions have been working tirelessly day and night in some very difficult conditions, to keep traffic moving safely. We owe a huge debt of gratitude to our gritter drivers and snow blowers, who are often the first traffic on many roads, leaving their own families behind to ensure we can travel safely. Their hard work and persistence ensured tangible results for our road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has received any (i) claims; or (ii) complaints, over the last five years, relating to accidents on roads, treated by members of the public using salt bins.
answer_text: My Department maintains records of salt bin locations and details of when bins are replenished with salt. However, we do not hold data on when members of the public have used salt from departmental bins to treat roads; where public liability claims have been received; or where public complaints or enquiries have been made in relation to accidents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the types of legislation or departmental guidance issued by his Department, since he has taken office; and (ii) whether the legislation or departmental guidance issued is (a) a replication of GB guidance; (b) Northern Ireland bespoke guidance; or (c) a replication of Republic of Ireland guidance.
answer_text: Since February 2024, my Department has introduced a range of primary and secondary legislation and associated guidance.Primary legislation introduced includes the Agriculture Act (Northern Ireland) 2025 which received Royal Assent on 16 September 2025, and the Dilapidation Bill introduced on 23 June 2025 and currently undergoing Committee scrutiny. This legislation is specific to Northern Ireland.DAERA has taken forward a small number of Legislative Consent Memorandums during this period, securing the support of the NI Assembly for Westminster to legislate in particular areas.My Department has introduced a significant volume of secondary legislation through Statutory Rules since 2024. Many of those Statutory Rules introduced relate to devolved matters and are therefore specific to Northern Ireland. A small number relate to implementing UK-wide regulations and therefore align with UK Government Statutory Instruments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the (a) type of legislation; and (b) departmental guidance, issued by her Department since she has taken office; and (ii) whether this legislation or departmental guidance is (a) a replication of GB guidance; (b) Northern Ireland bespoke guidance; or (c) a replication of Republic of Ireland guidance.
answer_text: Since taking up office in February 2024, my Department has introduced a range of primary and secondary legislation and associated guidance. Primary legislation introduced by my Department is specific to Northern Ireland.  My Department has also introduced a significant volume of secondary legislation through Statutory Rules since 2024. Many of those Statutory Rules relate to devolved matters and are therefore specific to Northern Ireland. A small number relate to implementing UK-wide regulations and therefore align with UK Government Statutory Instruments.A small number of Legislative Consent Memoranda have also been advanced during this period, securing the support of the Northern Ireland Assembly for the UK Government to legislate in particular areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to support franchised car dealers, to mitigate the loss of access to GB unsold stock pipelines.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my response to AQW 37632/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with (i) the National Franchised Dealers Association; and (ii) individual manufacturers, since January 2026, regarding the divergence between EU and GB standards.
answer_text: I am aware of the issue raised by the National Franchised Dealers Association and others in relation to Vehicle Type Approval, which is a reserved matter under the control of the Department for Transport. It is important that issues such as these, which arise because of Brexit, are resolved in a way that is fair, transparent and do not undermine competitiveness or consumer choice in the North. I note the Statement by the Minister for Roads and Buses on 18 December 2025, confirming that the British Government plans to legislate to mandate that vehicles placed on the market in Britain must hold dual British and EU type approval and marking, enabling their sale in both Britain and the north, in the absence of evidence that manufacturers are doing so voluntarily.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of the adoption of EU6e and the EU7 standards in Northern Ireland on the availability of new vehicle models for sale by franchised dealers, compared to Great Britain under GB Type Approval.
answer_text: Type approval is a reserved matter, and all policy and legislation is the responsibility of the British Government’s Department for Transport. In December the British Government made a statement to the Commons about its intention to legislate to mandate dual marking (GB Type approval and UK-NI/EU approval) if there is not a clear commitment from industry to make this the default approach for vehicles sold in the UK. My officials have had multiple discussions with the NFDA on this issue and will continue to push the British Government to make changes so the industry in the North is not disadvantaged.A call for evidence was published in December and will be open until 12 February 2026. I would encourage you to bring this to the attention of any constituents who may be involved in car sales in here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has made any assessment of the Department for the Economy Annual Report and Accounts, which have received a disclaimed opinion for the second consecutive year.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the role that the South West Acute Hospital has in improving hospital waiting times.
answer_text: The South West Acute Hospital continues to play an important role in stabilising and improving waiting times across Northern Ireland, particularly within the Western Trust area. My Department remains committed to ensuring that all available capacity on the site is fully utilised for the benefit of patients.Work is underway to explore the appointment of an additional gynaecology consultant, with associated operating sessions planned for delivery at SWAH. Once staff are appointed, this will allow two additional inpatient sessions and one daycase session per week to be delivered. My Department is also working to commission the remaining vacant endoscopy capacity in SWAH. This will enable an additional 6.5 endoscopy sessions per week to be delivered. Four of these sessions are already operational on the site.Additionally, the regional General Surgery Network continues to engage with the Trust to assess priorities that may include commissioning further theatre activity at SWAH.While core capacity is being developed, my Department will ensure that any available theatre capacity is utilised, including ongoing partnership arrangements with the independent sector. This will enable patients on orthopaedic waiting lists to have primary joint replacement surgery at SWAH. It is expected that approximately 550 primary joint replacements will be undertaken in the newly commissioned theatre space in the SWAH. These procedures will be predominantly primary knees, with 380 procedures expected to be delivered. A further 170 primary hips are also planned to be scheduled before the end of the financial year. From April – December 2025, 247 knees and 129 hips have been undertaken on the SWAH site.The activity already commissioned demonstrates the hospital’s ongoing value as a key regional asset in tacking long waits and expanding elective capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when single use plastic ban legislation will be implemented to bring it into line with England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: I am committed to taking action to restrict the consumption of a range of single-use plastic products where plastic-free alternatives are readily available and affordable, this includes transposing the Single-Use Plastic Directive. England, Scotland and Wales have introduced regulations banning the most problematic single-use plastic products. My officials are currently working to progress the legislation which will introduce similar restrictions on commonly littered single-use plastic items such as straws, cutlery and expanded polystyrene cups and containers within N. Ireland, in line with restrictions across England, Scotland, Wales and Ireland. Single-use plastics are a wasteful use of resources, generally discarded after being used once, negatively impacting upon our natural and marine environments. My Department has already introduced legislative actions aimed at tackling plastic pollution including regulations to ban the supply and sale of single-use vapes and wet wipes containing plastic in alignment with England, Scotland and Wales.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her Department's target of participation by large developers, in the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme.
answer_text: My Department has not set a target of participation by large developers. However, all renewable developers using eligible technologies with a project capacity of 5MW or greater will be able to participate in the scheme.I am confident that we are building the right foundations to incentivise sufficient electricity generation whilst ensuring the scheme delivers the best outcomes for energy consumers and that local communities benefit from the transition to net zero.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any controls in place to regulate imported packaging goods to the small business sector when recovering levies due under extended producer responsibility.
answer_text: The controls in place to regulate imported packaging goods to the small business sector are detailed in the Producer Responsibility Obligations (Packaging and Packaging Waste) Regulations 2024. The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in Northern Ireland (DAERA) is responsible for the enforcement of these regulations in Northern Ireland. PackUK is the scheme administrator for Extended Producer Responsibility for packaging and is responsible for setting and collecting fees paid by obligated packaging producers across the UK.Further guidance for the business sector on Extended Producer responsibility for packaging can also be found on the National Packaging Waste Database website, https://npwd.environment-agency.gov.uk/Public/Guidance.aspx?CategoryId=41fc1dbb-47a2-47fd-bb82-4938d83729e0&ReturnUrl=%2FPublic%2FNewsAndGuidance.aspx%3FReturnUrlId%3D64c793b7-1cf6-ed11-9f3b-98f2b327e115.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department plans to take if the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme does not meet its target.
answer_text: Delivering additional renewable electricity generation is complex, demanding a careful balance between security of supply, affordability, and legislative compliance.My Department’s priority is to ensure that Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee auctions take place at the right time, securing the best technologies and capacity to meet our targets at a fair price for consumers. Auction targets will be published prior to each launch and my Department will analyse the results of each auction before determining next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has in place to meet its target, of large developers participating in the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme.
answer_text: My Department has not set participation targets for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme for large developers. All renewable developers using eligible technologies with a project capacity of 5MW or greater will be able to participate in the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of the UK Government’s independent review into mental health, autism and ADHD services and its implications for Northern Ireland, particularly in relation to addressing rising demand and reducing waiting times.
answer_text: I am aware that the UK Government has recently announced an independent review into mental health, autism and ADHD services, in order to better understand prevalence, trends and inequalities to inform new models of support and pathways in England. It is widely recognised that mental health services in Northern Ireland have historically been underfunded in comparison with other UK jurisdictions. Our mental health services require additional funding to meet current and future needs. It is also recognised that waiting lists remain a challenge across the region, particularly given the rising demand for services.In response to these pressures the Department has taken a number of actions. For example, the Department commissioned an ADHD needs assessment in February 2025 to identify levels of demand to inform decisions on a future commissioned ADHD service, subject to budget availability. The report has now been received, and I and my officials are considering the findings, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter. The Department is also cognisant of growing waiting lists and lack of support for autistic adults. In February 2025, the Department appointed a project manager for adult autism services to better understand the population, pathways, presenting need and to further develop supports and services across the region.In the absence of any additional funding, we will strengthen our efforts to maximise the impact of every existing resource, ensuring they are utilised to their fullest potential for the benefit of our population.Mental health remains a personal priority for me. I have been clear that delivering the Strategy requires sustained and additional investment and I will continue to make the case for mental health until the ambition so clearly set out in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 is fully realised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with Evri and the GMB Union, since the Union’s statement warning that a new super-site was causing Christmas chaos and posing safety and staffing concerns.
answer_text: Since the opening of the Evri depot site, HSENI has visited the premises on three separate occasions - 30th September 2025, 26th November 2025 and 4th December 2025.  HSENI is continuing to work with Evri to ensure that legal compliance is achieved and sustained within the premises.  HSENI has also been liaising with other regulatory partners in relation to this site.HSENI has been engaging and working with GMB Union representatives in relation to Evri distribution partners within the supply chain on a regular basis. In addition, HSENI has also kept GMB Union representatives appraised of HSENI’s involvement in respect of the matter that you raise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) plans his Department has to address regional inequalities in access to mental health, autism and ADHD services; and (ii) measures being considered to ensure equal provision for rural communities.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts provide services that are designed to take account of local population needs, and in line with section 75 equality requirements, including rural impact, that make most effective use of the resources available to them. Additionally, Action 31 of the Mental Health Strategy commits to, “Develop a regional mental health service, operating across the five HSC Trusts, with regional professional leadership that is responsible for consistency in service delivery and development”. The intention of this action is to ensure ‘people have access to high quality, regionally consistent but locally based services within local communities’. (ii)CAMHs The Mental Health Strategy sets out key actions for children and young people, with regards access and quality of care, and including support to the prevention and early intervention pathway. doh-mhs-strategy-2021-2031.pdf.  Working Together: A Pathway for Children and Young People through CAMHS (2018) sets out the standards that are applied regardless of whatever level of service is required in response to children and young people presenting with emotional and mental health difficulties.  It is anticipated that any additional funding received by CAMHS as part of the Mental Health Strategy funding will assist with the development of new initiatives to help ease pressures across all CAMHS services in both rural and urban areas.		The Emotional Wellbeing teams in Schools (EWTS) programme was launched in 2023 and funded under the Mental Health Strategy.  The main focus of this initiative was to promote emotional wellbeing and strengthen self-esteem and resilience in all our children & young people across NI, both in rural and urban areas. EWTS is a health-led, trauma and Social Emotional Learning (SEL) informed programme of support for all post primary schools.Adult MH Within Adult Mental Health Services, a stepped care approach is taken to ensure that people referred for support in respect of their emotional and mental health are offered the correct level of support, matched to presenting need. The You in Mind, Regional Mental Health Care Pathway, explains how a person who may require mental health support, can access mental health care, and the steps involved. This pathway includes specialist mental health services. There are a range of specialist mental health services accessible to service users, including; the Forensic Mental Health Service, Psychological Therapy Services, Perinatal Mental Health Service and Eating Disorder Services. Looking ahead, the Mental Health Strategy commits to continued development of our understanding of specialisms within a general mental health service across both adult and children’s services.   Neurodevelopmental Pathways including Autism My Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework proposes an inclusive and needs-based approach to neurodiversity which will not

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people in Fermanagh and South Tyrone currently on waiting lists for (i) autism; and (ii) ADHD assessments; and (ii) any steps being taken to reduce these waiting times.
answer_text: (i) Data on the number of people currently on waiting lists for autism assessment is collected at Trust and regional level only. Information on waiting lists is not available by Constituency area. Fermanagh and South Tyrone is situated within the geographical areas covered by Southern Health and Social Care Trust and Western Health and Social Care Trust. As of October 2025, the number of people waiting for an autism assessment within these Trusts for children and adults was as follows:  Number of children and adults waiting for Autism Diagnostic AssessmentHSC TrustPaediatric waiting listAdult waiting listSouthern1,681490Western2,517557*Total4,1981,047*Please note that this figure provided by Western Health and Social Care Trust includes 31 adults awaiting an autism assessment within Adult Learning Disability services. The information provided is sourced from Trust manual returns and not official statistics.(ii)  Data on the number of people currently on waiting lists for ADHD assessment is collated at a Trust and regional level only. Information on waiting lists is not available broken down by Constituency area. At Trust level, we can only provide the number of children waiting for an ADHD diagnostic assessment within CAMHS in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust which is 751 people. This is because there is currently no commissioned ADHD service within either Trust. ADHD provision has developed differently across Trusts in response to population needs and the expertise available within HSC teams. While some services are in place across the Health and Social Care system, there are significant inconsistencies and long waiting lists across Trusts.  The information provided is sourced from Trust manual returns and not official statistics.(ii) Waiting lists for assessment of autism and ADHD are lengthy and growing for children, young people and adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors including funding that is short of objectively assessed need. My Department is undertaking a number of actions in an effort to improve waiting times within a financially constrained environment.In May 2025, My Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework. The Framework was issued for public consultation and this exercise concluded on 26th September 2025. This Framework proposes an approach to neurodiversity which is inclusive and aligned to the needs of children and their families. The importance of diagnostic processes should not be underestimated, however the requirement to obtain a formal diagnosis as a precursor to the offer of support and assistance consigns many children to lengthy periods of time spent in anticipation of support and intervention which would have been more appropriately offered at an earlier stage. The approach proposed will place more emphasis upon earlier intervention irrespective of the outcome of the formal diagnostic process.My Department is currentl

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to ensure that mental health, autism and ADHD services are adequately resourced to meet increasing demand.
answer_text: The Department of Health is aware of rising demand for mental health, autism and ADHD services and the need to ensure that adequate services are in place to meet that demand. The Mental Health Strategy 2021 – 31 and its accompanying Funding Plan, published in June 2021, were intended to serve as a 10-year roadmap to promote good mental health and improve mental health services. The Funding Plan recognised that implementation of the Strategy’s 35 actions would require a significant investment, estimated at £1.2 billion over ten years (based on 2021 costs).  However, to date, no additional funding has been provided to my Department to support implementation of the Strategy and so existing Departmental resources have been used to progress many of the actions.  A total of £12.3 million, has been utilised across 20 specific Strategy actions from 2021 to the end of 2024/25. This represents only 16% of the funding identified (£76.93m) as required for the Mental Health Strategy by the end of 2024/25. The absence of dedicated additional funding has necessitated a partial and phased implementation and, undoubtably, progress has been constrained in areas due to the challenging financial position.  Mental health remains a personal priority for me and I will continue to make the case for sustained and additional investment for mental health until the ambition set out in 2021 is fully realised.The Autism Strategy 2023-2028 was published, along with an initial two-year 2023-2025 Delivery Plan, in December 2023. The Strategy outlines commitments and actions across government, reflecting that each Department has a role to improve outcomes for those with autism. Due to unprecedented budgetary challenges, an initial programme budget of £300k per annum was allocated by the Department of Health to complement a series of ‘low cost/no cost’ actions within a two-year delivery plan.  Significant progress and improved outcomes have already been made within the first two years of delivery, as reflected in the year two progress report of the 2023-2025 delivery plan on the DoH website.  Work is at an advanced stage on the next delivery plan which will cover the remaining three year period of the Strategy. However, given ongoing budgetary challenges it is anticipated that the current £300K annual budget will continue to complement “low cost/no cost” actions within the delivery plan.In relation to ADHD, the Department commissioned an ADHD needs assessment in February 2025, which aimed to identify levels of demand in order to inform future ADHD commissioned services, subject to future budget availability. The report has now been received, and I and my officials are considering the findings, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any incentives or measures her Department intends to introduce, to address the competitive disadvantage caused by VAT differentials between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland, particularly in support of the Fermanagh Hidden Heartland's tourism campaign and the wider hospitality sector.
answer_text: I was pleased to visit Fermanagh recently and announce the extension of the Hidden Heartlands branding to Fermanagh. I am aware of the cost of doing business challenges facing our tourism and hospitality sectors, including the higher VAT rates faced by hospitality businesses here relative to the South. I am currently engaging with the hospitality industry regarding VAT and their proposals for a targeted VAT reduction for hospitality.The hospitality and tourism sectors have, understandably, expressed concerns surrounding the changes to Employer’s National Insurance and Minimum Wage implemented in early April. These changes, coming on the back of a challenging operating environment for many years, add further to the rising cost of doing business. Ultimately, VAT is an excepted matter and decisions to change the rate of VAT rest with the British Government who have not shown any willingness to address this matter so far. The Minister of Finance continues to urge the Chief Secretary to the Treasury to reduce the cost of doing business, particularly in the North. I will continue to support the Minister of Finance and advocate alongside him at every opportunity. My Department and partner organisations already provide a broad range of support to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets. Invest NI for example has a portfolio of grants, loans and investment funds, many of which are specifically targeted at early stage and small businesses.  This includes grants to help businesses in the North reduce energy costs and build resilience through green efficiency. The grant is part of a suite of support that helps businesses make efficiencies in their use of energy and resources. In addition, the Department’s Regional Economic Plan is focussed on identifying key economic opportunities and priority actions for each part of the North, supported by a £45m Regional Development Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current capacity for new water and wastewater connections, in the Dungannon area; and (ii) the measures being taken to address any constraints.
answer_text: The management of the public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:(i)	In respect of new water connections, there are currently no constraints within the Dungannon area.  With regard to new wastewater connections, there are currently significant constraints within the wastewater network in the Dungannon area, which are impacting proposed new development, due to the presence of storm overflows which are classified as Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharges (UIDs). These UIDs are also classified as 'high polluting assets' which means that these areas are closed to any further connections due to the likelihood of increasing environmental pollution.  (ii) 	NI Water is investing approximately £45m to upgrade Dungannon wastewater treatment works within the PC21 Price Control period. This upgrade will increase the capacity at the treatment works. However, there are no UID upgrades planned within the Dungannon catchment in the current Price Control 21 period (2021/22 - 2027/28).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the current occupancy rates for enterprise centres in Fermanagh and South Tyrone; and (ii) a breakdown of the types of businesses or services operating within these centres.
answer_text: Local Enterprise Agencies (LEAs) are private organisations who are functionally independent of my Department.  Accordingly, my Department does not hold this information.You may wish to request this information directly using the email addresses below. Fermanagh Enterprise at info@fermanaghenterprise.com or Omagh Enterprise at info@omaghenterprise.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the upgrade works at the Dungannon Wastewater Treatment Works, located at Moygashel Lane, will be completed.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The current anticipated completion date for the upgrade works at Dungannon wastewater treatment works, located at Moygashel Lane, is 31 March 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether her Department has carried out any assessment of the impact of late parcel deliveries on rural communities; and (ii) to detail any measures being considered to improve service reliability.
answer_text: Ofcom regulates the postal industry in the north of Ireland. My Department has no remit for postal services. However, in January 2025 the Consumer Council commissioned research titled ‘Consumers experiences of final mile delivery issues in Northern Ireland’. The Consumer Council is seeking a meeting with Evri to address consumer concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure that delivery companies operating in Northern Ireland, meet acceptable standards for timely delivery.
answer_text: Parcels and post are reserved to the British Government at Westminster and are not devolved to the Northern Ireland Executive. Ofcom regulates the postal industry in the north of Ireland.		Delivery companies are required to operate within the framework of UK and EU regulations.		My department has no statutory obligations in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will engage with Evri, to address consumer complaints about late deliveries during peak periods, such as Christmas.
answer_text: Evri is an unregulated postal operator. Ofcom rules mean that parcel delivery companies must have a complaints procedure that consumers can follow.The Consumer Council is engaging with Evri on this matter to arrange a meeting to discuss ongoing consumer concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions her Department has had with parcel delivery companies, including Evri, regarding delays in deliveries to consumers in Northern Ireland, over the last two years.
answer_text: My Department has no remit for parcel delivery companies. However, the Consumer Council is seeking a meeting with EVRI to address ongoing consumer concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any coordination that exists between his Department and the Public Health Agency in relation to the removal of wild bird carcasses from public spaces.
answer_text: The responsibility for removal of dead birds, which are not collected by the Department for surveillance purposes, rests with the landowner. 		DAERA officials are in close contact with the Public Health Agency and ensure that the PHA lines are issued through communications to landowners or the wider public when handling dead wild birds. 		This includes information on our website which can be found at:  	https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/wild-birds-and-advice-public-november-2025
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any responsibilities of (i) local councils; and (ii) public sector landowners, for the safe disposal of dead wild birds found on public land.
answer_text: My Department collects dead wild birds for avian influenza surveillance purposes to help us understand if, and when, the virus is present in Northern Ireland and how it is distributed geographically. Where dead wild birds are not required for surveillance the collection and disposal of dead wild birds rests with the landowner. DAERA officials met with local councils and wider public sector bodies on this issue 19 November 2025. Information in respect of wild bird surveillance, responsibility for disposal of dead wild birds not required for surveillance and Public Health Agency Advice has been shared for use within these organisations as appropriate. This includes information on our website which can be found at:  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/wild-birds-and-advice-public-november-2025		The public is encouraged to report any findings of dead wild birds using the DAERA Dead Wild Bird Online Reporting Tool which can be accessed at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/services/daera-dead-wild-bird-online-reporting-tool
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any steps taken once highly pathogenic avian influenza is confirmed in a specific area; and (ii) how this affects further wild bird testing.
answer_text: My Department collects dead wild birds for avian influenza surveillance purposes to help us understand if, and when, the virus is present in Northern Ireland and how it is distributed geographically. 		Once this is confirmed in a specific area, it is unlikely that any further testing of wild birds will be required and therefore further reports from that area may not be collected by DAERA.  In these instances, the collection and disposal of dead wild birds rests with the landowner. 		The public is still encouraged to report any findings of dead wild birds, as this information adds to the epidemiological picture, using the DAERA Dead Wild Bird Online Reporting Tool which can be accessed at: 	https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/services/daera-dead-wild-bird-online-reporting-tool		Further information is available on the DAERA website at: 	https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/wild-birds-and-advice-public-november-2025
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the process for the (a) reporting of; and (b) disposing of, dead wild birds suspected of having had avian influenza; and (ii) any guidance provided to the public and landowners in such cases.
answer_text: My Department collects dead wild birds for avian influenza surveillance purposes to help us understand if, and when, the virus is present in Northern Ireland and how it is distributed geographically. Where dead wild birds are not required for surveillance the collection and disposal of dead wild birds rests with the landowner. DAERA officials met with local councils and wider public sector bodies on this issue 19 November 2025. Information in respect of wild bird surveillance, responsibility for disposal of dead wild birds not required for surveillance and Public Health Agency Advice has been shared for use within these organisations as appropriate. This includes information on our website which can be found at:  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/wild-birds-and-advice-public-november-2025The public is encouraged to report any findings of dead wild birds using the DAERA Dead Wild Bird Online Reporting Tool which can be accessed at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/services/daera-dead-wild-bird-online-reporting-tool
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any criteria used by his Department to determine which dead wild birds are collected for avian influenza surveillance purposes.
answer_text: My Department collects dead wild birds for avian influenza surveillance purposes to help us understand if, and when, the virus is present in Northern Ireland and how it is distributed geographically.		Once this is confirmed in a specific area, it is unlikely that any further testing of wild birds will be required and therefore further reports from that area may not be collected by DAERA.  In these instances, the collection and disposal of dead wild birds rests with the landowner. 		Details of the latest avian influenza findings in wild birds in Northern Ireland can be found on the DAERA website at the following link:	https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/services/avian-influenza-map-viewer
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance provided to GP practices on prioritising requests for medical certificates following hospital discharge.
answer_text: Where a patient has recently been discharged from hospital, the GP should not need to issue with a Med 3 certificate, also known as a fit note.  The Med 3 certificate should have been issued by the hospital team responsible for the patient’s care.My Department has issued guidance on completion of fit notes to GPs in 2024. doh-fit-note-guidance-july-2024.pdf & doh-fit-letter.pdf.  In addition my Department issued guidance to Trust Chief Executives in 2025 Letter - Chief Professional Officer DoH to Chief Executives of HSS Trusts - legally certify fit notes.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that patients recovering from surgery can obtain the necessary medical certificates for work without delay.
answer_text: In January 2024 my Department issued updated guidance to the HSC confirming that Fit Notes could properly be issued by doctors, nurses, pharmacists, occupational therapists and physiotherapists to patients being discharged from hospital. The discharging clinician should complete the Med3 based on their assessment of the patient and information provided to them by the other professionals involved in the patient’s care.  This approach is intended to ensure that patients receive the necessary documentation without delay and are not signposted to other healthcare professionals purely for the purposes of certifying a fit note.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has assessed the adequacy of current GP practice systems for issuing first-time medical certificates, especially where hospital discharge letters are available.
answer_text: Where a patient has recently been discharged from hospital, the GP should not need to issue with a Med 3 certificate, also known as a fit note.  The Med 3 certificate should have been issued by the hospital team responsible for the patient’s care.My Department has issued guidance on completion of fit notes. Fitness to work must always be considered as part of the discharge planning process, including after a day procedure or Emergency Department treatment. A Med 3 provided to the patient must cover the whole of the anticipated period of absence from work.  doh-fit-note-guidance-july-2024.pdfWhen a GP is the clinician in charge of the care for a patient who will not be fit for work for longer than seven days then they should issue a Med 3 certificate.  As independent contractors each GP practice is responsible for ensuring their practice systems are adequate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will review the current telephone triage and call-back system in GP practices to ensure that non-urgent but essential requests, such as medical certificates, are processed in a timely manner.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients. Decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice. In recent years my Department has made significant investment to enable GPs to improve their telephony systems and employ additional staff to manage their systems efficiently.My Department has issued guidance on completion of fit notes to GPs in 2024. doh-fit-note-guidance-july-2024.pdf & doh-fit-letter.pdf.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration given to allowing nurse practitioners or other qualified healthcare professionals to issue initial medical certificates where a GP is unavailable, in order to reduce delays for patients.
answer_text: From 1 July 2022, nurses, occupational therapists, pharmacists, and physiotherapists have been able to legally certify Med3 Fit Notes. This applies to nurses, physiotherapists, and occupational therapists working in primary and secondary care and pharmacists employed in general practice and acute settings only. On 24 January 2024 the Chief Nursing Officer, Chief Allied Health Professions Officer, and Chief Pharmaceutical Officer issued guidance to the HSC on issuing Med3 Fit Notes by doctors, nurses, pharmacists, occupational therapists, and physiotherapists.Following this, on 22 July 2025, the Chief Professional Officers wrote to the HSC to advise that as of 24 April 2025, the electronic Med3 form had gone live on the Encompass system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of red squirrels at Castle Archdale Country Park; and (ii) any actions his Department is taking to protect the species following damage caused to their habitat due to Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Formal surveys of the red squirrel population in Castle Archdale Country Park are not undertaken by NIEA staff, so it is not possible to detail the precise numbers of the species present within the site. However, there is evidence of a healthy population within the local area; red squirrels are observed on a regular and frequent basis by NIEA staff working at the Country Park and have been frequently captured on a wildlife trail camera deployed in sections of woodland in 2025.Site management continues to undertake a range of management measures which can be considered beneficial to red squirrels, this includes the installation of specific advisory signage for red squirrels, as well as overall woodland and habitat management for biodiversity promotion, which has included mitigation works following adverse weather events such as Storm Éowyn.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of trees are aged over 400 years; and (ii) their locations.
answer_text: DAERA holds records which details the year, and species of trees planted by Forest Service.  However, data associated with veteran and ancient trees that precede Forest Service records, including those over 400 years is not available.  Forest Service manage approximately 700 hectares, classified as ancient woodland, across its estate, recognising the high conservation value of these sites whilst protecting and enhancing their biodiversity.Additionally, The Woodland Trust provide an interactive public platform on which ancient tree inventory details including their locations, are recorded and retained.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any tree planting scheme in place to replace the extensive damage to forestry following Storm Éowyn at Castle Archdale Country Park.
answer_text: My Department offers financial assistance to landowners for replanting through the Woodland Investment Grant (WIG), which supports woodland restoration and resilience. As Forest Service own and manage Castle Archdale Forest, and the Country Park is managed by the NIEA, the scheme does not apply to the Government agencies. Forest Service is reviewing regeneration plans for storm-damaged areas at Castle Archdale Forest in line with the UK Forestry Standard to ensure their continued sustainable management. As part of this review, Forest Service has engaged in a recently formed consultative group to ensure that environmental and heritage interests are considered.  The Department for Communities Heritage and Environment Division, NIEA, Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, and other stakeholders with an interest in the future of Castle Archdale are represented in the group.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of pine martens at Castle Archdale Country Park; and (ii) any actions his Department is taking to protect the species following extensive damage to their habitat due to Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Formal surveys of the pine marten population in Castle Archdale Country Park are not undertaken by NIEA staff, so it is not possible to detail the precise numbers of the species present within the site. However, pine martens continue to be observed by NIEA staff working at the Country Park and have been captured on a wildlife trail camera deployed in sections of woodland in 2025.Site management continues to undertake a range of management measures which can be considered beneficial to pine martens; this includes overall woodland and habitat management for biodiversity promotion, which has included mitigation works following adverse weather events such as Storm Éowyn.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures in place to protect the ancient oak tree at Castle Archdale Country Park.
answer_text: I understand that the ancient oak tree you refer to at Castle Archdale Forest was shortlisted as tree of the year in the Woodland Trust 2024 nominations. The tree is located within an area of the forest managed under a Low Impact Silvicultural System (LISS).  Tree management, felling and regeneration under this system, affords protection of important cultural and biodiversity attributes of the woodland area including the presence of veteran and ancient trees. Regarding the ancient oak tree at Castle Archdale, Forest Service has identified four conifer trees in the immediate vicinity of the oak for removal.  This aim is to protect the oak from risk of damage associated with the encroaching branches of these trees whilst ensuring that their removal is sufficiently gradual so as not to create unnecessary shock effects for the protected oak tree, in line with best practice.I am advised that all work will be carried out using specialist tree surgery operations, to minimise the risk of canopy damage and ground compaction affecting the root system.  Forest Service remains committed to proactive management of these important and sensitive sites to safeguard trees rich in culture and heritage. It has engaged in a recently formed consultative group to ensure that environmental and heritage interests are fully considered.  The Department for Communities Heritage and Environment Division, NIEA, Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, and other stakeholders with an interest in the future of Castle Archdale are represented in the group.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there will be tangible targets or performance indicators, attached to the £16.5 million financial package from the UK Treasury, to help businesses deal with the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: I am answering questions AQW 36189/22-27, AQW 36190/22-27, AQW 36191/22-27, AQW 36192/22-27 and AQW 36193/22-27 together.I note the Chancellor of the Exchequer’s announcement of a £16.6m package over three years to support businesses navigate the Windsor Framework. The need for the British Government to provide more and better support and guidance for businesses has been widely recognised, and the package is welcome in that context. The specifics of the funding have not been confirmed at this stage, beyond the Chancellor’s announcement as part of Budget 2025.
