question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the cut off period for the receipt of new applications to the Go Succeed programme, which closes at the end of March 2026.
answer_text: My Department does not have a role in the administration of Go Succeed. The relevant local council can provide information on applying for the Go Succeed programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether a successor programme is planned to replace Go Succeed programme.
answer_text: My Department does not have a role in the administration of Go Succeed, which is funded through the Shared Prosperity Fund. It was anticipated that this funding would be transferred to the Executive following the British Government’s Spending Review in June.		In anticipation of that announcement my Department was ready to establish a co-design group.  However, the British Government has instead created a role for the NIO. I have written to the Secretary of State seeking clarity on the future of this vital programme. Disappointingly, that clarity has not yet been provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the successor scheme to Explore Enterprise Programme, Get Started in Business, by Excalibur Press Projects team (i) was advertised; and (ii) when it went out for public tender.
answer_text: My Department does not have a role in the Get Started in Business programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any funding she is seeking for the replacement of the Go Succeed programme.
answer_text: My Department does not have a role in the administration of Go Succeed, which is funded through the Shared Prosperity Fund. 		I fully recognise the significance of maintaining a strong and responsive enterprise support infrastructure. I am actively pursuing further information on future funding arrangements for Go Succeed and will keep all stakeholders updated as the situation evolves.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of (i) the €14 million Shared Island Media initiative; and (ii) whether this interferes with independence of Northern Ireland’s media.
answer_text: While still in development, we understand the initiative aims to support new talent and capacity building in relation to cross border journalism and content, to enhance public understanding on issues of the day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the estimated number of zebra mussels in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: DAERA is funding an Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute project to survey the distribution and abundance of the zebra mussel (Dreissena polymorpha) in Lough Neagh. The project is due to report next year and this will provide information on the abundance and spread of zebra mussels in Lough Neagh.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department was involved in the Queen's University Belfast cross-border collaboration with the Dundalk Institute of Technology.
answer_text: I warmly welcome this partnership between Queen’s University Belfast and Dundalk Institute of Technology. As autonomous bodies, decisions of this nature rest with the institutions themselves.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has engaged with HM Treasury on a reduced VAT pilot scheme on hospitality, ahead of the Autumn Budget 2025.
answer_text: I have consistently engaged with the British Government on the challenges faced by our local hospitality and tourism sector, particularly in relation to the higher VAT rates faced by these businesses, relative to the South.I have raised the issue of VAT on the hospitality sector with the previous Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST), as part of our Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee (F:ISC) meetings. It was also raised during the meeting the deputy First Minister and I had with the Chancellor on 12 August, and I then subsequently wrote to the current CST, James Murray MP, urging him to act in this area ahead of the British Government’s Autumn Budget. VAT remains an Excepted Matter, and the power to change VAT rates rests with the British Government. Unfortunately, the British Government continues to show no willingness to address this issue. For my part, I will continue to make the case for fiscal devolution and following the Autumn Budget, my Department will now intensify work to progress this. We need a tax system which is fair, proportionate and progressive, and one which allows our businesses to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of (i) the Strategic Rates Policy Road Map oral statement, delivered by the Minister of Finance on 18 November 2025; and (ii) how this will support hospitality businesses.
answer_text: When I was Minister of Finance, I kick started a vital process of work required to build a progressive rates system based on the principles of fairness and equity. Our rates system should align with and underpin Executive priorities, stimulate our economy and support the growth of our taxbase by creating the conditions for businesses to thrive.Year 2 of the strategic review of the rating system for 2026/27 is now looking at two policy measures within the non-domestic rating system. The first measure is the Small Business Rate Relief (SBRR) scheme including the scope for enhanced sectoral targeting and application.I welcome the proposals brought forward by the current Finance Minister in his 18 November statement to the Assembly. The proposals aim to strengthen business support via the SBRR, funded by additional revenue from the domestic rating system. A rates paper referring to the Maximum Capital Value and the Early Payment Discount within the domestic rating system was submitted to the Executive in June 2025 which identified £9million in savings for public services. I understand the Finance Minister is now seeking the backing of his Executive colleagues and Members in the Assembly to realise that objective. The current SBRR scheme supports around 30,000 ratepayers at a cost of £22million to the Executive, and substantial support is already provided to the hospitality sector through that scheme. I expect that the hospitality sector would therefore benefit from any additional enhancement to the scheme, if it is agreed by the Executive. The cost of doing business remains a key concern for our hospitality industry and, as such, I welcome these initiatives of the Finance Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to address delays in responding to maintenance requests for Housing Executive properties.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Performance under the Response Maintenance contract is managed through a structured framework of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). These KPIs cover timeliness, quality, customer satisfaction, and compliance. The Housing Executive monitors these monthly using KPI reports, post-inspections, customer surveys, as well as regular progress meetings. All KPIs have defined Minimum Acceptable Performance (MAP) thresholds, reported on a red/green system, with underperformance triggering an appropriate formal management action to ensure corrective action is taken as and when required.When performance falls short, for example, works not completed within agreed timescales, low quality pass rates in post-inspections or customer dissatisfaction, this requires the Contractor to submit a Remedial/Improvement Plan, setting out root causes, corrective actions such as revised scheduling or enhanced quality checks, and clear recovery timelines. If plans are not satisfactory or improvements are not achieved, issues are escalated through Regional or Exceptional Contract Performance Review Meetings, where the potential outcomes range from further corrective requirements to contract termination for Contractor Default.The framework which the contracts are managed also embeds accountability through financial measures: Low Service Damages (LSDs) apply where KPIs fall below MAP.  Additionally, persistent non-compliance is tracked through Default Notices, with thresholds in place to escalate underperformance or initiate termination proceedings. Finally, the framework supports continuous improvement and benchmarking. Monthly and annual review meetings provide opportunities for both Housing Executive and Contractor to analyse results and agree improvements, while benchmarking with peer organisations (e.g., through Housemark) helps drive efficiency and quality. Where issues have wider impact across contracts, they may also be escalated to the Strategic Core Group for resolution. Together, these processes ensure performance in timeliness and quality is rigorously monitored, corrective action is enforced where required, and service delivery continually improves to meet customer and contractual expectations.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the preparations in place to respond to any extreme snowfall warnings in the coming period.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements have been finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are complete and salt stocks have been replenished. My Department commits significant resources to its winter service programme and every night, from the end of October, up to 300 people will be on standby ready to salt main roads, helping drivers across the north to cope with the wintry conditions. Contracts are also in place to enable contractors and farmers to help to clear roads during periods of prolonged snow. While my Department targets the limited resources available for winter service on the busier main through routes, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Our salt reserves have been filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of winter service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required.My Department’s Winter Service Policy can be viewed via the following link : RSPPG E022, and details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via DFI Roads Salted Network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to increase the provision of care packages to enable patients to be discharged from hospital and return to their homes.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any additional resources and funding that will be made available to local councils to manage the impact of extreme weather conditions.
answer_text: DAERA does not have any funding in 2025-26 set aside to allocate to councils for extreme weather conditions.  However, in such extreme weather scenarios, my Department can provide staff support and/or equipment assistance in a co-ordinated multi-agency approach.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Post Office in Northern Ireland, to provide clarity on the requirements for private individuals posting goods between Great Britain and Northern Ireland, under the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: The Windsor Framework establishes a new set of arrangements for the movement of goods between Britain and the north. The British Government is responsible for the implementation of these arrangements, including for consumer parcels, via HMRC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the extent of the Phase 1 works on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, including the specific sections of the route, covered by this Phase.
answer_text: The procurement strategy for the A5WTC project adopted an Early Contractor Involvement (ECI) approach, with a 2 phased delivery mechanism. Three contracts were awarded, covering the full length of the proposed new dual carriageway. In Phase 1 each contractor has been responsible for supporting the Department through the design process, Public Inquiry and statutory processes.  No parts of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme have been completed to date under Phase 1. Completion of any part of the scheme will only occur under Phase 2 when the contract for the full construction would be entered.Only on the successful completion of the statutory processes, including legal challenges and a number of other conditions precedent, would the Department be in a position to enter into Phase 2 which would include any contract award for the construction phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) which parts of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme have been completed to date, under Phase 1; and (ii) what works remain outstanding under Phase 1.
answer_text: The procurement strategy for the A5WTC project adopted an Early Contractor Involvement (ECI) approach, with a 2 phased delivery mechanism. Three contracts were awarded, covering the full length of the proposed new dual carriageway. In Phase 1 each contractor has been responsible for supporting the Department through the design process, Public Inquiry and statutory processes.  No parts of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme have been completed to date under Phase 1. Completion of any part of the scheme will only occur under Phase 2 when the contract for the full construction would be entered.Only on the successful completion of the statutory processes, including legal challenges and a number of other conditions precedent, would the Department be in a position to enter into Phase 2 which would include any contract award for the construction phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of cases of animals dying as a result of farm accidents recorded, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information. There is no centralised record detailing the number of cases of animals dying as a result of farm accidents. Public bodies, such as the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service or the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland, may deal with animals that have died following a farm accident without any requirement to notify the Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that individuals who have been medically retired after long-term employment, and who are not eligible for Personal Independence Payment, have access to adequate financial support.
answer_text: Individuals who have been medically retired after long-term employment and who are unfit for work may be eligible for New Style Employment Support Allowance based on their national insurance contribution record. Universal Credit can provide support with day to day living costs including the cost of housing, children and childcare.  Additional short term financial help in emergency or crisis situations may be accessed through the Finance Support service.The Department’s Make the Call service is available to help assess all the support and benefits an individual may be entitled to.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of instances of reported abuse against elected representatives, in each of the last five years, broken down by gender.
answer_text: Abuse and attacks on elected representatives are appalling, and I condemn this activity in the strongest possible terms. Putting up with such abuse should never be considered an acceptable part of any job, and every party in the Assembly has a role to play in calling it out and dialling down the toxic rhetoric that so often emboldens this type of behaviour outside the Chamber and online.The Department of Justice does not hold information on the instances of reported abuse against elected representatives as part of its prosecutions and convictions datasets.  The recording of information on offences reported to police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to instances of abuse against elected representatives reported to police, as detailed in your question, to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps the Executive Office is taking to promote participation in public life by elected representatives, in the face of abuse and threats.
answer_text: While The Executive Office does not have a formal remit to encourage participation in public life as elected representatives, we fully acknowledge and accept our responsibility as Ministers to condemn all abuse, intimidation and threats against elected representatives and to continue to publicly support and defend those who face such abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any resources being provided to the PSNI, to prevent violence against elected representatives.
answer_text: Violence, intimidation or threats against elected representatives are wholly unacceptable and must be condemned unequivocally. Such actions undermine democracy and the safety of those who serve the public.The prioritisation and utilisation of resources is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the oversight role of the Policing Board, it would not be appropriate for me to comment on specific resource allocations.However, I am confident that the PSNI is committed to supporting all elected representatives and doing everything they can to ensure we can carry out our roles free from violence.You may, therefore, wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures her Department is implementing to enhance protections for people in public life.
answer_text: The issue of protection and enhanced protection for those in public life is generally a personal matter. If a person in public life should receive a threat or be intimidated and/or harassed, this type of criminal activity is an operational matter for the PSNI, which is operationally independent and has responsibility for assessing and responding to threats and providing security advice to individuals who may be at risk.  Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by my Department and NI Policing Board, also play an important role in identifying and addressing community safety issues, such as home safety measures, for everyone in our society. PCSPs work alongside the PSNI to raise awareness of personal and home safety, delivering community safety initiatives and providing practical crime prevention advice across local areas.It is totally unacceptable for anyone, whether in their personal or professional life, to be subject to threats or intimidation.  Incidents of this nature are abhorrent and wholly unacceptable and should not be tolerated within our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether all schools under the Education Authority have a formal security lockdown procedure in place.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) School Lockdown Guidance was developed in response to requests from individual school leaders for support and guidance where they had experienced a threat to the safety of their staff or the safety of children in their care. The guidance also aligns with the anticipated implementation of The Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Act 2025, commonly referred to as ‘Martyn’s Law’. This legislation is likely to apply in due course to all premises to which the public have access, including schools. Until the regulations are brought in to give effect to the Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Act 2025, there is no legislative requirement for security lockdown procedures. However, it is an opportunity for schools to prepare and have procedures in place for its imminent implementation.The Act received Royal Assent in April 2025 and the Home Office has indicated that there is likely to be an implementation period of at least two years from that date before the Act comes into force.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how often security lockdown drills are required to be carried out in (i) primary; and (i) secondary schools.
answer_text: Emergency procedures are vital to ensure safe and robust arrangements are in place within schools to manage challenging incidents. These arrangements need to be tested regularly to support learning and ensure all systems work properly. The Education Authority has recommended that School Lockdown Procedures are practiced regularly, a minimum of at least once every academic year. This is the same requirement for both primary and secondary schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether the Education Authority has issued specific guidance for communicating with parents during security lockdowns; and (ii) whether this differs between primary and secondary schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority has provided schools with guidance for the development and delivery of School Lockdown Procedures. The guidance includes steps on communicating with parents/guardians during security lockdowns. This guidance is the same for both primary and secondary schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) training; and (ii) guidance, provided to school staff to implement security lockdown procedures effectively.
answer_text: In February 2025, the Education Authority (EA) provided principals and boards of governors with guidance for the development and delivery of their School Lockdown Procedures. The EA has advised that it has received minimal requests for training from school leaders, and the feedback has been that the guidance is very self-explanatory. However, where schools have sought clarification on any aspect of the guidance, the EA’s Health, Safety and Emergency Planning team have provided the necessary support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any reasoning behind the Education Authority implementing security lockdown guidance in schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s School Lockdown Guidance was developed in response to requests from individual school leaders for support and guidance where they had experienced a threat to the safety of their staff or the safety of children in their care. It is common practice for school leaders to risk assess any activities that pose a significant risk to the safety of pupils or staff, with a view to putting the necessary arrangements in place to mitigate any perceived risk and the Education Authority actively work with school leaders to support them in this regard.  The guidance also aligns with the anticipated implementation of The Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Act 2025, commonly referred to as ‘Martyn’s Law’. This legislation is likely to apply in due course to all premises to which the public have access, including schools. This legislation was initiated by the Home Office following the conclusion of the Manchester Arena Inquiry and London Bridge Inquests. Both the inquiry and inquests called for the introduction of legislation and guidance to protect the public.  It is important to emphasise that the Education Authority is not aware of any credible threats to schools at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the rationale for the 12-month deadline for transferring NHS pension rights from England to the HSC Pension Scheme in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether any flexibility exists for individuals who were unaware of the deadline at the time of joining the scheme.
answer_text: The HSC Pension Scheme regulations stipulate that when applying for the transfer of benefits the application must be made before the end of the period of one year starting on the day the member becomes eligible to be an active member of the scheme.  The rationale for the one year deadline is to encourage prompt action to ensure a seamless and fair transfer of equivalent pension benefits between corresponding public sector health schemes within the UK.  The statutory time limit is set to ensure actuarial certainty and valuation; and to aid administrative efficiency. HSC Pension Scheme Guides and a New Joiner Questionnaire are provided to employees when employment commences.  They set out the process of transferring pension benefits from another scheme and the timescales that must be adhered to.  The deadline is a statutory time limit specified in the scheme regulations and late applications may be accepted only in exceptional circumstances if it is confirmed that the member was previously denied access to, or information about, the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the options that exist for individuals who have moved into Northern Ireland Civil Service roles, and cannot transfer their pension from the UK Civil Service.
answer_text: Pension transfers are permitted in accordance with the Civil Service Pension Scheme rules. Members must apply to transfer-in their pension within the first 12 months of joining the Civil Service here. Applications after this 12 month deadline are only considered in exceptional circumstances at the discretion of the Scheme Manager, as permitted under the scheme rules. Members seeking consideration of a late transfer must provide the rationale as to why they believe their case warrants acceptance outside the statutory timeframe. If a transfer is not possible or requested, any accrued pension benefits built up in the Civil Service Pension Scheme in Britian may be preserved in that scheme. These benefits can be claimed at scheme pension age or earlier on an actuarially reduced basis. Civil Service Pensions Branch cannot advise on options which may exist outside the scope of the scheme rules.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the report on the Independent Review of Teacher Workload will be published.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether individuals employed within health and social care in Northern Ireland can apply to transfer previous NHS pension rights internally after the 12-month deadline has passed.
answer_text: The HSC Pension Scheme regulations stipulate that when applying for the transfer of benefits the application must be made before the end of the period of one year starting on the day the member becomes eligible to be an active member of the scheme.  This deadline is a statutory time limit specified in the scheme regulations and late applications may be accepted only in exceptional circumstances if it is confirmed that the member was previously denied access to, or information about, the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total investment in (a) greenways; and (b) cycle paths, under Active Travel, over the last five years, and (ii) any recorded data on reductions in transport-related emissions, attributable to this investment.
answer_text: Greenways are generally council led, with my Department providing grant funding of up to 50% of the capital costs of approved projects. In the last five years my department has issued or committed around £10m to councils for greenway schemes. Officials are also directly progressing a number of active travel capital projects along predominantly traffic free routes.My department does not monitor spend on cycle paths separately, however, these are delivered as part of dedicated active travel projects. Details of my department’s spend on active travel since the introduction of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 to financial year 2024/25 is available at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/details-spend-active-travel-introduction-climate-change-act-northern-ireland-2022.Reductions in transport-related emissions are not directly attributable to investment in active travel and can be influenced by many factors including vehicle technology improvements and modal shift. However, active travel contributes to emissions reduction when individuals substitute short car journeys with walking or cycling, alongside delivering health and environmental benefits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions his Department is taking to address transport poverty and lack of bus capacity, in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, particularly for pupils who face long travel times to access education.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the delivery of school transport, in line with my Department’s Home to School Transport Policy. The EA has advised me that it currently provides transport assistance for 5,837 pupils in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone area with around 1,700 pupils travelling on Ulsterbus services.  Due to the rural nature and the geographical spread of pupils, large public transport vehicles can be impractical in this area, therefore the EA operates 82 dedicated school bus services, transporting around 3,700 pupils to school on its own fleet of yellow school buses. An additional 400 pupils travel on 13 bus services under contracts the EA holds with private hire companies.  The EA endeavours to put appropriate transport arrangements in place for all eligible pupils. To ensure that school transport services can be provided for as many pupils as possible in rural areas, pupils can be required to take connecting services which can lead to longer journeys than expected, however provision is reviewed annually to minimise travel times as much as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department can take to support those with Huntingdon’s disease to have early access to the UK gene therapy trial.
answer_text: I am aware of recent media reports regarding a new gene therapy for Huntington’s disease. While promising, we must be mindful that the study is still in its very early stages and unfortunately there are no sites currently recruiting to this trial in Northern Ireland. However, care for patients with Huntington’s Disease is provided through the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Huntington’s Disease service and I would suggest that patients speak with their clinical consultant directly regarding eligibility for, and availability of, ongoing suitable clinical trials. Work to improve access to clinical trials for people living with a rare condition here is being taken forward under my Department’s Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Action Plan. The Action Plan and annual progress reports can be found on the Department’s website at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-rare-diseases-action-plan-202223-and-reports

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any progress can be made on the Northern Ireland Football Fund, while the legal challenge is active.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to work with colleagues in the Departmental Solicitors Office in relation to the legal challenges brought against the Performance Programme within The Northern Ireland Football Fund. At this stage I am not in a position to confirm what impact, if any, it will have on the progression to future stages of that Programme. Notwithstanding, my officials are continuing to assess the information submitted by applicants for completion of the robust due diligence checks, as outlined in my statement on 15th September 2025.Progress continues on the other two strands of The Northern Ireland Football Fund: The Grassroots Facilities Programme and development of a National Football Centre.  My officials are finalising the application process for the Grassroots Strand and are also reviewing a Strategic Outline Business Case for a National Football Centre submitted by the Irish FA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any meetings have taken place with football clubs following the submission of documentation for the next steps stage of the Northern Ireland Football Fund, as outlined in the Department’s guidance.
answer_text: I can confirm that no meetings have taken place yet with any club following the submission of documentation for the due diligence stage. 	Officials within my Department are currently working with colleagues in the Strategic Investment Board, Departmental Solicitors Office and DoF’s Central Procurement Directorate to examine the documents submitted by the applicants and will contact applicants in due course if necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what support his Department can provide to families and carers of individuals living with Huntington’s disease.
answer_text: Care for patients in Northern Ireland with Huntington’s Disease is co-ordinated through the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, which offers a dedicated Huntington’s Disease service based at Belfast City Hospital. The service offers emotional and practical support to patients and families or carers affected by Huntington’s Disease, as well as planning, providing and evaluating care programmes in consultation with patients and their families or carers. The service also provides an educational resource for families and healthcare professionals and works closely with other care professionals to help patients access further services that may be helpful.  In addition, the Huntington’s Disease Association Northern Ireland (HDANI) works throughout Northern Ireland providing support, information, advice and social connections to those living with the impact of Huntington’s Disease. https://www.hdani.org.uk/Huntington’s is considered a rare disease and my Department published the Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Action Plan in March 2022, setting out 14 high level actions designed to improve the lives of people living with a rare condition in Northern Ireland, with input from the rare disease community. The Action Plan and annual Progress Reports can be found on my Department’s website: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-rare-diseases-action-plan-202223-and-reports

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of incidents of failed street lighting, recorded in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information by constituency. However, I can confirm that for both the Fermanagh & Omagh and Mid Ulster District Councils, which includes the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency, there were the following recorded defects within the last five years.Financial YearRecorded street light defects2021-222,7082022-231,8662023-241,0182024-256842025-26 (to date)263

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any speed restrictions that have been applied to the A5 carriageway, in the last five years.
answer_text: In the last 5 years my Department has introduced new speed restrictions:On the A5 Tullyvar Road, Augnacloy where the 40mph restriction had been extended by 185m; and On the A5 Ballygawley Roundabout where a 50mph restriction has been introduced along with a controlled crossing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of noise complaints received in Fermanagh and South Tyrone in the last 12 months; and (ii) what enforcement actions have been taken.
answer_text: My Department publishes an annual noise complaint statistics report, which outlines the number of noise complaints received by each of the 11 district councils in Northern Ireland. It also provides figures on the number of notices served and prosecutions in relation to these complaints. The Noise Complaint Statistics 2024-25 report (covering 1 April 2024 – 31 March 2025) will be published in the coming weeks. The most recent report covering the 2023-24 period can be accessed via the following link: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/noise-complaint-statistics-northern-ireland-2023-2024
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with local heritage groups, following the discovery of an 8,000-year-old settlement, during the Derrygonnelly community excavation.
answer_text: My department has not had any engagement with local heritage groups on this excavation. The work was carried out by archaeologists from Queen’s University Belfast’s Centre for Community Archaeology, under an excavation licence issued by the department. Officials have, however, monitored the work, and I understand that a comprehensive report on any findings, must be supplied to the department within 6 months of the excavation. Once accepted, this will become publicly available in the Historic Environment Record of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people currently on healthcare waiting lists, in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: Following the recent rollout of the Encompass system across all five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts, data relating to healthcare waiting lists by post code is still undergoing validation. Until this process is complete, this information will not be available for official statistical use. My Department therefore cannot provide this data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department is providing support to traders in Benbrub, disrupted by the closure of the main Maydown Road, following damage caused by Storm Amy.
answer_text: I fully acknowledge the inconvenience caused by the closure and the impact it has had on the local business and residential community.The closure undoubtedly presents challenges to local business and the community, however my Department does not have the powers to provide compensation for economic losses resulting from road closures.I assure you my Department will continue to prioritise works at this location in order to accelerate the repair and have this important local road reopened as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the current budget for his Department; and (ii) what any shortfall represents in terms of frontline services and staff numbers.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how he is supporting women into farming.
answer_text: My Department continues to support women in farming by promoting agriculture courses at CAFRE through careers events, school engagement, and Open Days. Female students are regularly featured in publicity materials and social media campaigns. Provisional data from September 2025 shows that 37% of CAFRE agriculture enrolments are female.DAERA also highlights the role of women in farming through initiatives such as the pilot Farming for Generations scheme, which includes female representation in promotional activities. The scheme’s selection criteria were designed to encourage participation from families with a potential female successor in the farm business.In addition, my Department meets quarterly with a rural stakeholder forum. In response to a request for proposals to support rural development, the forum submitted an initial “Women in Agriculture” pilot programme in February 2025. Following a meeting in June, the forum agreed to refine and resubmit the proposal, though a revised version has not yet been received.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of local councils engaging in Friendly Cooperative Cities Agreements, with Chinese cities.
answer_text: The engagement with Chinese cities through the Friendly Cooperative Cities Agreements is an operational matter for each individual council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail progress on the Committee for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs' March 2022 Breaking the Grass Ceiling report, within his Department.
answer_text: My Department has taken forward several recommendations from the AERA Committee’s report, with a focus on improving access, representation, and support for women in agriculture:Training and Knowledge Transfer: Programmes under the Sustainable Agriculture Programme are open to all farm businesses. CAFRE continues to ensure women can access training through flexible formats, including digital options with 24/7 availability.Business Development and Representation: The Business Sustainability Groups now include farm family members. Of 1,387 enrolments to Business Development Groups, 427 businesses nominated a female representative alongside the lead applicant.Education and Participation: DAERA actively promotes agricultural education for young women. As of October 2024, women made up 37.8% of all agriculture course enrolments, rising to 53% in Higher Education. Provisional figures from September 2025 show this trend continuing.Generational Renewal: The Farming for Generations pilot scheme has featured female participants in its promotional activities and was designed to encourage female succession in farm businesses.Public Appointments: To address underrepresentation on Boards, the Department’s Public Appointments Unit has promoted female applications through testimonials and targeted outreach.Rural Stakeholder Engagement: The Department meets quarterly with a rural stakeholder forum. In response to a request for proposals to support rural development, the forum submitted an initial “Women in Agriculture” pilot programme in February 2025. Following a June meeting, the forum agreed to refine and resubmit the proposal, though a revised version has not yet been received.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the potential risks to farmers whose data has been compromised following the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute data breach.
answer_text: The data breach contained personally identifiable information for a number of farmers, namely names and addresses and in some instances, herd or flock numbers. It also included results of salmonella isolations. AFBI recognises that this could be a source of uncertainty and concern to affected farmers. No bank or financially sensitive details were released. AFBI promptly notified all affected individuals, and reported the incident to the Information Commissioner’s Office on the 4 October 2025. AFBI also took steps to inform my Department, and liaise with key stakeholders and the PSNI. Additionally, AFBI set up a dedicated helpline to provide information, advice and assistance to farmers and are continuing to engage with those affected to address any risks or concerns on a case-by-case basis. AFBI has advised that it is keeping risks under review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any compensation will be paid, in light of the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute data breach.
answer_text: AFBI does not have a compensation scheme in relation to the data breach. AFBI will continue to engage with those affected to address queries and concerns on a case-by-case basis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute data breach will impact any (i) ongoing research projects; (ii) funding; and (iii) partnerships that it is undertaking.
answer_text: AFBI has advised that there is no indication that the data breach will impact any ongoing research projects, funding or partnerships.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people currently living in (i) assisted; and (ii) supported, living facilities, in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘The Supporting People (SP) Programme, which is administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of the Department for Communities, provides grant funding towards to the provision of housing related support services to help vulnerable people live independently in the community.  The SP programme is focused on four thematic areas; working with people who are experiencing homelessness, young people, older people and people with a disability (including mental health and learning disabilities).  Assisted living facilities do not fall under the remit of the SP programme.The number of people currently living in supported accommodation facilities funded through the SP programme in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency area totals 391. This is broken down thematically as; 34 Homeless, 15 Young People, 279 Older People, 63 Disability & Mental Health. Note. Figures are based off information supplied by SP provider organisations.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute is communicating with affected farmers about the recent data breach; and (ii) any initial support steps being taken to protect their businesses.
answer_text: I am advised that AFBI issued letters to all affected farmers notifying them that personally identifiable information had been disclosed in error in a data breach. Letters included details of a helpline number for farmers to contact AFBI if they have any questions or concerns or require further information. This helpline remains open. AFBI has also engaged with key stakeholders to make them aware of the breach and continues to liaise with these bodies regarding guidance and advice that can be offered to support any concerned farmers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of retirements from the public sector, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWERRetirements from the wider public sector are not available. The number of retirements from the Civil Service here in each of the last five years, can be found in Figure 10 of NICS Personnel Statistics 2024 - Tables. 2025 figures are unavailable at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of pension payments paid to public sector retirees, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Minister of Finance is only responsible for the NI Civil Service pension schemes and the amounts payable to retirees under these schemes in the last five years is set out below.2024/25£’0002023/24£’0002022/23£’0002021/22£’0002020/21£’000Pensions to retired employees368,198342,371307,004290,347286,001Commutations and lump sum benefits on retirement91,91176,68674,89562,40655,857These amounts are included within our annual Superannuation and other Allowances Pension Scheme Statements specifically at Note 15.5 which are produced and published annually on the Departmental website DoF Annual Report and Accounts | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail forecasted public sector employee numbers, for each year until 2030.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance is responsible for supporting the recruitment process within the Civil Service here; however, individual departments determine their respective staffing requirements in line with their allocated budgets. Similarly, public sector organisations set their own staffing levels based on what is financially sustainable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department would consider tolling any future road projects, as a means of revenue of raising.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to conduct a high level review of possible alternative funding methods which could facilitate the progression of major infrastructure projects. This work is at an early stage and will take considerable time to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of employees working in the public sector.
answer_text: ANSWEREstimates of the number of public sector employee jobs are sourced from NISRA’s NI Quarterly Employee Survey (QES). The most recent estimate, at June 2025, shows that there are 230,120 public sector jobs here. Please note that the QES is a count of jobs rather than people; if a person has more than one job, they will be counted more than once.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support available to (i) businesses; and (ii) individuals, facing economic challenges and the threat of voluntary liquidation.
answer_text: Uncertainty in the workplace is challenging for both business owners and employees. While Invest NI does not offer direct support for voluntary liquidation, a range of services is available to help businesses facing economic difficulties. Invest NI provides expert guidance on competitiveness, cost reduction, supply chain, energy efficiency, and workforce skills. For practical advice and up-to-date information, I encourage businesses to visit https://www.nibusinessinfo.co.uk or contact Invest NI’s Business Support Team at 0800 181 4422 or enquiry@investni.com.Individuals facing redundancy or unemployment can access tailored guidance through my Department’s Careers Service, which works closely with the Department for Communities (DfC). We offer skills programmes such as Assured Skills Academies, Skills Focus, InnovateUs, and Skill Up, supporting both individuals and employers to upskill and reskill. Details are available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/skillup.DfC also provides a Redundancy Service for employers and employees (dfcemployerservices@communities-ni.gov.uk). The Labour Relations Agency offers webinars and confidential advice on redundancy (03300 555 300). The Law Centre NI also provides free, specialist legal advice on employment rights (028 9024 4401 or employmentadvice@lawcentreni.org).I am committed to ensuring those impacted by economic challenges are aware of the support available to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of insolvencies reported in Northern Ireland, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of insolvencies reported in the North in the last five financial years broken down by bankruptcy, compulsory winding up orders for limited companies and Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs) is detailed in the table below:-2020/20212021/20222022/20232023/20242024/2025Bankruptcies10412391222242Compulsory Winding Up Orders63733100126IVAs12191327137311261109Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs) are administered by private sector insolvency practitioners.  My Department is only responsible for maintaining a register of IVAs in the North of Ireland.Companies may also enter Creditors Voluntary Liquidation (CVLs), Administration and Company Voluntary Arrangements (CVAs) which are all administered by private sector insolvency practitioners.  My Department has no role in these therefore and I am unable to provide numbers in relation to these types of insolvencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the total spend of Annually Managed Expenditure, over the duration of the winding down of the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme, up to 2036.
answer_text: The costs of the closure proposal are estimated at £196m AME and £17m DEL bringing the total overall estimated costs of Closure to £213m. The forecast AME costs have been calculated on the assumption that all accredited installations that are live approved on Ofgem’s system can, and will, be eligible for Closure payments. These figures therefore represent the maximum costs of the closure payments. There are a number of inactive installations on the Scheme. If for any reason these, or other participants fail to complete accreditation for closure payments then the total will be less. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of claims for fraudulent or wrongful trading, registered, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: When a company enters insolvency the office holder is required to report any matters of potential misconduct by the directors of that company to my Department’s Insolvency Service.  Details are not kept, however, of all potential matters of misconduct in each case that is received.  As a result, the numbers of cases referred to the Insolvency Service, where fraudulent or wrongful trading was identified as potential misconduct, cannot be provided.  However, the total number of director disqualifications obtained in the last five financial years, and the number of these which were as a result of fraudulent and/or wrongful trading, can be provided and are detailed in the table below:-2020-20212021-20222022-20232023-20242024-2025Total Disqualifications Obtained1842192640Fraudulent Trading Allegation01039Wrongful Trading Allegation10000

answer_text: The number of insolvencies reported in the North of Ireland in the last five financial years for bankruptcy, compulsory winding up orders for limited companies and Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs), broken down by constituency, is detailed in the table below.Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs) are administered by private sector insolvency practitioners.
