question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism tax on fertiliser will have on the agri-food sector when it comes into effect in January 2027.
answer_text: The UK Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) is designed to prevent carbon leakage from UK businesses to other jurisdictions, ensure fair competition for UK producers, and encourage global emission reductions by applying a carbon price to imported goods equivalent to that faced by locally produced goods.Responsibility for the development of UK CBAM policy rests with the UK Government, however both I and my officials liaise closely with, and receive regular updates from, our counterparts across the relevant Whitehall Departments. Through this engagement I have stressed the importance of considering the interaction with the EU CBAM, which is due to come into effect from 1 January 2026.To date the UK Government have not published any assessment of impact which the UK CBAM may have on individual sectors, including on the agri food sector as a consequence of the inclusion of fertiliser production within scope. On 24 April this year, however, the Government published a consultation on the draft CBAM legislation, and alongside this His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs (HMRC) published a Draft tax information and impact note, which does not go into product specific detail. I am including a link to this document, which you may find helpful, below:https://www.gov.uk/government/consultations/draft-legislation-carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism/draft-tax-information-and-impact-note
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, for her assessment on (i) the impact to international confidence in the Northern Ireland economy; and (ii) whether this will act as a deterrent to future trade.
answer_text: I have made clear my expectation that public funds are not used to help arm Israel or indeed any country committing genocide. I do not consider this position a threat to international confidence in our economy, nor do I see it as a barrier to future trade.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the oversight mechanism her Department will include in the Renewable Heat Incentive (Closure of the Non-Domestic Scheme) Bill, to control costs with metering ending.
answer_text: The RHI (Closure of the Non-domestic Scheme) Bill provides the Department with the powers to bring forward Regulations in relation to the closure of the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme. The arrangements and mechanisms in relation to closure will subsequently be set out in the Regulations. The Regulations will be finalised taking into consideration the responses to the consultation.The public consultation (https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/consultations/closure-northern-ireland-non-domestic-renewable-heat-incentive-scheme) sets out the Department’s proposals for closure arrangements.The sections of the consultation document relevant to cost control are The Basis for Future Payments (Part C), Payment Banding and Adjustment (Part D) and Compliance (Part E).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department will take control of the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme, when Ofgem steps away in April 2026.
answer_text: My Department has been working with Ofgem to ensure that there will be a smooth transition of administration of the scheme under Ofgem to administration of closure under DfE.My Department is developing a system, in collaboration with DoF and external contractors, where participants will be able to manage a range of administrative tasks related to their accredited installation, including submission of an annual declaration to assess eligibility for closure payments. Arrangements are also being made for the secure transfer of participants’ data from Ofgem to DfE.My Department aims to implement closure payments during 2026, subject to the passage of legislation through the Assembly. A public consultation on proposals for closure is currently live, and can be viewed on the Department’s website at https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/consultations/closure-northern-ireland-non-domestic-renewable-heat-incentive-scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagements his Department has had with the UK Government on the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism tax on fertiliser and the impact it will have on the agri-food sector when it comes into effect in January 2027.
answer_text: I am in regular contact with my counterparts in the UK Government in relation to the UK Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), due to come into effect from 1 January 2027, as are my officials. UK CBAM is designed to prevent carbon leakage from UK businesses to other jurisdictions, ensure fair competition for UK producers, and encourage global emission reductions by applying a carbon price to imported goods equivalent to that faced by locally produced goods.Responsibility for the development of UK CBAM policy rests with the UK Government, however both I and my officials liaise closely with, and receive regular updates from, our counterparts across the relevant Whitehall Departments. Through this engagement I have stressed the importance of considering the interaction with the EU CBAM, which is due to come into effect from 1 January 2026.To date the UK Government have not published any assessment of impact which the UK CBAM may have on individual sectors, including on the agri food sector as a consequence of the inclusion of fertiliser production within scope. On 24 April this year, however, the Government published a consultation on the draft CBAM legislation, and alongside this His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs (HMRC) published a Draft tax information and impact note, which does not go into product specific detail. I am including a link to this document, which you may find helpful, below:https://www.gov.uk/government/consultations/draft-legislation-carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism/draft-tax-information-and-impact-note
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the key performance outcomes of having a Geno database for cattle.
answer_text: The key performance outcomes which will be delivered by the Bovine Genetics Project database are:The provision of a suite of physical benchmarking reports, at both individual animal and cattle enterprise level, to inform farmers on how their farm business is performing relative to their peers and identify areas for further investigation and improvement.The provision of genomic evaluations, for dairy and beef cattle, from which farmers can make informed breeding decisions to improve the genetic merit of their livestock, which will increase the economic and environmental sustainability of their farm business.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why the creche at the South West Acute Hospital prohibits caring for children with parents who are shift workers.
answer_text: The crèche premises at South Western Acute Hospital are rented from the Northern Ireland Health Group and delivered by an independent organisation, Fermanagh Early Years Centre. The centre offers the equivalent of 80 full time places and complies with all minimum standards for childminding and day care for children under age 12. These standards require the centre to take capacity and staff ratios into account in advance, with children attending on a regular full or part time basis between 7.30am and 6pm. There is flexibility within this pattern as parents, including those who work shifts, can make additional ad hoc requests for extra hours and to swap days. Taking minimum standards requirements into consideration, every effort is made to accommodate these requests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how long it would take to build an accurate Geno database for cattle farmers to genotype all their breeding animals and young calves.
answer_text: The contract to build the Bovine Genetics Project database was awarded to the Irish Cattle Breeding Federation and commenced on 1 January 2025. From 1 September 2026, farmers will be able to supply genotypes from all dairy and beef animals to this database and access genetic merit information of their animals, based on the genotypes provided.	Producers will be encouraged to genotype their animals through the proposed DAERA-funded Bovine Genetics Genotyping Scheme, which will reimburse producers for the costs incurred to genotype their animals.The Scheme will open on 1 September 2026 and will run until 15 May 2028.  The Scheme duration will provide producers with a sufficient time period to genotype their current and future breeding animals, and an initial calf crop.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of reported incidents involving illegal fireworks, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the number of these incidents that resulted in injury.
answer_text: My Department is the relevant authority in respect of explosives licensing and grants licences for the storage, possession, selling or use of legal fireworks.   The Chief Constable is responsible for the enforcements of any breaches of the legislation e.g. possession of fireworks without a valid licence issued by the Department of Justice.Whilst my Department does not hold the information that you have requested my officials sought the information from PSNI and their Operation Support Department has provided the following information.The table below provides the number of fireworks incidents, including injuries, reported to PSNI in the period 01/01/2020 - 16/10/2025.  From the PSNI data it is not possible to confirm whether the fireworks were possessed legally or illegally.YearNumber of fireworks incidentsFireworks + Injury2020194912021104012022106242023104922024103322025 (up to 16/10/25)5631

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how the Executive will ensure that victims’ voices are represented in the development, and implementation, of the UK Government's new Northern Ireland Troubles Bill.
answer_text: The Strategy for Victims and Survivors of the Troubles/Conflict 2024-2034 reinforces our commitment to continue to work with victims and survivors to meet their needs and to ensure that they are supported in dealing with the legacy of the past, and to be flexible in the face of changing needs and prevailing circumstances. It also recognises the importance of truth and justice to victims and survivors, and the need to work towards greater societal recognition of the hurt, loss, and trauma of our past.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the number of low-income families that will benefit from support for uniforms, following the enactment of the School Uniforms Bill, in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: The School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill requires guidelines for all primary and post-primary schools across Northern Ireland to address unfair costs aspects of school uniform policies.  These guidelines will be statutory.  Unfair costs aspects are defined in the Bill in the context of affordability of uniform requirements for lower income households.  The Bill extends eligibility for access to uniform grants to pupils in independent schools.  It also actively supports the use of uniform banks.In Fermanagh and South Tyrone, based on current school census information, there are 3,928 pupils across primary and post-primary schools who are entitled to Free School Meals.  It is my assessment that all lower income families with school-age children in Fermanagh and South Tyrone - and indeed all families of school-age children across Northern Ireland - will benefit from the outworkings of the guidelines and the Bill when these come into operation.  This will be in advance of the 2026/27 school year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail whether his Department has made any assessment on (i) the projected number of farms; and (ii) the anticipated growth within the agricultural sector, in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency, over the next five to ten years
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) has not projected farm numbers or assessed expected growth in Northern Ireland's agricultural sector over the next five to ten years.  Changes in the sector over time will be influenced by numerous factors, many of which are outside the control of DAERA such as world market conditions and individual business decisions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether the Transport Strategy 2035 will be completed by the end of 2025; and (ii) whether any main strategic roads in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency will be included.
answer_text: The draft Transport Strategy sets out a clear vision for a transport system that enables economic growth, strengthens regional balance, and enhances both urban and rural connectivity.  The public consultation on the draft Strategy closed at the end of September 2025.  My Officials have now commenced analysis of the responses received.  The feedback gathered is crucial to shaping a transport system that reflects the needs of all members of society. It is expected that this analysis will be completed by the end of 2025. However, a timeline for final publication cannot be confirmed until a full understanding of the range and detail of responses is achieved.Detailed schemes and interventions for the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency are developed through the Fermanagh and Omagh Sub Regional Transport Plan (FO SRTP).This plan will focus on improving the sustainability of the local transport system within the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area, including active travel, the local road network, public transport, and parking. My officials and their design team partners are currently refining the list of transport options for the plan area in line with the agreed vision and objectives. This is a collaborative process with ongoing stakeholder engagement with Fermanagh and Omagh District Council. In parallel, the Regional Strategic Transport Plan (RSTP) is also being developed by officials. This plan will focus on the strategic road network and is currently undergoing transport modelling analysis to develop a shortlist of interventions across road, bus, and Park & Ride facilities. Officials are working towards the publication of both the RSTP and FO SRTP for consultation in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of how a lower corporation tax would help boost the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: Tax is an important factor that companies consider when making investment decisions. However, the British Government's block grant adjustment mechanism, combined with its underfunding of public services, means that the devolution of Corporation Tax is currently unaffordable. It is my intention to create an attractive investment proposition for firms, by improving skills and ensuring that companies can take full advantage of the dual market access opportunities that exist through the Windsor framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagements his Department has had with the UK Government, advocating for a lifting of the two-child benefit cap.
answer_text: AnswerThe two-child limit has been discussed both formally and informally by me and by my officials during engagement with UK Government representatives and Department for Work and Pensions’ (DWP) officials. The UK Government has announced that the two-child limit will be abolished from April 2026.  My officials are engaging with DWP in terms of the legislative requirements which need to be brought forward to remove the restriction in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any assessment has been made on the effect a lower corporation tax of 15 per cent would have on the Northern Ireland block grant allocation.
answer_text: No formal assessment has been undertaken by my department regarding the impact a 15 per cent Corporation Tax rate would have on our block grant. To be undertaken effectively, this would need to be done by Treasury and HMRC who hold the data. This was the case with previous estimates of the cost of Corporation Tax devolution. However, even without this, the reality is it would cost hundreds of millions of pounds, on an annual basis. I have been clear that the devolution and reduction of Corporation Tax can only occur if it is affordable, furthers economic development, and if it ensures ordinary citizens benefit through the creation of well-paying jobs. Given we are so hard-pressed to fund essential public services, that scale of cost is unaffordable at this time in my view.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any control measures his Department will implement to prevent the spread of the Asian hornet.
answer_text: NIEA officials have traced and removed the first recorded Asian hornet nest in Northern Ireland in Dundonald and are continuing their monitoring and trapping programme in that area to ensure that there are no further nests. Learning points from this experience will be used to review and refine the published DAERA Asian Hornet Contingency Plan which focuses on response to reported hornets. Given this species ability to hide in imported goods and on transport it is extremely difficult to implement effective proactive measures to keep them out of Northern Ireland, and maintaining government and public vigilance is key.NIEA is working in collaboration with the Irish National Parks and Wildlife Service and the National Biodiversity Data Centre (Ireland) through the Shared Island Biosecurity and Invasive Species Initiative, to establish a more joined up approach to responding to the Asian hornet across the island of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the extent Northern Ireland’s current rate of corporation tax acts as a deterrent to inward investment; and (ii) the economic consequences of this.
answer_text: Tax is an important factor that companies consider when making investment decisions. However, the British Government's block grant adjustment mechanism, combined with its underfunding of public services, means that the devolution of Corporation Tax is currently unaffordable. It is my intention to create an attractive investment proposition for firms, by improving skills and ensuring that companies can take full advantage of the dual market access opportunities that exist through the Windsor framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of raising of the threshold on Agriculture Property Relief to £5 million.
answer_text: Analysis undertaken by my Department in December 2024 indicated that 49% of farms in NI (accounting for 80% of land farmed) have agricultural and business property values exceeding £1million (based on an average valuation at £21,000/acre).  Using an average valuation of £21,000/acre, 5% of farms (accounting for 23% of area farmed) in NI would have agricultural and business property value exceeding £5 million.Inheritance Tax is assessed on the market value of assets owned by the deceased which may vary considerably from the NI average and may not always correspond to the entire assets of the farm.  Furthermore, the exact detail of any changes is important and will need to be known before their impact could be assessed on inheritance tax liability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions being taken to reduce the waiting list for replacement, and improvement schemes within Housing Executive developments.
answer_text: I am continuing to press, as a matter of urgency, for the revitalisation of the Housing Executive, which will enable it to increase its investment in replacement and improvement schemes. In the interim, the Housing Executive is actively taking a range of actions to reduce the waiting time for replacement and improvement schemes. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive is facing significant challenges in labour availability and contractor capacity within the current market, which is impacting delivery on the ground. To address this, we are hosting Contractor Engagement events ahead of procurements and incorporating contractor feedback into our procurement strategies and contract conditions where appropriate to improve delivery outcomes. To improve delivery agility, we are developing a strategic procurement plan and reviewing future contract requirements to ensure they are responsive to both our funding position and market conditions.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his bovine TB cooperation project with the Department for Agriculture, Food and the Marine will work.
answer_text: On 10 October 2025 I announced, alongside Martin Heydon TD, Minister of Agriculture, Food and the Marine in the Republic of Ireland, an innovative, cross-border initiative which will aim to assist both jurisdictions in tackling the growing impact of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB).Both Departments will jointly implement a range of measures on a coordinated regional basis, with the aim of reducing bTB incidence and transmission. The roll out of the pilot and the assessment of how well the measures deployed within it are at reducing disease, will be consistent with, and contribute to, both Departments’ policies and approaches to working with farmers and key stakeholders to eradicate bTB. This builds on the TB Partnership Steering Group’s (TBPSG) Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication published in April this year, which identified regionalisation and partnership working as key strategic priorities.  Officials have discussed proposed measures, which are intended to take a holistic disease control approach, and potential area with the TBPSG.   Further detail of this initiative, once finalised and agreed, will be published in the weeks ahead.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount his Department projects to spend on tuberculosis compensation in the current financial year.
answer_text: My Department’s most recent projection for spend on bovine tuberculosis compensation for the 2025/26 financial year is £56,351,574. This is based on a projected rise in the number of reactor animals and the increasing market value of cattle.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all cases of illegal waste sites and dumping in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, in the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the covert nature of illegal waste disposal, it is not possible to provide a definitive count of illegal waste sites across Fermanagh and South Tyrone. However, the Department can confirm that the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) has initiated 13 formal enforcement cases within this area over the last five years.Lower-level incidents and infractions that have not progressed to formal enforcement cases have been addressed through alternative measures, including, but not limited to, the issuance of written warnings. The NIEA remains proactive in monitoring, investigating and enforcing against environmental crime. Site identification and prioritisation are based on a risk assessment framework that considers environmental harm, economic impact, and potential threats to public health. The number of illegal waste sites is fluid, new sites may be identified through intelligence or inspections, while others are remediated or brought under regulatory control. Please be assured that the NIEA remains fully committed to protecting the environment and maintaining public confidence in environmental regulation across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) the number of housing associations currently active in Fermanagh and South Tyrone; and (ii) the number of properties in that constituency managed by housing associations.
answer_text: Housing Regulation Branch undertakes annual assessments of the 19 Registered Housing Associations (RHAs) in Northern Ireland to evaluate their compliance with the Governance, Finance and Consumer standards contained in the Regulatory Framework. The information collected for these assessments provides aggregate data on the total number of properties managed by each RHA across Northern Ireland but does not include details of the geographic location of each of these properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the road improvement works on the Boho Road, approaching the Derrygonnelly Road, Enniskillen.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While Boho Road would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Fermanagh and Omagh District Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) the policy for ensuring the safety of Roads Service staff whilst responding to call-outs, either during or after storm damage; and (ii) whether there has been any change in the policy in recent years.
answer_text: The Department has a duty of care to keep staff safe at all times and in particular during hazardous activities such as during our response to weather events. The Department’s approach is set out in the Policy and Procedure Guide RsPPG_E035: Roads Emergency which is publicly available online at Emergency Response Plan RSPPG E035 | Department for Infrastructure.  In our H&S training it is emphasised that staff are empowered to abort any task if their personal safety is under threat.The Policy and Procedure Guidance document was last updated in September 2024 to reflect the overall change within the Emergency Resilience Network, aligning the Department with wider multi agency emergency protocols and the Met Office warning system.  A review of the document is ongoing to take account of the most recent severe weather events and will be published shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure public safety in urban car parks, particularly during night-time hours.
answer_text: Operational responsibility for car park management in urban car parks generally falls to the relevant council and private providers. In order to help identify and address community safety issues in these and other public areas, my Department and the NI Policing Board jointly fund Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), which are statutory standalone bodies located in each council area that engage and consult with local communities to help identify and address local community safety concerns, including crime and anti-social behaviour.   PCSPs deliver a range of actions in each council area to help address community issues during both daytime and night-time hours. These include identifying hotspots for anti-social behaviour; targeted crime prevention patrols in partnership with the PSNI; the use of community wardens;  the use of signage to deter vehicle crime and promotion of safe parking and driving practices; and community safety awareness campaigns aimed at deterring anti-social behaviour and increasing public confidence. I am mindful that dealing with any criminal activity in these areas remains an operational matter for the PSNI, with the Chief Constable operationally independent and accountable to the NI Policing Board. You may therefore wish to also direct any public safety concerns on the above issue to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the steps her Department is taking to address language and communication barriers in court proceedings.
answer_text: I recognise that a language or communication barrier can have a significant impact on those involved in court proceedings and therefore my Department provides a number of measures and services to assist, such as:The Northern Ireland Registered Intermediary Scheme Registered Intermediaries (RIs) are communication specialists who assist vulnerable victims, witnesses, suspects and defendants with communication deficits to provide their answers more effectively during police interviews and when giving evidence during trials. My Department recruits, trains and maintains a panel of around 30 RIs, most of whom come from a social work or speech and language therapist background. RIs are provided upon request from the PSNI, PPS or the person’s solicitor.Those regularly assisted by RIs include children (by virtue of their young age) and people with learning difficulties, mental health issues, social communication difficulties (such as individuals on the autism spectrum) and those with neurological disorders. RIs carefully assess each individual and provide a report for the judge and legal professionals detailing the person’s communication needs along with recommended steps to help them understand questions. RIs remain with the individual during the court proceedings to provide advice and assistance, such as rephrasing questions to ensure the witness fully understands. They will also alert the Court if the witness is in distress and / or needs a break.   When required, RIs will also assist prisoners and those on remand who are participating in court proceedings by video-link from a prison facility.Over 12,000 vulnerable people have been assisted by an RI since the scheme launched in May 2013; with use of the service growing annually. More information about the RI Scheme can be found on the Department’s website at the following link: Northern Ireland Registered Intermediary Scheme | Department of Justice.Court Interpretation & Translation ServicesThe Northern Ireland Courts & Tribunal Service (NICTS) can arrange for interpretation and translation services to meet the needs of non-English speakers and the deaf or hearing impaired.  The services provided ensure compliance with human rights and disability discrimination legislation and include interpretation and sign language interpretation during court proceedings, as well as speech to text (Palantype), telephone interpretation and written translation of court documents.  More information can be found on the Department’s website at this link: Interpreter and Translation Services | Department of JusticeImprovements to the Court Estate            NICTS is committed to improving the experience of court users as one of the four aims within its Estates Strategy ‘Making the Place for Justice’. NICTS continually strive to improve the accessibility of its estate including the introduction of reasonable adjustments such as induction loop systems for the hearing impaired in courthouses. NICTS has also completed

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any steps her Department can take to improve communication between broadband providers and customers who have been left with no service, following a weather event.
answer_text: My Department has no direct relationship with broadband retail service providers. However, I recognise the importance of timely and effective communication between broadband providers and their customers, and encourage providers to communicate with their customers regarding disruptions to availability. This information should include estimated repair dates and changes to those dates, where relevant.  Following Storm Éowyn I met with Fibrus Networks, the contractor for Project Stratum; Fibrus recognised that they could have communicated better with customers, particularly in the early days of the storm, and improved customer communication was an important part of Fibrus’s Post Incident Review into that storm.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the steps being taken to combat rural crime in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: A partnership approach is central to addressing crime, including agricultural crime, in all regions. The Department of Justice chairs the Rural Crime Partnership (RCP) with members including the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), the Ulster Farmers’ Union, NFU Mutual, the Young Farmers’ Club of Ulster, the Federation of Small Businesses, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs). The RCP shares information on emerging and/or re-occurring crimes, to support a coordinated response and preventative approach to addressing rural crime. RCP members regularly engage with the public via social media to raise awareness of rural crime issues and to encourage rural communities to report such crimes. My Department recently participated in Rural Crime Week NI, a local media campaign led by the PSNI, which provided an opportunity to raise awareness of the diverse issues faced in our rural communities and help provide information and advice to people who may need or want support. During Rural Crime Week PCSP trailer marking events were held, one of which was in Enniskillen where 27 trailers were marked. At a local level, PCSPs, which are funded jointly by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, play a key role in addressing crime, including agricultural crime, and building confidence locally, through engagement and consultation with communities, including rural areas. PCSPs play a pivotal role in supporting communities in identifying solutions to issues of local concern.  They also deliver a range of initiatives to raise awareness and support rural communities and businesses to protect their property and goods.  These initiatives include trailer/equipment marking events and such actions have helped to continue the downward trend in recorded rural and agricultural crime. There are also existing robust mechanisms in place for law enforcement agencies to work collaboratively through the Joint Agency Task Force (JAFT).  Tackling rural crime remains one of the six key priorities of the JATF. The JATF is a highly effective model for tackling cross-border organised crime, with strong practical and strategic collaboration between law enforcement agencies, including PSNI and An Garda Síochána. Notwithstanding the above, the operational detection and investigation of agricultural crime, including how resources are deployed, is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NI Policing Board, and you may therefore wish to also direct your question to the PSNI.The Department will continue to work in partnership with the PSNI and other relevant agencies to tackle crime in rural areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department will take to provide support to Food To Go outlets to ensure fairness and transparency when dealing with online food vendors.
answer_text: My Department and its partner organisations provide a broad range of support to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets. My Department’s Trading Standards Service (TSS) is responsible for promoting and maintaining fair trading, protecting consumers and enabling reputable businesses to thrive across the North. However, the legislative focus is upon consumer protection rather than business to business agreements. Ulitmately, it is for individual businesses to determine if third party vendors can enhance their business model. 	I remain committed to engaging with the sector to better understand their concerns and what steps can be taken to address these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the climate impact of the recently announced Londonderry to Dublin flight in relation to the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: At present, there is insufficient information available regarding the detail of the proposed resumption of flights between Dublin and City of Derry Airport, to determine the potential impact of this proposal.Under the UK Climate Change Act 2008, international aviation emissions from departures originating in the UK are not currently included in carbon budget reporting. As emissions from international aviation do not currently impact upon Northern Ireland’s Carbon Budget this is unlikely to impact on provisions made under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.Emissions from international aviation will however be captured within the scope of the UK and EU Emissions Trading Schemes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the total costs associated with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute's new marine research vessel the R.V Jocelyn Bell Burnell.
answer_text: The R.V Jocelyn Bell Burnell has been commissioned to replace AFBI’s existing research vessel, the R.V Corystes, which has reached the end of its working life.The total approved costs as per the business case are as follows:Capital Costs:The cost of the new vessel is £33.233 million (including inflation and optimism bias). A capital receipt of £4 million is anticipated from the disposal of the R.V Corystes, resulting in a net capital cost of £29.233 million.Resource and Depreciation Costs:Over the lifetime of the vessel (assumed for business case purposes to be 25 years), the Resource DEL costs (including inflation) are projected to be £52.955 million, and Depreciation DEL costs are estimated at £24.032 million, giving a total of £76.987 million. The operation of the new research RV is expected to lead to significant Resource savings of £28.8 million over the business case period when compared to maintaining the status quo.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Enterprise Northern Ireland network in Fermanagh, and Omagh and Mid Ulster, in providing jobs and growing the economy.
answer_text: Enterprise NI, and the Local Enterprise Agencies in Fermanagh & Omagh and Mid Ulster play a crucial role in supporting job creation and economic growth.  Through their role as a delivery partner for Go Succeed, they provide expert advice and guidance to entrepreneurs and businesses and through their properties they provide important workspace for businesses in these areas.   Enterprise NI are members of the SME Entrepreneurship Codesign Group established by my Department to develop actions to improve the start-up landscape across the north.  They are also mandatory members of the 11 Local Economic Partnerships that have been set up across the north to create tailored solutions to local economic problems.  Their inclusion in these two groups highlight the importance of their work to my economic vision and to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the flood alleviation plans her Department has in place for the Arney River, Fermanagh, this coming winter.
answer_text: Officials from my Department’s Rivers Directorate carry out scheduled maintenance on the designated reaches of the Arney River on a cyclical basis. There are 3 designated reaches of the Arney River. One reach is inspected and maintained once in every 3 years. The 2 other reaches are inspected and maintained at least once in every 6 years. The last inspection took place on 27 November 2023 with the subsequent maintenance works completed between August and October 2024.In addition, my Department operates an on-call duty and flooding officer rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year with staff on standby to respond to flooding. Routine cyclical inspections also take place on drainage infrastructure, the maintenance of these is usually increased before and during significant periods of heavy rainfall. Sandbag stores at operational depots, and those at more remote locations close to communities at known flood risk, are fully stocked in preparation for any potential flooding. If the potential threat for any flooding increases, sandbags can be placed on mobile units to enable a timely response as necessary.My Department also has a water level station sited on the Areny River at Tilery Bridge. The water level can be viewed at the following link: https://www.hydrometcloud.de/Rivers_Agency/index.jsp?menu=index. Within the area of Upper Lough Erne, my Department along with multi-agency partners, has developed a specific plan for when water levels rise and agreed thresholds are reached on Upper Lough Erne. This plan has been developed through the Flooding and Severe Weather Group, which is chaired by my Department, and assesses the risks and potential impacts of water levels on low lying roads in the locality around Lough Erne. In addition, and through the work of the Regional Community Flood Resilience Group, there has been engagement with the Upper Lough Erne community group to help them prepare for and respond to weather related emergencies, and to provide co-ordinated multi-agency support to individual properties or communities that may be cut off by flood water and require access to essential services. Through this community resilience work my Department seeks to provide an extra layer of support in addition to the normal response for those who may be impacted by flooding in periods of severe weather.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of individuals from Fermanagh and South Tyrone who have been members of the Invest NI Board, since the establishment of Invest NI in 2002.
answer_text: Neither Invest NI, nor my Department, holds this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department is improving Level 2 and above qualifications in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for South West College which provides a comprehensive suite of qualifications and pathways from Level 2 to Level 6, including Traineeships, Apprenticeships, Higher Level Apprenticeships and Foundation degrees, in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone area. Curriculum provision in South West College is informed and improved through local industry engagement through, for example, Industry Advisory Boards and Sectoral Partnerships. My Department has also embedded a robust quality-improvement planning process to underpin continuous improvement in Further Education colleges, including South West College, and for contracted training providers for Level 2 and above qualifications delivery in that area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of access to veterinary practice in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of any issues with regard to access to veterinary practice in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone area. 	Veterinary Northern Ireland provide a point of contact for the veterinary profession in Northern Ireland and are best placed to comment in relation to veterinary practice access in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline how he is improving access to the angling estate in Lough Erne.
answer_text: My Department manages Public Angling Estate (PAE) angling infrastructure through regular inspections to identify and carry out repairs and if necessary, replacement to maintain and enhance angler access to PAE sites around Lough Erne. New angling stands have been created at Cleenish Bridge and the Round O.The Department also undertakes aquatic weed harvesting on the River Erne PAE sections to allow anglers access to fish those areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of the economic impact the delayed business rates reform is having on our economy.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the answer to AQW 32674/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the rationale for the implementation of DNA sampling of beef and dairy cows.
answer_text: The Bovine Genetics Project (BGP) is one of the measures being progressed through the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.  The Project is of significant interest to industry and government due to the benefits of breeding animals with lower pollution potential, lower carbon footprints, enhanced production efficiency, and improved health and welfare.  Genetic improvement is included as one of the policies to help reduce greenhouse gas emissions in the draft Climate Action Plan (2023-2027), is identified as a key measure in the Bovine Tuberculosis in Northern Ireland Blueprint for eradication, and is one of the mitigations identified in the draft ammonia strategy.		DNA sampling or genotyping of bovine animals is being implemented as part of the Bovine Genetics Project, to ensure that robust and accurate data is available to our farm businesses, enabling them to accelerate the desired rate of genetic gain, and ensure that the desired outcomes of the Project are achieved for all stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why participation in the Bovine Genetics Project is planned to be a conditionality for the Farm Sustainability Payment in 2026.
answer_text: The Bovine Genetics Project (BGP) is one of the measures being progressed through the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.  		The Project is of significant interest to industry and government due to the benefits of breeding animals with improved health and welfare, enhanced production efficiency, lower carbon footprint and lower pollution potential. .  		Genetic improvement is identified as a key measure in the Bovine Tuberculosis in Northern Ireland Blueprint for eradication, included as one of the policies to help reduce greenhouse gas emissions in the draft Climate Action Plan (2023-2027)and is one of the mitigations identified in the draft ammonia strategy.		Participation in the Bovine Genetics Project is planned to be a conditionality of the Farm Sustainability Payment to encourage the uptake of the Project by farm businesses and to ensure the achievement of the benefits that genetic improvement can deliver, both for individual businesses and the wider industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement his Department has had with Retail NI and Hospitality Ulster, regarding business rates relief.
answer_text: ANSWERA series of meetings both on a one-to-one basis and as part of roundtable events have been undertaken by my Department with both Hospitality Ulster and Retail NI since the restoration of the Executive on business rates. Both organisations work closely with my Departmental officials in this area and helped to facilitate the circulation of an online survey on the Strategic Review to businesses between August and September, with the results contributing to the advice provided to me.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the condition of the forestry estate across Fermanagh and South Tyrone following Storm Amy.
answer_text: The Forest Estate including Fermanagh and South Tyrone suffered extensive windblow due to storms prior to storm Amy.  Harvesting and clearance of these areas is ongoing, following which regeneration will occur.   Forest Service’s initial assessment, following storm Amy, indicates that a number of existing windblown areas have been extended to varying degrees.  Additionally, there are further impacts involving single trees and small groups of a sporadic nature within the forests.   Forest Service, DFI Roads and NIE have already undertaken immediate priority clearance works affecting public infrastructure.  Forest Service is continuing clearance of windblown trees restricting access on the internal forest road and path networks and posing risks to public safety and infrastructure.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of her Department's preparedness for Storm Amy.
answer_text: Preparations for Storm Amy began at the earliest opportunity with multi-agency co-ordination taking place ahead of the weekend, well in advance of the storm arriving on Friday and prior to the severe weather warnings coming into effect.Alongside our multi-agency partners, officials worked in difficult conditions to respond and minimise disruption. This was a multi-weather event which brought wind and rain causing widespread fallen trees and flooding issues. Approximately 2005 sandbags were distributed and staff worked through the weekend to clear debris to help flood waters to get away. This work did not just start when the storm arrived - proactive culvert screen maintenance work was undertaken by staff prior to the event and existing flood alleviation schemes prevented flooding in many areas. This preventative work contributed to hundreds of homes being protected.As a result of Storm Amy, my Department responded to reports of approximately 1500 incidents and work on the clear-up is now well advanced. Following the initial clear up, it will take a period of time to assess and repair damage to the network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the flood alleviation plans her Department has in place around Enniskillen this coming winter.
answer_text: Officials from my Department’s Rivers Directorate carry out scheduled maintenance on the designated watercourses through Enniskillen Town on a cyclical basis. Urban reaches are inspected and maintained on an annual basis. Rural reaches and inspected and maintained at least once in every 6 years. In addition, my Department operates an on-call duty and flooding officer rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year with staff on standby to respond to flooding. Routine cyclical inspections also take place on drainage infrastructure, the maintenance of these is usually increased before and during significant periods of heavy rainfall. Sandbag stores at operational depots, and those at more remote locations close to communities at known flood risk, are fully stocked in preparation for any potential flooding. If the threat for any potential flooding increases, sandbags can be placed on mobile units to enable a timely response as necessary. My officials have a local plan in place if Derrychara Link Road in Enniskillen town becomes flooded due to high Lough levels.Additionally, my Department’s Rivers Directorate Operations staff maintain the water level in the Upper Lough, as far as weather conditions permit, through the management of the control structure downstream on the inter Lough channel at Portora, Enniskillen. Rivers Directorate operational staff are in regular contact with ESB representatives, and this coordinated approach ensures that water levels in Lough Erne are maintained within specified levels as agreed under the terms of an agreement made in 1950.The water levels in relation to Enniskillen Town can be viewed at the following link: https://www.hydrometcloud.de/Rivers_Agency/index.jsp?menu=index. Within the area of Upper Lough Erne, my Department along with multi-agency partners, has developed a specific plan for when water levels rise and agreed thresholds are reached on Upper Lough Erne. This plan has been developed through the Flooding and Severe Weather Group, which is chaired by my Department, and assesses the risks and potential impacts of water levels on low lying roads in the locality around Lough Erne.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of storm preparedness measures within his Department to ensure rotas can still be filled when existing staff were unable to get to their place of work due to Storm Amy.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her Department's assessment of the blockage at the entrance of Colebrooke River where it meets Lough Erne not being cleared in anticipation of the winter storm season.
answer_text: With reference to my Department’s correspondence with you on this matter (CORR-0559-2025 refers), the Colebrook River is part of a natural river system where material within the river will move as part of those natural hydromorphological processes. This natural conveyance of material includes the moving of increased volumes of sediment during higher flows of water within the river. Sediment will be deposited when flows dissipate, only to be moved on again when the river flows and water levels dictate, progressing through the natural system. This is also the case where one watercourse flows into a larger water body such as you describe where the Colebrooke discharges to Lough Erne. This natural conveyance of water and sediment can also involve out of bank flooding across the floodplain where the deposited material can replenish nutrients in the surrounding fields.Following a recent inspection, my engineers have advised that there are no such concerns where the river enters the Lough and that any naturally accumulated material is not impeding flow from the Colebrooke River to Lough Erne.It is important to highlight that the regulatory and environmental landscape has evolved significantly over the last number of years. There is now a greater environmental scrutiny and stricter compliance requirements for in-channel works, particularly in ecologically sensitive areas such as Lough Erne where the Colebrooke River discharges. This is particularly the case in relation to blue-green algae.My Department remains fully committed to the ongoing scheduled inspection and maintenance of the Colebrooke River. Should any action be deemed necessary in future, it will be considered in line with our statutory responsibilities and environmental obligations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail flood prevention measures her Department has prepared in regard to Storm Amy.
answer_text: Storm Amy was a multi-weather event with warnings issued by the Met Office for both wind and rain and as such preparations began at the earliest opportunity with multi-agency co-ordination taking place ahead of the weekend, well in advance of the storm arriving on Friday and prior to the severe weather warnings coming into effect.My Department has well established protocols in place for the response to severe weather events, including flooding. These emergency plans and processes are continually evaluated and developed throughout the year. The unpredictable nature of weather events means heavy rainfall can occur at any time and as winter arrives there can be an increased risk of adverse weather which could lead to flooding conditions e.g. during Storm Amy. In preparation the Department operates an on-call Duty and Flooding Officer Rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year. Additionally, and in preparation for and during flood events, local staff will utilise available resources to support our 50 community resilience groups in flood risk areas and respond, where possible, to requests for assistance where property has flooded or is threatened by flooding. Departmental depots are fully stocked with sandbags for temporary flood protection which will be distributed directly when required.Prior to the storm proactive culvert screen maintenance work was undertaken by staff with this work continuing right through the weekend. This work, in addition to existing flood alleviation schemes, prevented flooding in many areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the flood alleviation plans her Department has in place for the Colebrooke River, this coming winter.
answer_text: Officials from my Department’s Rivers Directorate carry out scheduled maintenance on the designated reaches of the Colebrooke River on a cyclical basis. There are 6 designated reaches of the Colebrooke River. One reach is inspected and maintained on an annual basis. The other 5 reaches are inspected and maintained every 6 years with the last inspection taking place on 23 October 2023 with the subsequent maintenance works completed between April and May 2024.In addition, my Department operates an on-call duty and flooding officer rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year with staff on standby to respond to flooding. Routine cyclical inspections also take place on drainage infrastructure, the maintenance of these is usually increased before and during significant periods of heavy rainfall. Sandbag stores at operational depots, and those at more remote locations close to communities at known flood risk, are fully stocked in preparation for any potential flooding. If the potential threat for any flooding increases, sandbags can be placed on mobile units to enable a timely response as necessary.My Department also has a water level station sited on Upper Lough Erne at Bellisle to which the Colebrooke River discharges into. The water level can be viewed at the following link: https://www.hydrometcloud.de/Rivers_Agency/index.jsp?menu=index. Within the area of Upper Lough Erne, my Department along with multi-agency partners, has developed a specific plan for when water levels rise and agreed thresholds are reached on Upper Lough Erne. This plan has been developed through the Flooding and Severe Weather Group, which is chaired by my Department, and assesses the risks and potential impacts of water levels on low lying roads in the locality around Lough Erne. In addition, and through the work of the Regional Community Flood Resilience Group, there has been engagement with the Upper Lough Erne community group to help them prepare for and respond to weather related emergencies, and to provide co-ordinated multi-agency support to individual properties or communities that may be cut off by flood water and require access to essential services. Through this community resilience work my Department seeks to provide an extra layer of support in addition to the normal response for those who may be impacted by flooding in periods of severe weather.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will put new road markings on the Drummeer Road, Enniskillen.
answer_text: My Department has recently awarded a new road marking contract for the County Fermanagh area. The renewal of road markings in this area is being completed on a priority basis commencing with the trunk road network. Drummeer Road is included on the current schedule for completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any ongoing works to identify exemptions for hospitality workers in her Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill
answer_text: As work continues on the Employment Rights Bill, my Department has been engaging with representatives of the hospitality sector to better understand their particular circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any progress made to date on business rates reform.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department is currently implementing a process of work for the rating system under the Strategic Roadmap for Rating announced on 9 December 2024. As part of that work for 2025/26, the Department last month concluded two Strategic Reviews for Non-Domestic Vacant Rating (NDVR) and Small Business Rate Relief (SBRR). This extensive work was completed over summer recess and within the stated deadlines. I am now evaluating the Review outcomes with a view to presenting final proposals to the Executive for consideration in the time ahead. The next steps will include implementing any agreed changes that can be made by April 2026 where feasible, with final decisions on rating policy to be made collectively by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the construction of Lisnaskea Health and Care Centre.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions his Department has had with the UK Government regarding the Creative Places Growth Fund, and whether this could benefit Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Creative Places Growth Fund is a Department for Culture, Media and Sport initiative specific to regions in England and will not see any direct benefits to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the number of people employed by the Assembly Commission on a (i) temporary contract; and (ii) agency contract.
answer_text: As at 1 October 2025, there were 2 staff members on a fixed term contract under the IS Scholarship scheme, and 43.5 Agency Workers placed on assignment with the Assembly Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the new UK Town of Culture competition announced by the Department for Culture, Media and Sport, whether his Department will be assisting with its rollout in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Town of Culture competition will be organised and delivered on a UK-wide basis by the Department for Culture, Media and Sport.  Details of the new initiative are not yet available.At this stage my Department has not been asked to provide assistance with the rollout of the Town of Culture competition but it stands ready to help where it can.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the flooding alleviation plans her Department has in place for Ballinamallard River, this coming winter.
answer_text: Officials from my Department’s Rivers Directorate carry out scheduled maintenance on the designated watercourses in the Ballinamallard area on a cyclical basis. The urban reaches of the designated Ballinamallard watercourses are inspected and maintained annually. The last inspection and works were carried out in October 2024. Rural reaches are inspected and maintained every year with the most recent inspections and maintenance works taking place in April 2025 and August 2025. In addition, my Department operates an on-call duty and flooding officer rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year with staff on standby to respond to flooding. Routine cyclical inspections also take place on drainage infrastructure, the maintenance of these is usually increased before and during significant periods of heavy rainfall. My Department recently installed a remote camera on a high-risk inlet screen located at the rear of properties in Drumkeen Court, Ballinamallard. Sandbag stores at operational depots, and those at more remote locations close to communities at known flood risk, are fully stocked in preparation for any potential flooding. If the threat for any potential flooding increases, sandbags can be placed on mobile units to enable a timely response as necessary.My Department also has a water level station sited on the Ballycassidy River, which the Ballinamallard River flows into. The water level can be viewed at the following link: https://www.hydrometcloud.de/Rivers_Agency/index.jsp?menu=index.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has filled the 41 vacancies at the his Department's offices at Inishkeen House, Enniskillen.
answer_text: As the Member will appreciate, vacancy management is by its nature fluid and numbers can change from month to month as vacancies are filled and new vacancies arise.  I am advised that, at 30 September 2025, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 37 vacancies for the DAERA office in Inishkeen House, Enniskillen. DAERA’s vacancies are, like those in other departments, filled in accordance with NICS policies and procedures and the Department of Finance’s NICSHR teams work with all departments on an ongoing basis to prioritise recruitment across all NICS departments within their allocated financial and staff resources.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has filled the 15 vacancies at the DAERA office in Dungannon.
answer_text: As the Member will appreciate, vacancy management is by its nature fluid and numbers can change from month to month as vacancies are filled and new vacancies arise.  I am advised that, at 30 September 2025, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 17 vacancies for the DAERA office in Dungannon.DAERA’s vacancies are, like those in other departments, filled in accordance with NICS policies and procedures and the Department of Finance’s NICSHR teams work with all departments on an ongoing basis to prioritise recruitment across all NICS departments within their allocated financial and staff resources.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Revised Operational Protocol.
answer_text: My officials are considering the responses to the consultation on the Strategic Environmental Assessment Report for the Ammonia Strategy and Operational Protocol.  This will inform the final version of the Revised Operational Protocol.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has filled the ten vacancies at the DfI Rivers Directorate in Ballinamallard.
answer_text: Responsibility for all recruitment within the NICS rests with their Human Resource team, and we work closely with them to fill vacancies across a range of disciplines.Interviews for an Industrial River Worker recruitment competition were held in September 2025. NICS HR are also progressing a recruitment competition at the P&T grade. Vacancies for both these grades at the Ballinamallard Depot will be filled as NICS HR progress the remaining stages of these recruitment competitions.  The District Foreperson vacancy is currently being filled by means of a Temporary Promotion pending the launch of a recruitment competition and the appointment of a successful candidate to the role.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will consider a digital at home Deposit Return Scheme.
answer_text: The scheme administrator, UK Deposit Management Organisation Ltd. (UK DMO) is responsible for delivering the Deposit Return Scheme (DRS) for single-use plastic and metal drinks containers in England, Northern Ireland and Scotland. This includes designing and delivering the scheme’s infrastructure. 	Through our regular industry engagement and international learnings from other schemes, there has been a broad consensus that a return to retail model is the most effective way to launch a DRS for drinks containers across the UK.However, my Department recognises the potential benefits of a digital at home model and I value the work previously done to test feasibility.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what support his Department is offering the South Eastern Health and Social Trust in their roll out of Aquablation Therapy to help address regional waiting lists.
answer_text: The South Eastern Trust urology service began delivering aquablation in May 2025. While the initial focus has been developing a local service for patients, the plan is to expand the service to ensure that all clinically appropriate patients can avail of the aquablation service. The aquablation regional referral criteria is being finalised and good progress is being made embedding the service in the Trust.  Larger glands are being incrementally added to the case mix, with the expectation that it will take four to five months to fully establish a regional service for patients with prostates up to 200cc.  Unfortunately, the South Eastern Trust urology team has faced workforce issues over the last three months and this has slowed the progress to roll out the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his engagements with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs regarding the ongoing spread of bluetongue in GB, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: I have engaged with Defra Ministers on a number of occasions in the past 12 months to discuss a wide range of issues, which includes the risk to these islands of animal disease incursions such as bluetongue (BTV). 		In addition, my officials monitor the situation closely and continue ongoing collaboration and engagement with colleagues from devolved administrations on the spread of BTV in Great Britain.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with Co-operation Ireland in relation to the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund.
answer_text: Co-operation Ireland was appointed on 12 September 2025 as the Intermediary Funding Body for the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund. Departmental officials have engaged regularly with Co-Operation Ireland throughout the development of this Fund and will continue to work closely with them throughout the delivery phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of potential energy savings expected from the roll-out of Smart Meters.
answer_text: Better information, through smart meters, will allow consumers to choose the best tariffs and services for their needs, to take control of their energy usage and better manage their electricity costs. Our economy will benefit by promoting more efficient energy consumption, reducing the cost of providing clean energy, and facilitating the transition to renewable energy sources.  System benefits include identifying faults more easily, more effective planning and operation of the electricity grid, including the reduction of carbon emissions through better use of renewable electricity and low carbon technologies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department supports employers, such as assistance with insurance costs, when taking on an apprentice.
answer_text: Whilst there is no specific funding available towards insurance costs for employers who take on apprentices, my Department fully funds the directed, off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship, investing over £45 million annually across the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes. This funding ensures that employers are not financially liable for the delivery of apprenticeship training. Businesses are also eligible for an employer incentive payment upon the apprentice’s successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework, ranging from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area. This incentive is designed to assist employers with the broader costs of supporting and mentoring apprentices.  Employers in the north are also exempt from paying Class 1 National Insurance contributions for apprentices under the age of 25, subject to certain conditions being met.  I want to help more employers and individuals benefit from our apprenticeship model.  I recently published an Apprenticeship Action Plan which aims to ensure that apprenticeship pathways bring real value to businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any targeted measures her Department is providing to support young people seeking a mechanical apprenticeship.
answer_text: My Department does not target specific apprenticeships.  Instead, a biannual marketing campaign operates to promote apprenticeships in all sectors.  This is supported by an annual NI Apprenticeship Week which acts to promote all available apprenticeship provision for prospective apprentices and to highlight opportunities on behalf of employers.Wider sector awareness is provided by my Department’s Careers Service through provision of face-to-face advice, guidance and careers information across the North of Ireland to clients of all ages. The Careers Occupational Unit has published a series of bulletins in a range of areas including one on Engineering and Advanced Manufacturing careers.  These bulletins provide an overview of career opportunities, including apprenticeships, and offer key information on entry routes and careers progression.The Level 2 and Level 3 Engineering apprenticeship frameworks are among the most popular apprenticeships in terms of participation numbers and include a pathway on ‘mechanical manufacturing engineering’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any targeted measures her Department is providing to support young people seeking an electrical apprenticeship.
answer_text: My Department does not target specific apprenticeships.  Instead, a biannual marketing campaign operates to promote apprenticeships in all sectors.  This is supported by an annual NI Apprenticeship Week which acts to promote all available apprenticeship provision for prospective apprentices and to highlight opportunities on behalf of employers. Wider sector awareness is provided by my Department’s Careers Service through provision of face-to-face advice, guidance and careers information across the North of Ireland to clients of all ages. The Careers Occupational Unit has published a series of bulletins in a range of areas including one on careers within Construction and the Built Environment, with specific reference to electrical-related trades.  These bulletins provide an overview of career opportunities, including apprenticeships, and offer key information on entry routes and careers progression. The Level 3 Electrotechnical apprenticeship framework is one of the three most popular apprenticeships in terms of participation numbers.
