To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline whether his Department will carry out works to update the fishing stands at Corranny Lough, Rosslea.
answer_text: DAERA Inland fisheries staff are scheduled to visit Corranny Lough during the first week of October to carry out work identified in the most recent inspection report.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the September 2023 University of Oxford journals (i) Global Change Biology - The combined effects of treated sewage discharge and land use on rivers; and (ii) Ecological Solutions and Evidence - Early detection and environmental drivers of sewage fungus outbreaks in rivers, that revealed sewage discharge into rivers has a greater impact on water quality and the animals and plants that live in rivers, than surrounding land use.
answer_text: y officials in the Chief Scientific Adviser’s Office have reviewed these papers and note the findings. The study took place on four unnamed English rivers, with samples taken in August, October and November, at sample points 5-10m upstream and downstream of sewage treatment discharge points. The limited number of the sample sites, infrequency of sampling, and proximity of sample points to treated sewage discharge points, make it difficult to draw conclusions on the relative impact of sewage discharges and land use in these English rivers. The relative impacts of agriculture, wastewater, septic tanks and other sources of pollution will vary between waterbodies, so it is important to note the findings of this University of Oxford research do not apply to all lakes or rivers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment on how the environmental damage caused by preparatory works removing trees, hedges and wild meadows for the A5 Western Transport Corridor Scheme aligns with his Department's aim to protect natural habits and the environment.
answer_text: There are a range of statutory procedures that must be followed before a road scheme can be constructed in Northern Ireland. They include various assessments to understand the potential impacts of the proposed scheme on local environments and habitats, as well as how these impacts can be reduced, removed or mitigated.		An Environmental Statement was produced for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.  I am advised that the compensatory measures included within the scheme are sufficient to ensure no overall loss of trees, hedges or wild meadows.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department is supporting farm businesses to improve their benchmarking performance, year on year.
answer_text: DAERA supports farm businesses to improve their benchmarking performance, year on year through a dedicated benchmarking portal. This support is delivered as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) Knowledge schemes. Farm business members or their authorised agents can use the online Benchmarking Application to record their farm business performance data and in return can access a comprehensive report detailing a series of Key Performance Indicators (KPI’s). These KPI’s can be used by the farm business to compare their performance year on year and with industry averages. The Knowledge schemes use benchmarking as an important tool in assisting farm businesses to improve performance and are supported by a dedicated adviser/facilitator.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on when stocking will take place in Corranny Lough, Rosslea.
answer_text: DAERA intends to recommence fish stocking of Public Angling Estate waters during the Autumn/Winter period of 2025/2026 providing the conditions allow to be compliant with the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 with respect to invasive species, namely zebra mussels. The department provides no advance notification of stocking, but details will be available on the DAERA website once stocking events take place.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the emergency plans her Department has in place for flooding this coming winter in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone, particularly the Fermanagh Lakelands.
answer_text: My Department is the Lead Government Department for the strategic co-ordination of the emergency response to significant flooding, and my officials have worked with partner organisations to develop a multi-agency severe weather plan. This plan details the pre-planned arrangements for a coordinated multi-agency response as a result of a warning for severe weather. The Department’s preparations for the response to severe weather, including flooding, is an ongoing process throughout the year. The unpredictable nature of weather events means heavy rainfall can occur at any time and as winter arrives there can be an increased risk of adverse weather which could lead to flooding conditions. Within the area of the Fermanagh Lakelands my Department, along with multi-agency partners, has developed a specific plan for when water levels rise and agreed thresholds are reached on Upper Lough Erne. This plan has been developed through the Flooding and Severe Weather Group, which is chaired by my Department, and assesses the risks and potential impacts of water levels on low lying roads in the locality around Lough Erne. In addition, and through the work of the Regional Community Flood Resilience Group, there has been engagement with the local Boho community group to help them prepare for and respond to weather related emergencies, and to provide co-ordinated multi-agency support to individual properties or communities that may be cut off by flood water and require access to essential services. Through this community resilience work my Department seeks to provide an extra layer of support in addition to the normal response for those who may be impacted by flooding in periods of severe weather. In addition, my Department operates an on-call duty and flooding officer rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year with staff on standby to respond to flooding. Routine cyclical inspections also take place on drainage infrastructure, the maintenance of these is usually increased before and during significant periods of heavy rainfall. Sandbag stores at operational depots, and those at more remote locations close to communities at known flood risk, are fully stocked in preparation for any potential flooding. In addition, as the threat for any potential flooding increases, sandbags can be placed on mobile units to enable a timely response as necessary.Additionally, my Department’s Rivers Directorate Operations staff maintain the water level in the Upper Lough, as far as weather conditions permit, through the management of the control structure downstream on the inter Lough channel at Portora, Enniskillen. Rivers Directorate operational staff are in regular contact with ESB representatives, and this coordinated approach ensures that water levels in Lough Erne are maintained within specified levels as agreed under the terms of an agreement made in 1950.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of driving licenses revoked in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, due to medical reasons, in the last five years.
answer_text: From 01 January 2020 to 31 December 2024, there were 113 driving licences revoked and a further 207 driving licence applications refused on medical grounds within the Fermanagh and South Tyrone parliamentary constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of the original 2,128 systems, accredited in 2017 by the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme, that received greater than £500, in each financial year, since 2017-18.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail all of the support mechanisms her Department offers to Manufacturing and Engineering Growth and Advancement in supporting its work.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy (DfE), working in close partnership with Invest NI, has provided a comprehensive suite of financial and non-financial support to the MEGA network, an industry-led collaborative initiative based in Mid Ulster, representing over 70 companies in the engineering and manufacturing sector.Financial support FundingAmountYearCollaborative Growth Programme (CGP)£166,000£170,000£14,800 (post Covid Recovery)201820222021Cluster Acceleration Fund (CAP)£55,0002025Non-financial support provided to MEGA includes facilitating network representatives to participate in an Invest NI-led study visit to the Basque Region (29 Sept–3 Oct), including the opportunity to engage with the internationally renowned Basque Advanced and Digital Manufacturing Cluster for the purposes of benchmarking best practices.In addition, all SME members of MEGA have had access to broader Invest NI support, including those focused on Supply Chain and Operational Excellence. They have also benefited from the wider subject-matter expertise of the Cluster Acceleration Programme team, including the opportunity to participate in networking events, workshops and cluster-focused conferences including a speaking role at the 2025 Global TCI Conference (The Competitiveness Institute), being hosted in Dublin from October 14 – 16.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the flooding alleviation plans her Department has in place for Cappy mill race, Lisbellaw, this coming winter.
answer_text: Officials from my Department’s Rivers Directorate carry out scheduled maintenance on the Cappy Mill Race on a cyclical basis. The urban reach of the Cappy Mill Race watercourse is inspected and maintained annually. The last inspection was on 11 April 2025 with the identified maintenance works carried out in April 2025. Rural reaches are inspected and maintained every 4 years with the last inspection taking place on 19 November 2021 and subsequent maintenance works completed in September 2022. This reach of the watercourse is due for inspection again this financial year. In addition, my Department operates an on-call duty and flooding officer rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year with staff on standby to respond to flooding. Routine cyclical inspections also take place on drainage infrastructure, the maintenance of these is usually increased before and during significant periods of heavy rainfall. Sandbag stores at operational depots, and those at more remote locations close to communities at known flood risk, are fully stocked in preparation for any potential flooding, two of which are in the nearby towns of Lisnaskea and Derrylin. If the threat for any potential flooding increases, sandbags can be placed on mobile units to enable a timely response as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Renewable Electricity Support Scheme meeting the target of 80 per cent of electricity consumption coming from renewable sources by 2030, given issues with (i) land availability; (ii) planning constraints; and (iii) grid connection capacity.
answer_text: Delivering additional renewable electricity generation to meet the 80% target is complex, demanding a careful balance between security of supply, affordability, and legislative compliance. It requires joined-up working, across government and with our delivery partners. My Department does not hold all the levers, so collaboration is essential to unlock progress. In planning for more renewable deployment in the future, my Department is committed to delivering the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee which is a critical milestone on our path to meet our climate change targets. I am confident that we are building the right foundations to ensure the scheme delivers the best outcomes for energy consumers and that local communities benefit from the transition to net zero. My Department has also worked closely with the Utility Regulator to enable the largest investment in our electricity grid through RP7, an enabler for connecting new renewable generation. I acknowledge the need for a more ambitious and responsive regional planning framework. A revised Strategic Planning Policy Statement on Renewable and Low Carbon Energy will be critical to enabling timely deployment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of blockages (i) reported on watercourses; and (ii) removed from watercourses by her Department, across Fermanagh and South Tyrone, over the last five years.
answer_text: My Department’s statutory powers under the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 include, on a permissive basis, for the maintenance of designated watercourses to allow the free flow of water. As such, Rivers Directorate remove blockages where they are causing a significant drainage impediment within the watercourse. In the last five years my officials estimate that over 1,000 blockages were removed from watercourses across the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions her Department is having with the Utility Regulator to offer environmental exemptions to the operators of Kilroot power station.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer to my statement of 1 October 2025, providing an update on the electricity supply for the North.Department for the Economy - UPDATE ON ELECTRICITY SUPPLY

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the rationale for her Department not revising the entry point for the Renewable Electricity Support Scheme from 5MW to 1MW.
answer_text: My Department’s rationale on eligibility criteria, such as minimum capacity requirements, for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG) is detailed in the Final Scheme Design, published on 19 September 2025.While the first auction will focus on projects of 5MW or above, I recognise the contribution that the distributed generation sector has made to our energy needs and am committed to supporting renewable generation from as diverse a mix of renewable sources as possible. Options for support for smaller scale generation will therefore continue to be evaluated ahead of future auctions and as part of our net zero ambitions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the specific technical barriers preventing the inclusion of over 1MW projects in the Renewable Electricity Support Scheme auction; and (ii) how her Department is working with SONI and NIE Networks to overcome these.
answer_text: My Department’s rationale on eligibility criteria, such as minimum capacity requirements, for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG) is detailed in the Final Scheme Design, published on 19 September 2025. While the first auction will focus on projects of 5MW or above, I recognise the contribution that the distributed generation sector has made to our energy needs and am committed to supporting renewable generation from as diverse a mix of renewable sources as possible. Options for support for smaller scale generation will therefore continue to be evaluated ahead of future auctions and as part of our net zero ambitions. 	Delivering additional renewable electricity generation is complex, demanding a careful balance between security of supply, affordability, and legislative compliance. Collaboration with key delivery partners, such as SONI and NIE Networks, has been an integral part of development to date and will remain pivotal to the success of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the progress of the Mid South West Growth Deal.
answer_text: Following the agreement of Heads of Terms for the Growth Deal in November 2024, officials in my Department, Tourism NI and Invest NI have continued to support the Councils and their partners to further develop projects with the aim of achieving Financial Deal signing by the end of 2026.The Mid South West Growth Deal represents a significant opportunity to support economic growth across the region.  It will make a significant contribution to my economic vision, by promoting regional balance, creating good jobs, increasing productivity and delivering a substantial industrial decarbonisation programme for the Region. My Department is committed to continued co-design and partnership working to ensure timely delivery of the Deal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the negative impact the Rates Support Grant Budget 2025-26 will have on local government authorities.
answer_text: The funding position for my Department remains constrained for 2025/26, as was the case for the previous financial year. The Rates Support Grant (RSG) budget for 2025/26 is at the same level as 2024/25 at £3.124m. The impact of the Rates Support Grant Budget 2025-26 allocation is a matter for councils to consider in the context of income received from other funding streams, including the district rate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the financial sustainability of local government funding for the forthcoming 2026-27 financial year.
answer_text: As local Councils in Northern Ireland are bodies corporate, and autonomous in nature, they are responsible for setting their own budgets/estimates each year, based on their projected income from funding streams including district rates, service fees and charges and grants from Central Government. 		Council budgets for the 2026/27 financial year have not yet been set.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will appointment an individual with a business background from the South West Region to the board of Invest NI to promote regional balance, following the recent resignation of a board member.
answer_text: My Department is committed to the principles of public appointments based on merit with independent assessment, openness and transparency of process. My Department is also committed to equality of opportunity and welcomes applications from all suitably qualified applicants irrespective of religious belief, gender, race, political opinion, age, disability, marital status, sexual orientation, or whether or not they have dependents. A public appointment competition for new members to the Invest NI Board will launch in October. Appointments will be made on merit from those individuals who best satisfy the selection criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Desertcreat site, as part of the Mid South West Growth Deal.
answer_text: Plans for the development of a new business park focused on green innovation and sustainable economic growth on lands at Desertcreat are progressing well. The site is a key strategic component of the Mid South West Growth Deal and is earmarked for the creation of a Green Innovation Park.Funding for the project is being provided through the Growth Deal and the next key milestone is an Outline Business Case. Invest NI is working in partnership with the Strategic Investment Board on the delivery of the OBC, which is scheduled for completion by late March 2026. While this is an ambitious and challenging target, Invest NI and partners are currently on course to meet this deadline.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the role of ward clerks following the roll-out of encompass.
answer_text: HSC Trusts have confirmed that ward clerks continue to be an important and valued role within the health service.  Some Trusts have undertaken assessments aimed at identifying any changes to ward clerk duties; the level of capacity required for these duties; and any additional work that the ward clerks could undertake to assist the clinical teams, commensurate to their banding and their training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the progress to date on the full implementation of the Independent Review of Invest Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As of 1 September 2025, 28 of the 35 Strategic Actions within the Invest NI Action Plan, have been implemented. The remaining seven Strategic Actions are due for completion in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will review the peripherality scoring for Local Enterprise Partnerships, given that Fermanagh and Omagh District Council and Mid Ulster District Council are categorised the same as Londonderry and Strabane District Council, in the scoring matrix.
answer_text: Peripherality is a contextual measure in the funding formula for Local Economic Partnerships to ensure that funding takes into account the spatial context. The OECD Regional Typology classification systems provides two ways of looking at this, a degree of urbanisation within an area and one based on metropolitan / remote area status. These have been used in tandem to produce a composite measure of council area peripherality where council areas are deemed to be peripheral or non-peripheral based on both rurality and whether they are part of (or have access to) a large, functional metropolitan area. While we considered the merits of greater differentiation between peripheral areas, departmental analysts advised that this would not be possible using the existing OECD classification system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department is supporting job creation in the South West Region.
answer_text: In the context of the relatively low levels of unemployment across the North, including the South West Region, the focus of my Department is on the creation of Good Jobs. Aligned to my regional balance priority, Invest NI is implementing a new regional approach where it is working closely with a range of stakeholders to promote entrepreneurship, boost business investment, support more businesses to look at export markets and accelerate investments in innovation – all of which contribute to job creation.In support of this new regional approach, Invest NI has set the ambitious target to increase the proportion of investments outside of the Belfast Metropolitan Area to 65% by the end of its strategy period, in 2026-27. My Department has also established Local Economic Partnerships in each Council area and we are working with the range of stakeholders across the North, including the South West region, through these Local Economic Partnerships. To ensure there is full engagement and involvement by local stakeholders, my department is providing a dedicated fund of £45m across the 11 Council areas. The Local Economic Partnerships will identify the specific needs and interventions required to aid the economic development of each area, which will contribute to the creation of good jobs in the South West region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the return on investment on the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.
answer_text: The Enniskillen Southern Bypass is a strategically important scheme for my Department and will provide significant benefits to the town and its surrounding road network. The scheme has a Benefit to Cost Ratio (BCR) of 1.85, which represents a return of £1.85 for every £1 invested.  The scheme is also expected to reduce the strategic traffic movements through Enniskillen town centre; improving journey time reliability; enhancing road safety and reducing greenhouse gas emissions when compared against the existing local network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the work being undertaken to identify barriers and proposals to create jobs across the (i) Fermanagh and Omagh; and (ii) Mid Ulster, Local Economic Partnerships.
answer_text: The Fermanagh and Omagh and Mid Ulster Local Economic Partnerships are working on Action Plans to address economic challenges in their local areas, availing of their allocations from the £45m Regional Balance Fund. Both Partnerships are at an early stage in this work, however my Department and Invest NI are working closely with them to progress at pace to achieve this important milestone.  Initial indications are that the Action Plans will include projects that support creation of high-quality jobs, and each Partnership will have the ability to prioritise actions to meet the identified needs of their local area. I look forward to the Fermanagh and Omagh and Mid Ulster Partnerships finalising their Action Plans in coming months and then moving forward with delivery that benefits everyone.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department is supporting the expansion of the Acute Care at Home Service in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A review of Intermediate Care services led by the Chief Nursing Officer was undertaken across 2023 and early 2024 and included the core function of Hospital at Home provision. A project report has been developed, and has been agreed with a range of recommendations to be taken forward in partnership with my department and Executive Service Leads across all geographical Trusts in the next phase of this work.An Intermediate Care Steering Group has been established in September 2025 to take forward transformation of Intermediate Care Services and a new Intermediate Care Hospital at Home Regional Working Group agreed. The main purpose of the working group is to standardise the provision of Hospital at Home services across the region, including the Western Health and Social Care Trust, ensuring equity of access, maximising rehabilitation opportunities and reducing or delaying reliance on longer term services for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff employed at the South West Acute Hospital currently on temporary promotion, broken down by department.
answer_text: Number of temporary promotions / acting ups in SWAHDirectorateTemporary Promotions/Acting Ups in SWAHAdult Mental Health & Disability Services4Children & Families 4Community & Older People's Services8Nursing, Midwifery & Allied Health Professions11Surgery, Paediatrics & Women's Health 15Unscheduled Care, Medicine, Cancer & Clinical Services32Corporate Services10Total84

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of boosting renewable energy capacity in the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area.
answer_text: The Final Scheme Design for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG) outlines how the scheme will increase investment in renewables and ensure that local communities benefit from the transition to net zero. Delivering additional renewable electricity generation is complex, demanding a careful balance between security of supply, affordability, and legislative compliance.It requires joined-up working, across government and with our delivery partners. My Department does not hold all the levers, so collaboration is essential to unlock progress.The Energy Strategy Action Plan 2025 outlines a range of actions my Department is taking forward this year to increase the levels of renewable electricity generation across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline when he plans to lift the recruitment freeze on new nursing staff in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Individual HSC Trusts, as the employer, are responsible for recruitment across HSC. There is currently no recruitment freeze on new nursing staff in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.It is worth acknowledging the significant progress which has been made in recent years to stabilise and grow the HSC workforce.  Since my Department published the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy in 2018, there has been a 17.7% increase in whole time equivalent staff directly employed across the HSC.  That amounts to 10,062 additional whole time equivalent staff across the HSC, including 1,019 (+24.3%) more medical and dental staff, 2,938 (+19.4%) more registered nurses and midwives and 2,026 (+24.9%) more professional and technical staff. For the Western HSC Trust this has meant an overall increase in whole time equivalent staff of 1,334 (+14.8%) since 2018 including 525 (19.5%) more registered nurses and midwives.These increases in staffing numbers have been supported by the strategic approach to workforce development outlined in the Workforce Strategy including strategic workforce planning, increased commissioning of pre- and post-registration training, better attraction and recruitment of staff, reducing agency dependency and developing initiatives to improve staff retention.  While we have made important strides in stabilising and growing our workforce, the positive impact of additional staffing is not felt uniformly across the HSC with some key workforce challenges in particular services and locations.  My Department is committed to addressing these challenges through continued implementation of the Workforce Strategy and will shortly publish the Strategy’s third action plan which will outline priorities identified for progression over the next three years.  Continued implementation of the Workforce Strategy will require sustained and increased funding to ensure we provide the investment we know is required to meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what support her Department is providing to the provision of data centres, to help grow the digital economy.
answer_text: While no direct financial support has been offered to date for the establishment of data centres in the North, my Department, working closely with Invest NI, recognises the increasingly important role that data centres can play in driving economic growth and supporting innovation. Data centres are fundamental to underpinning a modern digital infrastructure, which is essential for a thriving digital economy.Invest NI continues to review proposals for data centre developments, carefully balancing these opportunities against wider strategic, economic, and environmental considerations. The requirements for data centres are complex, particularly in relation to infrastructure such as sites, water, electricity, and renewable energy - resources that are also in high demand from our priority manufacturing sector.Nonetheless, Invest NI acknowledges the critical role of data centres in enabling digitalisation, supporting technology services, and facilitating advancements in areas such as cloud computing and artificial intelligence. My Department remains committed to working with potential providers and stakeholders to ensure that any future data centre developments align with our broader economic and environmental objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to improve the provision of domiciliary carers in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust has given assurances to my Department that they have fully deployed all resources available for the provision of domiciliary care services.  At present the Trust provides domiciliary care services to 3643 individuals totalling approximately 42,000 weekly hours of care which equates to 100,000 weekly homecare visits.   Despite this level of service deployed the Trust does have a level of home care unmet need. This is due to a lack of capacity in particular geographical areas at the time.  At the end of August 2025 there were 338 individuals waiting for a full care package and 341 waiting for part of their package.The Western Trust has recently completed a significant transformation project ‘Homecare Optimisation’ to optimise the service capacity available.  This initiative enabled 738 additional cases to be accommodated within existing capacity totalling 7611 weekly care hours.The Trust continues to work closely with the local homecare providers to ensure that the service is deployed effectively.  However, in keeping with the experiences of the other Trusts, homecare providers cite recruitment and retention challenges and rurality as the main barriers to developing additional capacity.As a regulated service all homecare providers that the Trust contracts with are subject to annual inspection from the regulatory body, RQIA.  Scrutiny of all recent inspection reports demonstrates that at the time of inspection services were safe, effective and of good quality.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his Department's assessment of zoning land to protect high quality farmland from provision of solar farms and woodland.
answer_text: Zoning of land is a matter for each local council as they take forward their Local Development Plans (LDPs). The development process involves public consultation to ensure that LDPs balance community needs with the protection of important environmental and landscape features. Consideration and granting of permissions for solar farms on any land is subject to statutory planning processes. In relation to planting woodland on farmland, the Department’s Forest Service administers grant schemes which apply to all land categories. Approvals are subject to the protection afforded through environmentally designated areas and considerations under environmental impact assessment regulations. These requirements apply to the creation of woodlands regardless of quality of land proposed for planting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement his Department is having with the UK Civil Service, around making it easier for secondments to the Northern Ireland Civil Service from the UK Civil Service.
answer_text: Our Civil Service Secondment Policy provides for inward and outward secondments to or from other employers, including the Civil Service, for a period of up to three years with the potential of extension up to five years. The policy is currently under review.Since October 2024, the Civil Service here has participated in an Intergovernmental Placements Pilot to encourage the exchange of knowledge and skills through the use of intergovernmental secondments.Learning from the pilot is being used to inform the ongoing secondment policy review, the aim of which is to provide greater policy flexibility to support the use of secondments between other civil service jurisdictions, public sector organisations, and organisations in the private and third sectors.My Department continues to work to explore the potential for further secondment opportunities between civil service jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support his Department is offering the Western Health and Social Care Trust following the decision by North West Care not to extend their current contracts in the three Fermanagh lots impacting 330 service users.
answer_text: I am aware that the decision by North West Care not to extend their current contracts in the three Fermanagh lots from the 30th November 2025 will impact on 330 individuals and 100 staff.  My Department has been working with the Western Health and Social Care Trust in relation to what mitigations they have in place to cause least disruption to service users and staff.The two other incumbent providers in Fermanagh, ie: Care Plus and Lakeland Community Care are contractually obliged to take on North West Care’s service provision with affected staff to transfer under TUPE arrangements.  Affected service users would also in turn transfer to the other two providers (although not governed by TUPE).   Western Trust are working very closely with all three providers and have a phased implementation plan with one locality transferring this month, the second in mid-October and final one by mid-November to allow for bedding-in ahead of Christmas and New Year period.The concern always is that staff may seek other employment when they hear of the decision by North West Care.  However, the Western Trust have given assurance to my Department that staff have been introduced to their new employer very quickly after the announcement was made by North West Care.  My Department will continue to liaise with and provide support to the Western Trust requires until the process of moving across is completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her officials have sought the engagement of the Competition and Markets Authority to protect the position of the 800 independent small businesses contacted to support Pilgrim foods, an issue carefully articulated in the report by Buglass Energy Advisory Limited on the Northern Ireland Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive, Research into Hardship, commissioned by her Department.
answer_text: My officials have not engaged with the Competition and Markets Authority, however the consultation on closure proposals will be targeted at Scheme participants to seek their views directly, to ensure that the objective of fair and equal treatment is achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what support his Department can give to young people seeking to become involved in Superbike sports
answer_text: The responsibility for developing pathways into sport lies primarily with the recognised governing bodies, in this case 2&4 Wheel Motorsport Steering Group and Motorcycle Union of Ireland (Ulster Centre), who may be able to provide more information on how young people can get involved in Superbike sports as well as a list of local clubs.Sport NI have advised that alternative funding sources may be available. It may be beneficial to contact the Sports Development Officer at their local council who could provide information regarding local funding opportunities.In addition, the Mary Peters Trust help young people, both disabled and able bodied, achieve their sporting dreams and ambitions. Further information on applying for funding can be found at https://marypeterstrust.org/ and by contacting The Mary Peters Trust on 028 9068 6730 or via email, info@marypeterstrust.org.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on adult ADHD services.
answer_text: My Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what engagement his Department has had with the Rivers Trust regarding Lough Neagh.
answer_text: My Department has been working collectively over the last 2 years with local government and other stakeholders to deliver the Planning Improvement Programme which aims to enhance and make a tangible difference to the planning system in the North.  The next phase of the programme is underway, and it refocuses on specific areas of action and initiatives which best support the long-term sustainability of the system and improve overall performance. This phase focuses on evidence-based consideration of planning authority practices and procedures to help identify challenges, blockages and delays in the planning process. There has been constructive and ongoing engagement with the 12 local planning authorities, including observational visits to Planning Committee meetings to help inform opportunities for support, shared learning and best practice, with the aim of improving performance. Wider stakeholder engagement with all sectors is in place to understand the areas for key improvements in the planning system. Improvement measures also include completion of the review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 to consider the classes and thresholds for local, major, and regionally significant development.  This follows new legislation introduced to improve Pre-Application Community Consultation and Pre-Determination Hearings.Improving the effectiveness and efficiency of the Local Development Plan process remains a key priority for my Department, and officials are working jointly with councils on various tasks aimed at streamlining the plan preparation process.  Officials are also working closely with councils on enforcement practice, resourcing and prioritisation, in the context of environmental and legal obligations, and are scoping challenges and opportunities around increasing levels of cost recovery in the planning system.Work is progressing on the design and implementation of a regional trainee planner scheme (in collaboration with councils, Belfast Met and the RTPI NI) ensuring a focus on resourcing the system and succession planning for the future.The NI Planning Statistics 2024/25 show continued improvement in the planning system, with the average processing time for major applications falling to 39.6 weeks, the lowest since the transfer of powers, with five councils meeting the 30-week target.  Similarly, good progress through the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum has seen 78% of responses to statutory consultations submitted on time, in the first nine months of 2024/2025.  This reflects a trend of positive improvements in the planning system.The planning improvement agenda is an opportunity, as well as a collective responsibility, for all those involved in the process, to ensure that NI has a modern, fit for purpose planning system which delivers the right socio-economic and environmental outcomes for all our citizens.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why there has been delay in the delivery of the recommendations of the (i) 2019 Northern Ireland Affairs Select Committee inquiry report, Changes to the Northern Ireland Renewable Heat Incentive scheme payments; (ii) Buglass Energy Advisory Limited report on the Northern Ireland Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive, Research into Hardship; and (iii) February 2023 Court of Appeal judgement advocacy of a full, fair and final resolution, for the participants of the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive scheme, within months and not years.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges the importance of the recommendations made in the 2019 Northern Ireland Affairs Committee report, the Buglass Energy Advisory Limited hardship research, and the February 2023 Court of Appeal judgment. Each of these contributions has informed the Department’s ongoing work to deliver a managed closure that ensures fairness for participants in the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive (NDRHI) scheme. My Department is committed to progressing this matter as quickly as possible. The launch of a public consultation on the proposed closure of the Scheme is imminent. This will provide stakeholders with an opportunity to comment on my Department’s proposals and will inform the final policy and legislative approach. I will also bring forward legislation to uplift the tariff in advance of this winter, while closure arrangements are being finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her proposals to close the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: Following Executive approval on 18 September in relation to the Scheme, my Department will act swiftly to launch a public consultation and introduce the necessary legislation.Following a period of extensive engagement with stakeholders, this consultation will now bring forward the details of how the scheme will be closed in a manner which is fair to both the participants and the tax payer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether appointments to the Northern Ireland Tourism Board achieves her goal of regional balance across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The most recent appointment to the Tourism NI Board was in 2023.  All appointments were conducted in line with The Commissioner for Public Appointments for Northern Ireland (CPANI) guidance. The Board is committed to working closely with the Department to deliver on the Department's Tourism Vision and Action Plan. The Vision and Action Plan is structured around my four economic priorities including Regional Balance. Likewise, Tourism NI’s Operating Plan includes Regional Balance as one of its key objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rolling monthly average of Civil Servants (i) working from home; and (ii) working in the office, across all Civil Service estate property in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the response provided to AQW 15617/22-27.The review of the Civil Service Hybrid Working Policy has recently been completed. As part of the policy review, a new case management system has been developed, which, following policy implementation, will enable management information to be produced, including the number of staff operating hybrid working across departments, the number of in-office days and office location. The updated Hybrid Working Policy will be implemented across all departments from 1 September to 31 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Board of the Western Health and Social Care Trust fairly represents the entirety of the Western Health and Social Care Trust area in its representation.
answer_text: The Chairperson and Non-Executive Directors of HSC Trust Boards are appointed following a competition in line with the Code of Practice for Ministerial Public Appointments in Northern Ireland from the Office of the Commissioner for Public Appointments for Northern Ireland. All Executive appointments are based on merit and do not consider personal factors, such as whether they are geographically representative of the community the Trust serves. Whilst as Minister I would be keen that Boards are as representative as possible, making decisions on such considerations would not be permissible under current equality legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current occupancy rates of all Civil Service estate property across Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance (DoF) owns and leases a number of office buildings across Fermanagh and Tyrone for the use of Civil Service departments.  The Departments occupying the buildings have provided details of the number of staff based at each building at 31 March 2025 which is detailed in the table below.BuildingStaffCookstown Jobs & Benefits Office51DfI Rivers Headquarters, Cookstown1002nd Floor Unit 4, Molesworth Place, Cookstown22Dungannon Crown Buildings174Castle Barracks, Enniskillen28Enniskillen Crown Buildings72Inishkeen House, Enniskillen159Boaz House, Omagh67Omagh County Hall127Omagh Crown Buildings107Sperrin House, Omagh93Strabane New Crown Buildings86

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether his Department has identified any public sector property across Fermanagh and South Tyrone as surplus.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance (DoF) is responsible for the strategic management of the NI Civil Service Office Estate and owns a number of buildings in Fermanagh and Tyrone: Omagh County Hall; Castle Barracks, Enniskillen; Strabane New Crown Buildings; and Inishkeen House, Enniskillen.The Department is progressing an ambitious Office Estate Strategy with the aim of reducing the estate footprint by 40% by 2028. While no buildings have been declared surplus in Fermanagh and Tyrone at this stage, following consideration of accommodation needs in Enniskillen, NICS staff in Castle Barracks are being relocated locally to the Enniskillen Jobs and Benefits Office and Iniskeen House. When complete, this project will allow Castle Barracks to be declared surplus and brought forward through the formal processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the encompass roll-out across the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Encompass completed the programme’s core ‘go lives’ of the Epic system in the last two Trusts, Southern and Western, on 8 May 2025. Both Trusts have stabilised well, building on the lessons learned through earlier implementations and support from colleagues throughout HSC. The Trusts and the encompass programme continue to work to build on the excellent ‘personalisation’ levels across the Trusts, aiding efficient clinician working in the new system, on resolution of any issues arising and on the next steps to maximise the benefits of the transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of (i) properties; and (ii) business in Fermanagh and South Tyrone that will benefit from Project Gigabit.
answer_text: Project Gigabit is still in the procurement phase as my officials, along with Building Digital UK (BDUK) are assessing new commercial rollout plans for the North that became available over the summer. That assessment is due to complete shortly, allowing the procurement to complete and a contract awarded. Details of premises benefitting from the project will become available shortly afterwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the roll-out of Project Gigabit across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Project Gigabit is still in the procurement phase as my officials, along with Building Digital UK (BDUK) are assessing new commercial rollout plans for the North that became available over the summer. That assessment is due to complete shortly, allowing the procurement to complete and a contract awarded. Details of premises benefitting from the project will become available shortly afterwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the sheep trade.
answer_text: The weekly agricultural market report produced by my Department shows that sheep prices have continued to remain strong this year when compared to historic levels.  For example, the agricultural market report for the week ending 30 August 2025 shows deadweight prices for finished clean sheep averaging 641 pence per kilogram which is a 11 per cent increase from that of the equivalent week in 2024 and a 30 per cent increase from the equivalent week in 2023.  The rises in sheep prices experienced during 2024 and 2025 are reflective of tighter supply within markets during this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the benefits of the 49th Fermanagh Fishing Classic for the angling estate.
answer_text: No formal assessment has taken place on the benefits of this year’s Fermanagh Fishing Classic.  It did however serve to promote Lough Erne (as part of the Public Angling Estate) as a premier fishing venue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her current proposals to close the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: My Department is drafting both primary and subordinate legislation to close the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme. The primary legislation will provide the powers for the Department to bring forward closure regulations, and the subordinate legislation will set out the detail of the closure arrangements. Subject to Executive approval, my Department aims to introduce the primary legislation, the RHI (Closure of the Non-Domestic Scheme) Bill, to the Assembly as soon as possible. Work on drafting of the subordinate legislation is being progressed alongside the development of the Bill. Following the necessary approvals my Department intends to consult on the closure arrangements, before introducing to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the beef trade in Northern Ireland  .
answer_text: The weekly agricultural market report produced by my Department shows that beef prices have been particularly strong this year when compared to historic levels.  For example, the agricultural market report for the week ending 30 August 2025 shows deadweight prices for steers averaging 651 pence per kilogram which is a 36 per cent increase from that of the equivalent week in 2024.  This upward movement in beef prices over the past year reflects tighter supply within markets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what guidance his Department is offering farmers impacted by the A5 Western Transport Corridor Scheme following the decision by the Department for Infrastructure to appeal the High Court judgement on the Scheme.
answer_text: As the Member will be aware from my comments in the Chamber on 9 September, I have decided that it is appropriate for my department to apply to the court to intervene in the appeal of the High Court JudgmentThe focus of my intervention is to clarify the interpretation of the legislation so that it can be fully implemented as intended.  In the interim, it is important that there is regular engagement with landowners impacted by the A5 High Court Judgment and associated appeal and I have written to the Minister for Infrastructure to highlight this point.  My officials will also continue to keep in contact with their DfI counterparts to ensure that DfI has any information it needs, including on impacts for our farming community, to inform its decisions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the reasons for the delay in initiating the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass project.
answer_text: My Department’s major project portfolio is complex with a range of projects at various stages of development and delivery. Every project will have its own specific considerations but in common with all Departments,  I am very mindful at present of the impact of the judgement in the A5WTC project which was extremely disappointing and where I have lodged an appeal.	  	It would not be appropriate for me to comment in detail on the judgement while the appeal is live but, as my officials work hard to prepare for the appeal hearing, I will also take the necessary time to carefully consider the potential impact of the judgement on other projects as they move through the next stages of their delivery, which will include the A4 Enniskillen Bypass.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether they will consider rural isolation and rural settings when allocating funding for Ending Violence against Women and Girls.
answer_text: Rural isolation and rural settings were considered as part of the development of the EVAWG Strategic Framework via the completion of a published Rural Needs Impact Assessment.  Working in partnership with every local Council will help to equip grassroot groups across cities, towns, villages and rural areas to prevent and respond to violence against women and girls. Regional Change Fund delivery partners are also providing EVAWG programmes in schools, communities and workplaces in both urban and rural settings. A ‘Community Led’ review is progressing to identify additional needs and barriers for women and girls in rural settings. This, alongside learning from current Change Fund activities will help shape and inform future allocation of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) what inspection methods are used to assess watercourse damage and blockages; and (ii) whether these inspection methods are carried out in person or by satellite imagery.
answer_text: My Department inspects and carries out maintenance work in relation to any designated watercourse under the terms of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 on a cyclical basis. Inspection and maintenance records are managed through my Department’s Watercourse Maintenance Database, which supports programme planning and performance reporting.Currently, watercourse inspections are carried out by my officials – experienced flood risk professionals – who walk along the riverbanks and, where appropriate, enter the river channel for the purposes of inspections and identifying any necessary works. The observations are then recorded onto a paper copy watercourse inspection map. Any identified works will then be reviewed and considered for inclusion in the works programme for the following financial year. Geographic Information Systems (GIS) are used to support the planning and delivery of watercourse inspections maps and maintenance. GIS enables my officials to visualise designated networks, assess environmental constraints, and plan inspection routes. My officials are currently investigating the use of emerging technologies, including drones and satellite imagery, as innovative tools to support future watercourse inspections.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the July 2024 Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan conclusion that 62 per cent of phosphorus inputs to Northern Ireland’s waterbodies come from agriculture, 24 per cent from wastewater treatment and 12 per cent from septic tanks, for his assessment of whether the modelling systems and methodology used by his Department, the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute and third-party sources is evidence-based and a true reflection of root cause of blue-green algae.
answer_text: The Lough Neagh report and Action plan states significant blooms of blue-green algae are due to a combination of factors. High nutrient levels in water bodies are known to be the main cause of blue-green algae. In Lough Neagh the risk is exacerbated by increasing water temperatures (caused by climate change), and the presence of the invasive zebra mussel which selectively grazes on other algal species and increase light penetration. The phosphorus input percentages quoted are from the RephoKUs project delivered by the Agri-food and Biosciences Institute’s (AFBI) and academic partners at Leeds University, Lancaster University and the UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology. This is evidence-based research which was academically peer-reviewed before it was published in 2020.  My Department will continue to follow an evidence-based approach to policy development in line with the DAERA Science Strategy 2020-2035.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the progress to date on the development of an Artificial Intelligence strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials are developing a Framework for the application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies within and across the department’s economic remit, to ensure the North can maximise the benefits of this potentially transformational technology for our people and our businesses. This work is being informed by ongoing engagement with local industry and academia representatives to identify the opportunities and challenges regarding AI within our ecosystem.  Consideration is being given to the ambitious targets set out in the British Government’s AI Opportunities Action Plan and how we can build on the work of current initiatives, such as the Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre (AICC), Momentum One Zero in Belfast and the Cognitive Analytics & Digital Robotics Innovation Centre (CADRIC) in Derry, to take advantage of the opportunities that exist to grow the region’s economy.  My officials will continue to engage widely, including with stakeholders, ALBs and the Executive’s Chief Science and Technology Adviser as we progress policy in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the areas of expertise his most recent appointments to Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute board will bring to the agri-food sector.
answer_text: The appointments made by me to the Board of Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) were made on merit through open competition, in accordance with the Commissioner for Public Appointments’ Code of Practice for Ministerial Appointments in Northern Ireland and the relevant provisions of the Agriculture (NI) Order 2004.The appointments collectively bring a range of skills and expertise necessary to support AFBI deliver Programme for Government and Departmental priorities. This includes knowledge and experience in the areas of animal health, scientific research, agri food and governance which are required to support AFBI in helping address the environmental challenges faced, while simultaneously supporting the agri food sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the criteria applied for the appointment of membership to the Tourism Partnership Board to achieve regional balance.
answer_text: Membership of the Tourism Partnership Board is drawn from across the Tourism and Hospitality sectors as well as representatives from key Executive Departments, tourism agencies and local government. The Board has representation from tourism and hospitality umbrella organisations that have membership right across the North. It includes representatives from all three airports and from tourism businesses located in dispersed locations.  It also includes the National Trust and National Museums NI who have sites in multiple locations. Members were invited to join Tourism Partnership Board by the Chair Stephen McNally following consultation with Departmental officials and Tourism NI colleagues. Details on the Tourism Partnership Board membership and its terms of reference are available on my Department’s website: Tourism Partnership Board | Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what support and guidance his Department is offering to the Western Health and Social Care Trust in the development of a vision plan for the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: Following the pause in the consultation on Emergency General Surgery in the Western Trust, I have asked the Trust to use this immediate time to restore confidence by producing a vision plan emphasising how the South West Acute Hospital will be supported to meet both the needs of its current and future population.  The Western Trust’s Board will meet on 2 October 20205, to make decisions on the current paused consultation, and the work ahead on a “Vision” for SWAH.  The Trust will work closely with staff, community leaders, elected representatives, the public and officials from my Department as they prepare the Vision.  The temporary suspension of Emergency General Surgery at SWAH will remain at this time.My Department remains very mindful of the challenges which all Trusts in Northern Ireland face in both maintaining and reorganising services, not least in our smaller hospitals. I have been assured that the Trust, supported by the DoH, remains fully committed to the long-term development and vision for SWAH.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the rationale for the Western Health and Social Care Trust to hold only one in-person public consultation event in Co. Fermanagh following the board's decision to launch a public consultation on the permanent suspension of Emergency General Surgery at the South West Acute Hospital in Enniskillen.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the adoption of Ashdale, Enniskillen; and (ii) for an updated timeframe for progress being made.
answer_text: The Ashdale Development requires substantial works to both roads and NI Water infrastructure to achieve adoption. Unfortunately, the bond amount is not sufficient to complete these works. Following meetings between my officials, NI Water and residents, the majority of homeowners have agreed to contribute to funding the deficit needed to carry out all the required remedial works. Once the total agreed amount has been received, my department will begin the enforcement process to have the development adopted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the break clauses her Department had with contractors involved with the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The principal construction contracts for the A5 Western Transport Corridor were established using a two-phase approach, which included a designated break point following Phase 1.  Phase 2, which authorises the initiation of full construction activities, has not been executed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the adoption of the sewerage system in Meadow Farm, Enniskillen; (ii) to detail the shortfall in the current bond with the former developer; and (iii) for an updated timeframe for progress being made.
answer_text: The adoption of wastewater infrastructure in developments is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:Meadow Farm, Enniskillen is a development for which the road and sewerage infrastructure was not completed to the standard required for adoption.  It has therefore, remained private and the responsibility falls to the residents, as the developer is no longer operating.  Both the roads and sewers require considerable remedial works to be carried out before they could be considered for adoption.  NI Water has recently carried out a survey of the majority of the site and plans to include a CCTV survey to establish the extent of the remedial work required to the sewerage infrastructure.  The small amount that remains in the bonds for this development falls well short of the estimated cost of the remedial works required to both the roads and sewers within the development.  Neither the Department nor NI Water is funded to carry out such repairs to private developments.Site investigations and survey work are being undertaken by NI Water to establish the extent of the remedial work required to the sewerage infrastructure.  It is expected that this will be completed and reviewed by late August. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the adoption of the sewerage system in the Corn Mill development, Bellanaleck; (ii) to detail the shortfall in the current bond with the developer; and (iii) for an updated timeframe for progress being made.
answer_text: The adoption of wastewater infrastructure in developments is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water previously carried out some investigation work on site to ascertain the standard of the sewerage infrastructure, which confirmed that the site was not completed to adoptable standard.  NI Water has recently been advised, via a third party, that repair works have been completed.  NI Water now intends to commence further investigation work, to include an onsite visit, to assess the present condition of the infrastructure and confirm if it has since been completed to an adoptable standard;NI Water has advised that there is no bond in place for the sewerage infrastructure, as the bond provider went into liquidation, therefore, the responsibility for full costs rests with the developer; andNI Water now intends to carry out further site investigations imminently to ascertain if, in fact, works have been completed and the site has been brought up to adoptable standard. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of engaging with UK Government on the benefits of VAT reduction on takeaway food.
answer_text: I’m aware of the challenges facing our hospitality industry with rising costs, and particularly in relation to the high VAT rates faced by businesses here, relative to the South. I recently met with representatives of the NI Food to Go Association who raised the issue with me. The association is working on a proposal to the Independent Hospitality Alliance (a wide group for the food to go sector in Britain) regarding a possible reduction of VAT for takeaway food and the benefits for businesses in the food to go sector. I have asked the Association to share the proposal with me. VAT is an excepted matter and decisions to change the rate of VAT rests with the British Government, who have unfortunately not shown any willingness to address this matter so far.When Finance Minister, I commissioned a Cost of Doing business research report. It was published in May this year and sets out further evidence concerning the disparity of VAT, especially for our hospitality businesses operating near the border.This is an issue that the Finance Minister has raised with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST) in his letter of 10 April, and when they met on 24 April. I understand the Finance Minister has urged the CST to consider the case for a reduction in VAT to enable our hospitality businesses to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy. I will continue to work with the Finance Minister as we continue to press the British Government to consider all possible means of putting a VAT regime in place that enables our businesses to compete within the all-Ireland economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the viability of A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass following the recent court ruling on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The A5 judgement was extremely detailed, and it was the first legal test for the Climate Change Act and for Climate Action Plan – something every department will now have to deal with the outworkings of.  	We are working through the judgement, and it is too soon to conclude on its impact to wider infrastructure projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any exemptions her Department anticipates may be part of the Good Jobs Bill ban on zero-hour contracts, given the needs of the Food to Go sector which relies on seasonal and temporary work throughout the year.
answer_text: My proposals aim to end exploitative zero hours contracts by introducing a right for zero and low hours workers who work consistent and regular hours, to move to a banded hours contract.  I recognise that some businesses rely on genuinely seasonal or casual work and some workers wish to work on this basis.  I have therefore decided to permit a limited number of exemptions. I recently met with representatives from the Food to Go sector.  I, and my officials, will continue to engage further with this sector and other relevant stakeholders as we develop our legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any role her Department will have in the Competition and Markets Authority inquiry into the Boeing Company's anticipated acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems Holdings.
answer_text: The Competition and Markets Authority is responsible for investigating mergers and the investigation process is independent of government.  Consequently, my Department has no role in the CMA’s inquiry.I have engaged regularly with all stakeholders, including Boeing, over the past several months in relation to the sale of Spirit.  I met with representatives from Boeing last week who provided me with reassurances that, under Boeing’s ownership, the priority will be to ensure production stability for the company. They also see real benefits for Boeing here in the North, particularly given the skills and capabilities of the local workforce. I continue to work with Executive colleagues, Invest NI, the British Government and all involved to deliver the best outcome for the site, for all the workers and the wider supply chain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any investment intentions or assurances offered to her Department from the Boeing Company as they are set to take control of the remainder of Spirit AeroSystems Holding's plant in Belfast.
answer_text: I have engaged regularly with all stakeholders over the past several months in relation to the sale of Spirit’s local operation. I met recently with senior representatives of Boeing, who recognise the benefits of operating here in the North, particualrly the skills and capabilities of the local workforce. Any discussions on the potential for future investment are commercially sensitive and therefore, confidential. I continue to work with Executive colleagues, Invest NI, the British Government and all involved to deliver the best outcome for the site, for its workers and for those employed throughout the wider supply chain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement his Department has had with the UK Government in relation to the negative impact extended producer responsibility for packaging is having on local businesses.
answer_text: My officials have engaged in frequent meetings on a four-nation basis during the development and implementation of Extended Producer Responsibility for packaging (pEPR). Local industry has also been represented through stakeholder forums and steering groups. This engagement has ensured that the interests of Northern Ireland’s businesses have been heard and reflected in key policy decisions and refinements to this scheme which supports Northern Ireland’s transition to a circular economy.I have personally written to Defra Minister Creagh highlighting concerns around the potential impact of pEPR on our local industry without greater certainty around future direction. This correspondence gained assurances from the UK Government that options to reform the Packaging Recycling Note system will be explored and an effective fee modulation will be implemented from year 2 of pEPR. Fee modulation will see lower producer costs for packaging that is highly recyclable. pEPR will also reward reusable packaging as fees will only be charged the first time a product is placed on the market.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how her officials propose to address the significant under compensation for the remaining participants in the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme; and (ii) whether the remaining participants from the 1,200 small medium businesses, accredited to her Department's only emissions reduction scheme, will be compensated.
answer_text: My Department is progressing legislation to close the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive (RHI) Scheme, in line with the New Decade, New Approach agreement and the Executive’s July 2024 decision. A central aim is to ensure fairness for both scheme participants and taxpayers. Officials are engaging with stakeholders, and a public consultation is planned to inform final proposals. Draft legislation to uplift small and medium biomass tariffs, which was intended to address under-compensation while closure proposals were being developed, was brought forward by my Department in October 2024 but was not approved by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why there has been prevarication in the delivery of the recommendations of (i) the Northern Ireland Affairs Select Committee Inquiry Report, Changes to the Northern Ireland Renewable Heat Incentive scheme payments; (ii) the Buglass Energy Advisory Limited 2020 research into hardship report, as commissioned by her Department; and (iii) the February 2023 Court of Appeal judgement advocacy of a full, fair and final resolution for the participants of the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme within months and not years.
answer_text: My Department is working urgently on progressing legislation to close the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme, in line with the New Decade, New Approach agreement and in line with the Executive’s agreement in July 2024. A key objective of the work being undertaken is ensuring that closure proposals achieve fairness, both for participants on the Scheme and for the taxpayer. My officials have been engaging with key stakeholders in the development of closure proposals. A public consultation on closure proposals is also planned.	Under my predecessor, the Department also brought draft legislation before the Assembly to uplift the small and medium biomass tariffs on the Scheme. This would have resulted in a significant increase in payments to participants; however the Assembly did not approve the draft regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of physical assaults that have occurred against medical staff, such as doctors, nurses and health care assistants, in the Western Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) hospital; and (ii) medical site.
