question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to confirm that HM Treasury will not advance Annually Managed Expenditure (AME) funding for the purposes of terminating the Renewable Heat Incentive scheme.
answer_text: ANSWERThe business case to close the non-domestic RHI Scheme has been submitted to Treasury for review and consideration of the continued use of Annually Managed Expenditure (AME) for payments to Scheme participants in line with the Department for Economy’s proposals. We await the outcome of Treasury’s consideration of the business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline whether any changes have been made to the designation of the Colebrooke River since 2010.
answer_text: The Colebrooke River where it enters upper Lough Erne is undesignated and the flow of water remains unimpeded, presenting no issue with regards to flood risk or drainage. The mouth of the Colebrooke River is also in an environmentally sensitive area. There are several national and international designations which would impose significant regulatory and environmental constraints on any potential dredging works and disposing of any material.  The build-up of material in question comprises of an accumulation of sands, gravel and other natural material which is deposited when the river slows down. On a river as wide and as powerful as the Colebrooke, these accumulations would easily be drowned out during high flows. This sedimentation is a natural river process. Any flood risk along this stretch of the Colebrooke River is confined to the low-lying agricultural grounds adjacent to the river and there is no risk to property or infrastructure.There have been no amendments to the designation of the Colebrooke River since 2010.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the regrowth and repair of habitats after the Slieve Beagh Mountain, Clogher Valley gorse fires and wildfires.
answer_text: Departmental officials visited Slieve Beagh Area of Special Scientific Interest (ASSI)/Special Area of Conservation (SAC) on 6th June 2025 to assess the impact. Whilst the damage is extensive, officials are content that given time and no other pressures, e.g. heavy grazing, the area will recover over time. The Department will continue to monitor the condition of the site and its features.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she considered the statute requirements of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 when giving her approval for the closure of the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme by means of the urgent procedure.
answer_text: In July 2024, the Executive confirmed its preferred option to close the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme. This decision was taken by FMdFM under Urgent Procedure. My Department is urgently progressing proposals for closure of the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme. These proposals will take into consideration the requirements of the Climate Change (Northern Ireland) Act 2022. Further Executive approval will be needed for the closure proposals and the legislation to implement them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the rationale behind her Department’s decision not to carry out maintenance works to the entrance of the Colebrooke River, which is designated under the terms of the Drainage (Northern Ireland) Order 1973, given that similar maintenance works were carried out to clear blockages at the mouth of the river in 2011.
answer_text: The Colebrooke River where it enters upper Lough Erne is undesignated and the flow of water remains unimpeded, presenting no issue with regards to flood risk or drainage. The mouth of the Colebrooke River is also in an environmentally sensitive area. There are several national and international designations which would impose significant regulatory and environmental constraints on any potential dredging works and disposing of any material.  The build-up of material in question comprises of an accumulation of sands, gravel and other natural material which is deposited when the river slows down. On a river as wide and as powerful as the Colebrooke, these accumulations would easily be drowned out during high flows. This sedimentation is a natural river process. Any flood risk along this stretch of the Colebrooke River is confined to the low-lying agricultural grounds adjacent to the river and there is no risk to property or infrastructure.There have been no amendments to the designation of the Colebrooke River since 2010.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered bingo as a key activity for over 50s to engage in to maintain mental agility and physical well-being.
answer_text: While bingo has demonstrated benefits for social connection and cognitive stimulation in older adults, it has not been prioritised as a standalone intervention for mental agility or physical well-being in our current programming. Our evidence-based approach focuses on more comprehensive strategies for healthy ageing, including: - Cognitive health: Take 5 Steps to Wellbeing, arts and health / lifelong learning programmes, and dementia friendly initiatives- Physical well-being: Structured exercise programmes (strength, balance, and aerobic activities, including physical activity referral programmes) aligned with NICE and WHO guidelines- Social participation: Multifaceted community programmes addressing isolation through diverse activities such as Age Friendly programmes in each council area.It is important to note the gambling concerns that may arise when considering bingo as a key activity for over 50s to engage in to maintain mental agility and physical well-being. Although most senior bingo is primarily played for low- stakes, it should be considered that, if played for money, some older adults may lose track of expenditure and be at risk of developing risky habits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether there has been an increase or decrease in the levels of online gambling since the COVID-19 pandemic.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data that shows the prevalence of online gambling since the COVID-19 pandemic specifically. However, whilst showing an overall decreasing trend in any form of gambling participation here, from 67% in 2016 to 57% in 2024, results of  Northern Ireland Gambling Prevalence Surveys indicate that the proportion of those who had gambled online in the previous twelve-month period increased from 15.8% to 23% in the same time period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has considered the benefits of local bingo halls for the high street and the night-time economy, particularly in rural towns and villages.
answer_text: My Department, alongside Tourism NI and the three Business Improvement Districts of Belfast, is currently conducting research into the night-time economy. This work is focusing on Belfast, Derry and Enniskillen but will seek to place a value on the night-time economy across the north. It has included significant stakeholder engagement. While the research is not finalised, preliminary recommendations include developing a diverse offer for patrons. Although local bingo halls have not been raised during the stakeholder engagement process, they could form part of an improved offering. Regional Balance is also one of my Department’s four economic priorities and ensuring our rural towns and villages enjoy economic growth is a key aspect of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what support his Department is giving to Fermanagh and Omagh District Council to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the United States of America's Declaration of Independence next year.
answer_text: I announced £250k earlier this year for organisations to mark the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence, and my officials are working on the development of the funding scheme.It will be several months before it will be ready to receive applications. A further announcement will be made when preparations are completed, and the scheme opens to applicants.The Department for Communities provides around £2.1m in annual funding to the Ulster-Scots Agency, who have the legislative responsibility for the promotion of greater awareness of Ulster Scots cultural issues. The Ulster-Scots Agency are also developing proposals that celebrate the cultural links to this historic 250th anniversary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what support his Department is giving to Mid Ulster District Council to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the United States of Americia's Declaration of Independence next year.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will be carrying out an Environmental Impact Assessment on the land vested during the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: Since its inception in 2007, the A5 Western Transport Corridor (A5WTC) has been subject to an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA). The Department has therefore over the years prepared a number of Environmental Statements and Addenda reporting the findings of studies and assessments which have been undertaken to investigate to what extent the acquisition of land and construction of the A5WTC would likely have on the environment. All these documents have been available on the A5WTC website since their publication dates and have been subject to scrutiny through the public inquiry process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what considerations her Department undertook to establish if the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme would meet the legislation set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, given her Department's own Environmental Statement Addendum in 2022, which stated that the residual effect of greenhouse gas emissions during both construction and operation of the scheme, would have a moderate adverse, significant, effect.
answer_text: The steps taken by my Department to address the mandatory greenhouse gas emissions targets are detailed in the Statement by The Department on the Report on the Public Local Inquiries into the A5 Western Transport Corridor Road Scheme, published in October 2024, Recommendations 14 – 17 starting at paragraph 5.3.16 This is available on https://www.a5wtc.com/pages/departmental-statements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will be carrying out a Rural Impact Assessment on farmland price increases in areas surrounding the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 applies to the development, adoption, implementation and revising of policies, strategies and plans and to the design and delivery of public services. DfI published the Rural Needs Impact Assessment (RNIA) - 2024 carried out for the A5 Western Transport Corridor project on 26 September 2024 and is available on:https://www.a5wtc.com/pages/scheme-related-documents. The price of farmland in areas around the project is, however, driven by market forces beyond the control of my Department and a Rural Impact Assessment specific to this topic is not considered appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what consideration values her Department will use to determine how Landowners affected by the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme will be compensated.
answer_text: The judge quashed the decision and the orders on the 27 June 2025. The Department will work with all landowners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the timeframe as to when her Department will return vested lands as part of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The judge quashed the decision and the orders on the 27 June 2025. The Department will work with all landowners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her engagement with the UK Government to secure the future for workers and the facilities at Spirit AeroSystems, following the deal by Airbus to acquire part of the operations.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what representation she has made to the UK Government regarding the impact of foreign glass that undercuts domestic suppliers from high carbon markets.
answer_text: Policy responsibility for Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) in the North rests with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. I have previously written to the Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to highlight concerns raised by local businesses around EPR Scheme fees. This included the Packaging Recovery Notes (PRNs) system, which local businesses feel incentivises the export of glass cullet, making domestic production less competitive, losing its economic value to the local economy and generating unnecessary carbon emissions. I stressed in my letter to Minister Muir, as the Minister with policy responsibility for this area, the importance of relaying these concerns to the Department of Environment Food and Rural Affairs (Defra), especially considering the potential economic opportunity lost to the local economy. My Department is currently finalising its Circular Economy Strategy, and included is a proposal to aim maximise the value of materials locally. The Strategy illustrates my ambition to create a low-carbon circular economy providing significant economic benefits to the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the review of further education.
answer_text: Further Education Colleges play a critical role in delivering the skills which the economy needs by providing learners with high quality vocational and technical education. With courses available from access level to post graduate level, colleges have courses to suit learners of all ages. My Department has begun initial preparatory work on a transformation programme which will be taken forward in conjunction with Further Education Colleges over the next 24 months to review the Further Education delivery model.  	The programme will promote greater consistency in the provision of technical and vocational educational pathways as well as innovation support for employers in pursuit of my Economic Vision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what steps her Department is taking to address the high cost of industrial energy.
answer_text: The Path to Net Zero Energy Strategy aims to move away from the imported fossil fuels that put us in the position of a “price-taker”.  One of the key objectives of the strategy is to ensure that all consumers, business and domestic, have access to renewable and affordable energy to enable an adequate standard of competitiveness. The focus is now on priorities for delivery to ensure that we maximise the benefits for our society and economy as soon as possible. Additionally, Invest NI delivers a suite of support, including specialist advice and investment capital, to enable businesses to become more sustainable through green efficiencies.  The full detail of the range of support is available on the Invest NI website - Net zero and the green economy | Invest Northern Ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment on how alcohol duty is impacting alcohol suppliers and their supply chain in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out an assessment on the impact of alcohol duty, which is a reserved matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what engagement her Department has had with the UK Government regarding the negative impact extended producer responsibility for packaging is having on local businesses.
answer_text: Policy responsibility for Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) in the North rests with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. I have previously written to the Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to highlight concerns raised by local businesses around EPR Scheme fees. This included the Packaging Recovery Notes (PRNs) system, which local businesses feel incentivises the export of glass cullet, making domestic production less competitive, losing its economic value to the local economy and generating unnecessary carbon emissions. I stressed in my letter to Minister Muir, as the Minister with policy responsibility for this area, the importance of relaying these concerns to the Department of Environment Food and Rural Affairs (Defra), especially considering the potential economic opportunity lost to the local economy. My Department is currently finalising its Circular Economy Strategy, and included is a proposal to aim maximise the value of materials locally. The Strategy illustrates my ambition to create a low-carbon circular economy providing significant economic benefits to the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to outline what plans are in place for repurposing Lack Primary School once the current school term ends; and (ii) to provide assurances to the local community that this building will not be left to fall into dereliction like St Mary’s Brollagh, Belleek and the former Duke of Westminster School, Kesh.
answer_text: A decision on the future use of the current Lack Primary School will not be taken until the school closes.The Education Authority (EA) will first consider other potential educational uses for the site dependent on the demand pressures for education provision in the area. However, if no alternative educational use is identified and the site is declared surplus the EA must comply with the Land and Property Services (LPS) guidance for the disposal of surplus public sector property to ensure best value for money is obtained through the site's disposal. This guidance is publicly available and can be accessed via the Department of Finance website using the following link: Disposal of surplus land and property - publications | Department of Finance (finance-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when construction of the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass will commence.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.Procurement for the construction of Enniskillen Southern Bypass is at an advanced stage, with tender assessments well underway.  Following completion of this, it is hoped that a contractor will be appointed to begin construction in late Summer 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what engagement her Department has had with the Border Region Manufacturing Cluster, funded through Enterprise Ireland’s Regional Technology Clustering Fund, since its establishment in 2021.
answer_text: The Invest NI Cluster Acceleration Programme (CAP) provides funding to the North West Generating Engineering and Manufacturing Excellence network (GEMX) which has a membership of over 60 businesses and stakeholders and representing 5,869 jobs. Border Region Manufacturing Cluster (BORMAC) are a stakeholder member of GEMX.  Invest NI do not provide any financial support to BORMAC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of applications to the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme to date.
answer_text: From scheme launch, 2 June 2025 to the present date, 27 June 2025, there has been a total of 180 applications received in the first 4 weeks of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of a biomethane strategy.
answer_text: My Department is currently working on a number of biomethane actions as part of the 2025 Energy Strategy action plan (2025 Energy Strategy Action Plan). This includes progressing Action 6, which will support the sustainability of biomethane being utilised in the gas network in the North. Key areas of focus include:Progressing Support Mechanisms.Consulting on options for Gas Network Connection Costs.Investigating Standards/Certifications.Work is ongoing to analyse a range of options aimed at addressing the challenges and costs for biomethane producers connecting to the gas network. My Department intends to consult on the treatment of connection costs later this year. My Department will continue to work with the Utility Regulator, Gas Network Operators and other key stakeholders as part of the Energy Strategy action plan to support the development of a sustainable biomethane sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the recent members' survey, conducted by UK Hospitality, the British Institute of Innkeeping, the British Beer and Pub Association, and Hospitality Ulster, that showed almost 27 per cent of hospitality businesses are now operating at a loss and that only 6 per cent of respondents reported making more profit at this stage in 2025 compared to 2024.
answer_text: Our hospitality sector has displayed remarkable resilience in emerging from the pandemic and adapting to rising costs. I’m fully aware concerns have been raised surrounding the tax and spending changes implemented in early April as announced in the British Government’s Autumn Statement.  In my previous role as Finance Minister, I commissioned the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre to examine the Cost of Doing Business in the north. The research was recently published, and I, along with the current Finance Minister, will be using this as evidence to further advocate for taxation changes to support our local businesses.  Minister O’Dowd raised the issues with employer’s national insurance contributions and VAT with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee in February. I will continue to work closely with the Minister of Finance in lobbying for changes to support our businesses. My Department along with Invest NI and Tourism NI provide a broad range of support to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets. I am also committed to engaging with the local hospitality sector to understand the issues it is facing. Later this week I will address a meeting of the Tourism Partnership Board whose members include the Chief Executive of Hospitality Ulster.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department is supporting apprenticeships in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: The ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeships (HLA) programmes are demand-led, with apprentices recruited and retained by employers in line with business needs. My Department funds 100% of the cost of the directed, off-the-job, training element of an apprenticeship. The ApprenticeshipsNI Programme also provides the employer with an incentive payment upon successful completion of the apprenticeship. These programmes are supported by sectoral partnerships to ensure the programme of learning aligns with our economic needs and a wide range of promotional activity and engagement. Alongside cross-cutting support there have been other regional initiatives such as funding the development of MEGA’s Higher Level Apprenticeship in Mechanical and Manufacturing Engineering to support local companies in the west and the NI Apprenticeship Week southern regional working group which consists of local stakeholders to promote apprenticeships in the region. The most recent ApprenticeshipsNI statistical bulletin indicates that Fermanagh and South Tyrone had the highest number of participants on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme out of all parliamentary constituencies across the North with 878 at January 2025.  The constituency also has a total of 225 participants pursuing Higher Level Apprenticeship qualifications at Further Education Colleges. This is the highest number of participants out of all the parliamentary constituencies across the North. A further 20 participants with postcodes in Fermanagh and South Tyrone are pursuing Higher Level Apprenticeship qualifications at Higher Education Institutions.  It should be noted that Parliamentary Constituency is based on an apprentice’s home postcode. Although the apprentice may live in a particular Parliamentary Constituency, they may attend work and training elsewhere.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department is supporting up-skilling in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: My Skills Fund supports a number of programmes that provide individuals with opportunities to skill, re-skill, and upskill. These programmes are delivered through local further and higher education institutions, including South West College, which has campuses in Omagh, Enniskillen, and Dungannon. The Skill Up, Step Up, and Breaking Barriers programmes offer a range of free upskilling and reskilling opportunities. In addition, my Department collaborates with local businesses and further education colleges to deliver Skills Focus, InnovateUs, and Assured Skills Academies. Details of all upskilling programmes are available on the NI Direct website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will review the Lough Erne Operating Regime, to ensure its adequate to meet modern day needs and comply with the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The flood of November 2009 on the Lough Erne system eclipsed all previously known records. In the aftermath of that flood my Department carried out an in-depth review of the Operating Regime for the Erne System to ensure that the arrangements and parameters for the management of the Erne System were adequate to meet modern day needs. A copy of the review can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-lough-erne-operating-regime. Whilst the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 primarily focuses on reduction targets for emissions, my Department have well developed robust plans to mitigate the impacts of climate change, including those related to flood risk. Rivers officials have recently confirmed that the current Lough Erne Operating Regime remains effective and appropriate in addressing these challenges. Accordingly, my Department has no plans to undertake a further review of the regime at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how much of his Department's initial budget for 2025-26 will be directly allocated to projects in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: The Department’s initial budget for 2025-26 is not allocated in the format you have requested and therefore cannot be released. The Department’s initial budget allocations are subject to a public consultation process. This will ensure that stakeholder views are considered before final allocations can be confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what measures are at her disposal to achieve the agreed water levels in Upper Lough of between (i) 150 feet and 154 feet, April to September, and 155 feet, October to March; and (ii) in Lower Lough between 147 feet and 152 feet.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate Operations staff maintain the water level in the Upper Lough, as far as weather conditions permit, through the management of the control structure downstream on the inter Lough channel at Portora, Enniskillen.The Electricity Supply Board (ESB) in the South of Ireland manage the water level in the Lower Lough via the control structure downstream at Cliff, County Donegal. Officials from my Department liaise closely with engineers from ESB on a regular basis, especially when heavy rain is forecast, to try and minimise flood risk in the Erne catchment. My Operations staff monitor and record daily the water level from the four water level stations on the Erne system at Belle Isle, Portora (Upstream and Downstream) and at Rosscor. These levels are published each day on the Departments website at Lough Erne levels | Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether car hire companies are required to charge a fee for motorists, renting a vehicle in Northern Ireland, with the intention to travel to the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Regulation of car hire charges is not within the remit of my Department. I am not aware of any statutory requirement to charge a fee for motorists renting a vehicle in the North with the intention to travel to the South.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he plans to recruit additional general practitioners for the new Lisnaskea Health and Care Centre.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what measures are in place to assist farmers to diversify into wheatgrass farming.
answer_text: My Department has no specific measures in place to assist farmers to diversify into wheatgrass farming.

answer_text: My Department has no specific measures in place to assist farmers to diversify into worm farming.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Forest Service’s ongoing industry engagement with wind energy developers to assess the potential of wind energy generation on forestry land.
answer_text: The Wind Energy Market Engagement exercise was launched on 6 March 2025 and invited wind energy developers to engage in the assessment of forest areas on the Agency’s estate, which are of interest to the sector for future wind energy generation. Responses were received from many commercial wind energy developers and whilst analysis is still ongoing, Forest Service will continue to liaise with and update interested parties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of what potential opportunities his Department's estate presents to achieve 80 per cent electricity consumption from renewable sources by 2030, as set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Department continues to explore opportunities for the generation of electricity from renewable sources across its estate. As part of this, Forest Service commissioned a market engagement exercise in March 2025 exploring the potential for wind energy development on the forest estate. Analysis of responses from commercial developers is ongoing and will inform decision making. My Department also has worked planned in 2025/26 to increase the number of sites across the estate which can produce on-site renewable electricity from photo-voltaic panels (PVs), with further sites being scoped for future installations. This includes the Department’s new build programme where facilities such as the Animal Health Sciences Building and CAFRE Campus redevelopment facilities have included PVs in their design enabling renewable generation of electricity on-site.In addition, electricity is also generated from a renewable bio-gas powered Combined Heat and Power Plant at the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute’s Hillsborough site. Work is underway to consider potential opportunities to further enhance electricity generation from this Plant.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to resurface Derrychara Drive, Enniskillen.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as carriageway resurfacing.Whilst there are many roads which would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to limited budgets, resurfacing and maintenance work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those areas deemed most in need of intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections of Derrychara Drive and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether, under existing legislative provision, the power exists to vest privately-owned land for the purposes of the creation of a public greenway in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: A public greenway would fall under the definition of a road within article 2 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993. As such, my Department has the power under article 110 (1) of that order to acquire land for the purposes of the creation of a public greenway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to resurface the Derrychara Road, Enniskillen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the budget set aside to bring the old Devenish College site up to standard, pending its planned use by Enniskillen Royal Grammar School.
answer_text: A contract has recently been awarded to undertaken refurbishment works on the former Devenish College site at Algeo Drive, Enniskillen. The contract value is in the region of nine hundred and twenty thousand pounds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the key performance indicators which his Department has in place for the requesting and processing of Land Registry documents for legal professionals.
answer_text: The key performance indicator for the requesting and processing of Land Registry documents for legal professionals is 90 per cent of documents are to be issued within 15 days of the receipt of a request.In the financial year 2024/2025, 13,325 requests were received and 96.8 per cent were issued within the 15 day target time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Ulster Canal project.
answer_text: Waterways Ireland is currently delivering the restoration of the Ulster Canal from Lough Erne to Clones. The overall project is divided into 3 phases.  Phase 1, from Lough Erne to Castle Saunderson opened for navigation in 2019 and phase 2, from Clones to Clonfad opened in June 2024. Phase 3, from Clonfad to Castle Saunderson, consists of 10km of new and refurbished navigable canal and towpath. Waterways Ireland is currently progressing land acquisition, planning and detailed design elements.  The anticipated timeline for completion of this final phase connection from Lough Erne to Clones is c.2029 subject to funding, land acquisition and planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average depth of Lough Erne in 2000, compared to today in 2025.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate operational staff have a remit to ensure that water levels in Lough Erne are maintained within specified levels, insofar as climatic conditions allow, as stipulated under the terms of an agreement made in 1950. Further information on this agreement is available at the weblink below:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/lough-erne-levelsMy Department would not have records of water depth along the Erne system. Nautical charts and depth maps can be provided at: fishermap.org and depth contours can be found via this link: Marine Navigation App | Nautical Charts App

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the number of dredging operations on Lower and Upper Lough Erne for the purposes of (a) flood prevention; and (b) improving water flow, since 2010; and (ii) for a breakdown of the costs.
answer_text: My Departments Rivers Directorate does not dredge Upper Lough Erne or Lower Lough Erne. Dredging involves removing material from the bed of a Lough or watercourse. Excavating the bed of the Lough would not improve its overall drainage function. Any natural accumulation of material which could impede flow is moved on through normal river processes which are important to maintain the natural equilibrium within the entire Erne system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any stakeholder engagement her Department has had in relation to the development of an Artificial Intelligence strategy in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Tree Preservation Orders in force, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Details of Tree Preservation Orders are held on a public register maintained by each local council, you may wish to contact each directly to request this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she intends to regulate the use of watercraft on Lough Erne, particularly personal watercraft, within the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Waterways Ireland, as the navigation authority, is responsible for enforcement of the LoughErne (Navigation) Bye-Laws (Northern Ireland) 1978 and the Lough Erne (Navigation)(Amendment) Bye-Laws (Northern Ireland) 1986. Waterways Ireland intends to update these Bye-Laws, and that future updates will include personal watercraft related regulations.  It is planned the work to revise the Erne Bye Laws will commence before the end of the year; however, this is dependent on the revised Bye Laws for the Shannon and Canals being approved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Northern Ireland Statistics & Research Agency (NISRA) holds accurate data on the size of the Food to Go Industry.
answer_text: NISRA holds accurate data on the size of the food-to-go industry through: the maintenance of the Inter-Departmental Business Register (IDBR), a database of all businesses that are registered for Value Added Tax (VAT) and/or operate a Pay-As-You-Earn (PAYE) scheme here;running several statutory surveys each year based on a representative sample of businesses on the IDBR to collect detailed and up-to-date information, including employment and turnover; andpublishing accredited official statistics, in accordance with the Code of Practice for Statistics, to describe the size and other characteristics of local industries. The Food-to-Go industry can be approximated using two standard industrial classification (SIC) codes: SIC 56102 (“Unlicensed restaurants and cafes”), and SIC 56103 (“Take away food shops and mobile food stands”).As of March 2024, there were 2,220 businesses operating here within the Food to Go industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether renewable power delivered through interconnectors count towards our renewable energy goals as set out in Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 established a target for 80% of our electricity consumption to be from renewable sources by 2030. My Department publishes statistics on Electricity Consumption and Renewable Generation in Northern Ireland. The definition and coverage of the measure reported is: indigenous metered renewable generation as a proportion of metered electricity consumption. Electricity imported through interconnection does not therefore contribute towards the measure of performance against our renewable electricity target.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she plans to bring forward legislation to govern the food delivery industry, particularly around commission costs to local businesses who use the service.
answer_text: I am meeting with The Food To Go Association shortly and will consider their proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what steps her Department is taking to establish a Northern Ireland food business register for the Food to Go Sector.
answer_text: My Department is not taking steps to establish a food business register for the Food to Go Sector as such a register already exists. Registration of food business is already required by law. Anyone starting a new food business must register with their local council at least 28 days before trading and local councils must keep a public list of all registered food businesses, containing the name, address and nature of the food business.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the growth of the Northern Ireland Food to Go Sector over the next 10 years.
answer_text: The Food to Go sector plays an important role within our visitor and wider economy. Over one third of expenditure by overnight visitors is on food and drink, equating to approximately £420m annually. Take away food shops & mobile food stands and unlicensed restaurants & cafes had an approximate Gross Value Added (aGVA) of £125m and £197m respectively, collectively representing around 25% of the hospitality sector’s total aGVA in 2022. The sector supports employment and provides a diverse food offer to meet the needs of both visitors and local people. In some cases, it provides an opportunity to showcase local produce and hence supports a more circular economy. I anticipate our Food to Go sector will continue to grow and innovate, reflecting our diversifying and rich cultural heritage. My Department launched a 10 Year Tourism Vision and Action Plan in January and set a strategic goal to grow expenditure from our overnight visitors to £2 billion by 2035.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what new proposals will be included in the replacement scheme when her Department closes the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: My Department is urgently progressing proposals for closure of the current Non-Domestic RHI Scheme. I hope to be in a position to launch a closure consultation in the coming months, with the aim of closure, subject to necessary approvals and agreements by 2026.Closure of the current scheme is a Departmental priority and once arrangements are in place my Department will focus on the details of a future non-domestic renewable heat policy or scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what support is available to parents whose young infants are suffering from childhood gastro-oesophageal reflux in the (i) Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (iI) Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT):The Western Trust provides support to parents of infants experiencing gastro-oesophageal reflux (GOR) through its Children and Families services. This includes access to health visitors, community children's nurses, and paediatric services. The Trust promotes evidence-based parenting programmes, such as the Solihull Approach and Parents Plus Programmes, which can help parents manage feeding and behavioural challenges associated with GOR. These programmes are coordinated by the Child Development Interventions Coordinator (CDIC) to ensure consistent support across the region.  It is worth noting that while many babies have GOR, only some have symptomatic GORD (gastro-oesohageal reflux disease) and these are the babies and families who need more support which is provided by Health Visiting and dietetics as per above.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT)The Southern Trust’s Health Visiting service offers information and advice to parents on managing common childhood illnesses and conditions, including childhood gastro-oesophageal reflux (GOR). Heath Visitors offer targeted support to those parents with babies experiencing discomfort feeding.  Out of hours Health Visitors provide an infant feeding helpline Monday to Friday 6pm- 9pm and contacts to the helpline are followed by the family health visitor the next working day. Health Visitors also liaise with GPs to follow up recommendations from the Regional Infant Feeding guidelines. Parents are encouraged to utilise resources such as the "Birth to Five" book, which provides guidance on caring for children up to five years old and contact details for useful organisations.  If health visiting intervention hasn’t alleviated the feeding difficulties the Trust also provide a specialised Paediatric Feeding Service (PFS) that works with parents to assess and support infants with feeding difficulties, including those related to GOR. The PFS team provides guidance on feeding techniques and collaborates with other healthcare professionals to develop individualised care plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people (i) eligible for the Healthy Start scheme; and (ii) currently receiving support through the programme, in the (a) Western Health and Social care Trust; and (b) Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Data for the Healthy Start scheme is regularly published by the NHS Business Services Authority, who administer the scheme on behalf of England, Wales and Northern Ireland. You can access the most up to data at https://www.healthystart.nhs.uk/healthcare-professionals/ .  In respect of your specific questions – Unfortunately, data is not currently available for the number of people eligible for the NHS Healthy Start scheme. This is due to an issue with the data provided to the NHS Business Services Authority, which they require in order to calculate uptake for the scheme. Work is ongoing with data providers to resolve this issue. Importantly however, the current data issues do not prevent anyone from joining the scheme or continuing to access the scheme if they are eligible.We no longer receive data by Health & Social Care Trust area. Latest figures show there are 10,403 people receiving support from the Healthy Start Scheme across Northern Ireland. The Northern Ireland scheme figures are categorised by council area, using postcode data from the Office of National Statistics.  While not directly relating to the Trusts you have requested, the latest available figures by local council are provided below:Local Council  People on Digital Scheme  Antrim and Newtownabbey  614  Ards and North Down  535  Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon  1,029  Belfast  2,935  Causeway Coast and Glens  728  Derry City and Strabane  1,434  Fermanagh and Omagh  524  Lisburn and Castlereagh  477  Mid and East Antrim  624  Mid Ulster  623  Newry, Mourne and Down  865  Unknown  15**  ** Some postcodes held within the source datasets are incorrect and therefore are reported as an 'unknown' postcode.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what engagement he has had with the UK Government around the ongoing challenges for sporting guns and ammunition distributors in relation to the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: My Department has policy responsibility for devolved firearms and explosives matters in Northern Ireland.  Import and export matters are reserved, under the Northern Ireland Act 1998, and are the responsibility of the Department for Business and Trade.  My officials engage regularly with their counterparts in GB who keep them informed of any firearms and explosives developments that may impact on our devolved responsibilities.  Whilst I have not engaged specifically on this issue, no concerns have been raised in respect of the import, export, or distribution of firearms or explosives (ammunition) in relation to the Windsor Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the impact of the UK Government's Immigration White Paper on Northern Ireland’s economy.
answer_text: My officials are currently considering the content of the Immigration White Paper.  However, the proposals to remove non-graduate level occupations from the Skilled Worker visa route and the suggested increase to the salary threshold are likely to result in a reduction in Immigration. My Department’s Skills Barometer recently forecast the future skills needs for the North, with an estimated average undersupply of 5,440 individuals per annum. The only places where this undersupply can be addressed are through reducing economic inactivity or immigration. Any reductions in economic immigration can only have a detrimental impact on our economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why the Food to Go Association was not invited to join the Economic Vision Engagement Forum.
answer_text: It is not feasible for every business group and trade union to be part of the Economic Vision Engagement Forum. The broad hospitality sector is represented on the Forum and I previously met with Michael Henderson, CEO of the Food to Go Association along with my predecessor, Minister Murphy on 17 October 2024. I have recently accepted an invitation to meet again with Mr Henderson.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what resources are in place to protect Cuilcagh Mountain from gorse fires and wildfires.
answer_text: Response to wildfires is under the command and control of the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) which has its own resources in place.Within my own Department, NIEA and Forest Service have several vehicles that can be used to assist with wildfire response, including specialist wildfire All-Terrain-Vehicles which have been pre-deployed with NIFRS for use during wildfire incidents.NIEA and Forest Service also have staffing resources available which can assist with wildfire response, including the provision of advice about natural heritage or forestry interests occurring within the area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what resources are in place to protect the Slieve Beagh Mountain in Clogher Valley from gorse fires and wildfires.
answer_text: Response to wildfires is under the command and control of the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) which has its own resources in place.Within my own Department, NIEA has specialist wildfire All-Terrain-Vehicles which have been pre-deployed with NIFRS for use during wildfire incidents, including at Slieve Beagh.NIEA also has staffing resource available which can assist with wildfire response, including the provision of advice about natural heritage interests occurring within the site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her assessment of the impact the trade agreement between the UK and the United States of America will have on Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The ‘Economic Prosperity Deal’ announced on 8 May is limited in scope and subject to further negotiations. While I welcome the removal of the US tariff on steel/aluminium and most derivative products, the baseline US tariff of 10% remains largely intact, which is a challenge for our exporters – particularly in the pharma, machinery and drinks sectors, representing the lion’s share of our exports to the US. The tariffs issue remains volatile and complex for businesses to navigate. My Tariffs Working Group is continuing to meet on a regular basis to inform my understanding of the issues on a cross-sectoral basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline whether the Veterinary Medicinal Products Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2019/6) will apply in Northern Ireland following the RoI decision to defer the roll-out until 1 September 2025.
answer_text: Following the coming into force of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the Veterinary Medicinal Products Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2019/6) is under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.  This Regulation does not sit within my ministerial responsibilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what engagement her Department has had with the Northern Ireland Food to Go sector in the last 12 months.
answer_text: I recognise the important contribution of the Food to Go sector to our hospitality offering and broader economy. As such, my Department is committed to engaging with stakeholders in the Food to Go sector, notably the NI Food to Go Association. As Finance Minister in October last year, I met with the board and Chief Executive of NI Food to Go Association, Michael Henderson, alongside the then Minister for the Economy, Conor Murphy. The meeting was constructive with discussion on the cost of doing business, single use plastics and the Good Jobs Bill among other issues. Following this meeting, Minister Murphy recorded a short video in support of the inuagural NI Food to Go Association Awards in November. In December last year, DfE officials shared statistics on the Food to Go sector with Michael Henderson. More recently, my officials have raised the issue of abuse of staff in takeaway venues with the PSNI through the Department’s representation on the Purple Flag Committee. Michael Henderson subsequently presented to the Purple Flag Committee with DfE officials in attendance. My Department has commissioned research into the night-time economy and the issues affecting it. The findings will have relevance to the Food to Go Sector and we look forward to sharing those findings when published. I anticipate my Department will have further engagement with the sector as we seek to build a vibrant economy that delivers for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the Rapid Diagnosis Centre in South Tyrone Hospital.
answer_text: The Rapid Diagnostic Centres (RDCs) are a significant initiative to enhance diagnostic processes for cancer. The RDC at South Tyrone Hospital opened in December 2022 alongside the RDC in Whiteabbey Hospital. These centres provide a pathway for patients exhibiting vague symptoms that could potentially indicate cancer. RDCs provide a streamlined, multidisciplinary approach to diagnosis. The RDC at South Tyrone Hospital operates two clinics per week, on three weeks per month and is staffed by a clinician, a nurse, a radiologist and radiographer. The RDC pilot commenced with referral from Southern Trust GPs initially. This expanded to all GP federations 2024. The RDC model has seen capital investment for two additional CT and MRI scanners. CT scanners are currently operational, while the MRI will be commissioned during 25/26. This equipment will also be used to address backlogs in red flag and urgent diagnostic imaging across the region. From January 1, 2023, to 31st March 2025, South Tyrone RDC received 952 referrals to the Vague Symptom Pathway. 556 patients were seen at clinic during this time period. Of these 30% were referred to other specialities for further investigation and tests. To date there have been 23 confirmed cancers relating to lung cancer, urological cancers, haematological cancers and breast, gynaecological and skin cancers. The addition of MRI equipment in these centres will see a further ramping up of diagnostic capacity for red flag and urgent activity. The RDC initiative is part of broader efforts to enhance cancer diagnostic services. We are also working to develop new, tumour site-specific Rapid Diagnosis Pathways for patients with suspected cancer. The establishment of these centres is part of our broader strategy to reduce waiting times for cancer diagnostic tests through ringfenced capacity for elective care patients and also improve early cancer detection rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how her Department is developing closer alignment with primary and secondary schools' career pathways to ensure Northern Ireland has an appropriate resource of skilled employees for the digital economy.
answer_text: Ensuring a pipeline of skills for the digital economy requires a forward looking strategy which spans education, training and industry. The Digital Skills Action Plan, developed in conjunction with the Department of Education and stakeholders from industry and academia, launched in September 2024. It contains a range of actions, including to remove barriers to taking up opportunities in the digital sector and aimed at increasing uptake in the relevant qualifications at GCSE and A level.My Department’s Careers Service currently operates in over 200 post primary schools, helping young people make informed career decisions, with pathways that best suits their skills, talents, career ambitions and individual circumstances. The Careers Service produces guides on various local sectors, to raise awareness of career opportunities at all levels and associated entry routes, including on the digital sector. Officials from my Department and their counterparts in the Department of Education have been developing a joint Careers Action Plan. The Plan will establish a more cohesive, vibrant, and sustainable careers ecosystem, based on effective partnership working, from primary school through to adult clients. The Plan includes actions to introduce careers education in primary schools, with impactful initiatives and programmes appropriate for every stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions her Department has had with ESB International in relation to water levels in Lough Erne at the Cathaleen's Fall Hydro Generation Power Station.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate operational staff are in regular contact with ESB representatives, and this coordinated approach ensures that water levels in Lough Erne are maintained within specified levels, insofar as climatic conditions allow, as agreed under the terms of an agreement made in 1950.Further information on this agreement is available at the weblink belowhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/lough-erne-levelsFor your information an independent review of the operating regime is available at the link belowhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-lough-erne-operating-regime

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any enforcement measures available to her Department in the event of illegal dumping in Lough Erne.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate does not regulate illegal dumping. Their watercourse maintenance responsibility is to protect the drainage function of watercourses.	My Department is not responsible for the removal of litter or debris from the bed or banks of a watercourse such as Lough Erne, unless it is considered that the materials may cause a significant impediment to the free flow of water.	If a significant blockage impeding free flow is identified then my Department has authority under the Drainage Order to take enforcement action to ensure the drainage function is maintained. 	Enforcement powers relating to illegal dumping on lands around the Lough may rest with local council. Polluting of the water in the Lough through illegal dumping is an issue managed by colleagues in DAERA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of her Department’s expenditure on the maintenance of watercourses in Fermanagh and South Tyrone in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate operations are undertaken across an Eastern and a Western Region. The Western Region includes operational areas based in Coleraine, Omagh and Fermanagh. These operational areas reflect river catchments and are not commensurate with administrative/constituency boundaries. Additionally, my Department provides funding to Waterways Ireland who invest in the maintenance of watercourses in Fermanagh, including Lough Erne.The expenditure of the Omagh and Fermanagh Operational Areas and Waterways Ireland are provided in the table below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 25609/22-27, to detail the current number of pike in Lough Erne.
answer_text: DAERA does not undertake full stock assessments for pike in Lough Erne. Pike are captured in the triennial general fisheries assessment surveys of Lough Erne carried out by AFBI and data collected is on a Catch per unit effort (CPUE) basis which his comparable across all the surveys carried out to date. Therefore, the current number of pike in lough Erne are not known.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why farmers who own larger farms are being prioritised in his Business Sustainability Groups.
answer_text: Business Sustainability Groups are one element of the wider Farming for Sustainability - Knowledge Transfer Scheme which will offer support to all farm businesses in the form of information, training and peer-to-peer learning groups.  Support to farm businesses through peer-to-peer learning groups will be delivered in the form of Business Sustainability Groups (BSGs) and Themed Groups. Both offer the opportunity for farmers to use knowledge, co-operation and innovation to improve the sustainability and performance of their businesses. Farm size is not stipulated within the eligibility criteria for either group. Selection criteria for BSGs, which considers farm size, age and gender, will only be used if the programme is oversubscribed. The selection criteria was developed in collaboration with the Agriculture Programme Stakeholder Group (APSG).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the provision of pre-school settings in Clogher Valley and Dungannon.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is responsible for ensuring that sufficient pre-school provision is available in all areas to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target age child whose parents want it.The pre-school admissions process has two stages and the EA has advised that at this point, 97% of target aged children whose parents applied for a pre-school place have already received an offer of a funded place in a setting of their preference.The EA has advised that there are currently 2,316 funded places available across Northern Ireland to accommodate the 620 children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference. The EA has advised that in the Mid Ulster Council area there were 2,081 funded pre-school places available for 2025/26 with a total of 1,922 first preference applications received during Stage One of the admissions process.  1,874 target age children received an offer of a funded place as at close of Stage One (not all first preference) with a further 59 children yet to receive an offer of a place.  Sufficient pre-school provision remains within the Mid Ulster Council area with 207 funded pre-school places still available.  Provision will be continually monitored by the EA throughout Stage Two of the admissions process and where additional funded places are required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings accordingly.Parents whose children have not yet received the offer of a funded place in one of their preferred settings have already received advice on the next steps and have been invited to submit further preferences from Monday 7 April.  A list of providers with funded places still available has been posted on the EA website.I would encourage parents to engage with the admissions process to the end and provide further preferences when invited to do so in order to maximise the chances of their child being offered a place in one of their preferred settings.    Parents will be advised of Stage Two application outcomes on 10th June.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given Northern Ireland will not be included in the National Energy System Operator Review of energy infrastructure following the power outage that affected Heathrow Airport, to detail any steps she is taking to ensure Northern Ireland's airports are protected from a similar incident.
answer_text: The National Energy System Operator’s (NESO) remit is to provide independent advice on security and resilience of the British energy system. However, my Department works closely with the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ), and key findings and lessons from NESO’s review will be shared with my Department and relevant stakeholders to ensure wide resilience to energy risks. My Department also works closely with NIE Networks and other stakeholders including the Utility Regulator and the System Operator (SONI) on the security of electricity supplies in the North.Following the power outage at Heathrow Airport, departmental officials have liaised with NIE Networks and it has provided the following assurances in relation to the resilience of airports in the North:The three airports in the North are not of the scale of Heathrow, with electrical demand not large enough to require supplies at 33kV or higher; All airports have alternative supply options, with the feeds into Belfast International and Belfast City Airport being particularly robust; Airports have direct access to NIE Network’s Control Room via a major customer phone number in the event of any disturbance to their electricity supply; The airports, like all other entities, have back up generation which is reflective of their own business continuity risk appetite; andThe airports are excluded from emergency planned power cut (rota load shedding) arrangements by virtue of their qualification for inclusion on the Protected Sites List.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the current state of Northern Ireland's night-time economy.
answer_text: The night-time economy is an important aspect of our broader economy with implications for our attractiveness as a place to live, work, invest and visit. To better understand the current state of the night-time economy, my Department has collaborated with Tourism NI and the three Belfast Business Improvement Districts to commission research. The research will examine the value of our night-time economy, identify challenges that impact its development, and provide recommendations based on successful policies implemented elsewhere. The work is due to be completed this summer, and I look forward to reviewing the findings. I am aware that the hospitality sector has expressed concerns surrounding the tax and spending changes implemented in early April as announced in the British Government’s Autumn Statement. Increases in the minimum wage and employer’s national insurance contributions will increases costs across many sectors but the impacts have been especially felt in hospitality given that it is labour intensive. When Minister of Finance, I commissioned a Cost of Doing Business research project which is currently being finalised. The study will cover a broad range of factors including the impacts of the recent Budget announcements as well as other contributing factors such as insurance, property and energy costs and the disparities in tax regimes, such as VAT, between the North and the South. I am committed to engaging with stakeholders in the night-time economy and my Department has representation on the Purple Flag Committee and Hospitality Ulster’s Night-Time Commission. I am also due to meet with the Department for Infrastructure and Belfast City Council to discuss the night-time economy and late-night transport.

answer_text: The total cost of the Review was £37k.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions she has had with NI Water in relation to the discharge of raw sewage into rivers and lakes when the water infrastructure reaches capacity.
answer_text: My Department’s role is to set policy and provide funding to NI Water in the form of a subsidy, in lieu of domestic water charges.  I have met twice with the Chair of NI Water since taking up this post. The management of the wastewater system is an operational matter for NI Water.  As such NI Water is regulated by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency in their role as environmental regulator. I have provided NI Water with £500m of public money this year to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North.   During the first 4 years of Price Control 21 (2021-25) NI Water has invested approximately £83.6m to address 12 unsatisfactory intermittent discharges (UIDs) from its wastewater assets. NI Water is also carrying out a phased installation of event duration monitors (EDMs) at combined sewer overflows and the information provided by EDMs will give NI Water an accurate assessment of how discharge points are performing and will help to inform future investment plans, ensuring that money is targeted where most needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current level of stock of pike in Lough Erne.
answer_text: The last survey of pike in Lough Erne took place in 2022 and demonstrated a rise in pike numbers compared with previous surveys dating back to 2010.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail which farmers are eligible to apply for Business Sustainability Groups.
answer_text: Business Sustainability Groups are one element of the wider Farming for Sustainability - Knowledge Transfer Scheme which will offer support to all farm businesses in the form of information, training and peer-to-peer learning groups.  Support to farm businesses through peer-to-peer learning groups will be delivered in the form of Business Sustainability Groups (BSGs) and Themed Groups. Both offer the opportunity for farmers to use knowledge, co-operation and innovation to improve the sustainability and performance of their businesses. To be eligible to apply to BSGs farm businesses must meet the following eligibility criteria:Have a valid DAERA category 1 farm business ID number.The farm business owner and proposed group members must be 18 years old or over.Be willing to host a BSG meeting and share knowledge and information with fellow group members.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with NI Water in relation to the discharge of raw sewage into rivers and lakes when the water network reaches capacity.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates discharges from NI Water’s wastewater treatment works and collection systems to the water environment under the Water Order (as amended) 1999. A regulatory regime is in place, through the Water Order Discharge Consents that requires NI Water to treat sewage to a level that can be sustained by the receiving environment and to maintain their treatments to avoid pollution.It is important to understand that all sewer systems are designed with emergency spill points known as network combined sewer overflows (CSOs) or as Emergency Overflows (EOs). These overflows are required to reduce the risk of sewage escaping from sewers and causing the flooding of homes, schools and businesses with sewage during periods of heavy rainfall. When an overflow discharges outside of the design parameters it is considered an unsatisfactory discharge (UID)NI Water’s wastewater infrastructure currently operates with numerous unsatisfactory discharges of untreated sewage across Northern Ireland and significant improvement is needed. The NIEA is engaged with NI Water on its programme of installing event duration monitors within its network to better assess and understand the frequency and duration of unsatisfactory discharges into the environment. Where NI Water falls below the standards of its Discharge Consent and / or where the company has been the cause of a pollution incident, then DAERA’s Enforcement Policy is applied in these circumstances.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many of the 311 employed consultant staff in the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) are contracted for WHSCT-wide work across the WHSCT area.
answer_text: The Western Trust have a large number of Consultant and Specialty Doctors and Associate Specialist staff who work across its three main acute hospital sites – Altnagelvin, South West Acute Hospital and Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex. This workforce includes general surgeons, breast surgeons, orthopaedic surgeons, urology and anaesthetic staff.  In addition, the Trust also has Consultants who work on Northern Trust sites in both urology and ophthalmology while some of our medical specialties also provide outreach clinics such as renal medicine, neurology and rheumatology.Many of the Trust’s Consultant Workforce would have contracts which may require them to work at locations other than their specified base but to provide exact numbers of Consultants specifically on Trust-wide contracts would require an individual manual review of each Consultant’s job plan.  It would therefore not be possible to define those from the 311 stated who have ‘Trust wide’ contracts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has to develop St. Lucia Barracks, Omagh.
answer_text: My Department plans to commence disposal of the St Lucia site later this year. It is intended for a joint exercise with the British Ministry of Defence to allow for the site to be advertised in one or two lots. The disposal will follow the standard practice for disposal of surplus public sector lands and property.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of employees on long-term sickness in each DFI Roads' division; and (ii) the steps she is taking to address a reduced operational level of staffing due to sickness.
answer_text: The number of employees on long term sickness in each of my Department’s Roads divisions is outlined in the table below.Roads DivisionNumber of staff on long term sicknessDfI East9DfI North5DfI South9DfI West7Total30Where the long term sickness of an employee is identified as causing a significant impact to the delivery of critical services within my Department, officials will consider remedial measures to mitigate this impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when she plans to bring forward proposals to close the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: My Department is urgently progressing proposals for closure of the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme. I hope to be in a position to launch a closure consultation in the coming months, with the aim of closure, subject to necessary approvals and agreements by 2026.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any work that has been carried out on securing an exemption on tariffs for Northern Ireland in advance of the United States of America applying tariffs on trade with the UK and EU.
answer_text: Whilst International trade is a reserved issue, we continue to engage US, UK and EU counterparts to ensure the trade interests of our economy are addressed and fully considered in any decisions.
